AI works through natural language, which means that early in a project, with clients and stakeholders, we can talk our way toward a visual direction. Without building a 3D model or texturing anything, we can explore options and align much earlier and faster.

While doing that, something almost magical happens: AI also inspires us. It becomes a way to ideate architecture and then turn it into reality, such as in the pavilion project in Milan.

Architecture is mostly about life within a space, and one space can be many things. It can be an exhibition space, a disco, or a dining space. It can have different moods and atmospheres. AI helps us simulate all of that quickly, which helps clients understand possible uses and futures of a space.

This is especially interesting in disciplines such as landscape, where rendering vegetation is complex. AI opens new opportunities there. One thing I particularly value is the ability to simulate environmental risks such as flooding scenarios. People react differently when they can see the risks, and that helps us bring meaningful conversations forward earlier.

AI is also powerful when working with data. We bring together reports and regulations and use tools like NotebookLM to interact with that information, understand whether we are moving in the right direction, structure it, and brainstorm possible directions.

This also applies in other disciplines. For example, if the client or local architect provides a planting database, AI can help turn that into the right planting palette.

We can then present this work back to clients using combinations of tools. What matters is that this is bottom-up. The person doing this is not an AI expert; they are a designer. They may start with something simple in Revit, add color, and then produce something visually compelling. Even during construction, work can be engaging and tell a story.

AI is very strong in helping us tell stories about futures. We can produce sketches that align with our visual identity and our brand, but also with the specific project. We can customize outputs to specific workflows, including in Revit, and use them efficiently during projects.

If you do not want polished sketches, we can also create looser diagrams. That can be useful because it avoids overcommitting to materiality or detailing. At the other end of the spectrum, if you want high quality, we can add a great deal of detail and bring the output close to rendering-company quality, starting from a simpler render and combining it with location-specific imagery, such as from Google Earth.

AI also adapts well to people. You can sketch on top of it and add new features. You can start from almost anything, even a blank base. That is how we test facades and materiality. It opens opportunities to move from a sketch to a rough 3D model.

So AI helps us do what we already do in a better and more meaningful way, while also opening the door to things we could not do before.

Ashgan Arasteh: I sit in our development department and work on tools, but I come from an architecture background, and story matters a great deal in architecture.

I often think about tool development as a story. We begin with a challenge—for example, finding the best environmental analysis tool, or understanding how to bring biogenic materials into public architecture. In both architecture and development, the challenge is clearly defined.

Then, based on that story logic, we separate concerns through a narrative. We ask: what are the areas where digital agents can contribute, and where do human agents need to contribute? We gather those areas into one framework where we can define the logic and the workflow.

Through that framework, breakthroughs can happen. We find digital solutions that reflect the same kinds of design breakthroughs we seek in architecture—better mobility, better low-carbon material selection, and so on. These topics are especially significant in Denmark.

But this is not only about digital agents. It is also about human agents.

I am fortunate to work with talented colleagues across many domains. We have industrial PhDs contributing to the knowledge base, and colleagues with expertise in wood construction, BIM management, landscape design, urban design, and more. We cannot create one omniscient entity that does everything, but we can bring all of those people and their knowledge into an ecosystem of tools.

That is what we are building: tools where knowledge from noise simulation, material innovation, or any other specialty can be embedded into the design environment. It is not just about LLMs, agentic tools, toolchains, or technical components. It is about actual people with knowledge shaping the agency of the system.

That creates a good narrative within our company, and narrative matters to us because we tell stories.

Nitzan Bartov: To wrap up, what has the impact of this approach been at Henning Larsen?

The first thing we see very clearly is a cultural shift. In the last year, people across the company—not just in Copenhagen—have become much more open about talking about AI, much more curious about it, and much more willing to experiment. Colleagues now call one another and say, “I tried this thing—do you want to see it?”

To give one concrete example, we created an AI chat on Teams. Normally there is not much motivation to join another AI chat, but nearly half the company voluntarily joined this one and actively uses it. People share what they tried, what worked, what did not work, and what they think about new models.

We are also seeing people develop new skills. They are getting comfortable with prompting, with modeling for AI visualization, and with identifying new ways of modeling, communicating, and working comfortably in AI-supported environments.

We are also starting to gather performance data, and it is very clear that people using AI for specific tasks consistently report time savings of 70% to 80%. Across the board, they also say it improves quality because they are choosing the right tools for the right tasks.

We are also beginning to see cost savings, though we will need more time before we have more precise numbers.

Most importantly, people understand that this is not about “learning AI” once, putting that knowledge in a box, and then moving on. AI is developing at a pace that requires continuous learning. There is no endpoint, at least not in the foreseeable future.

That is the main lesson I would like to leave with you.

Thank you for listening.
detailed_summary:
Nitzan Bartov opened the presentation by positioning Henning Larsen as a unique case study for AI adoption, emphasizing that the firm’s approach is rooted in culture rather than governance. Henning Larsen, a global architecture practice based in Denmark with over 650 employees and projects in more than 30 countries, operates across architecture, landscape, urbanism, interiors, and lighting. The company’s guiding principles—sustainability, circularity, design excellence, research, and data—shape its approach to innovation, including AI. Bartov, who is also conducting PhD research on AI in architecture, was tasked with defining how Henning Larsen should integrate AI into its workflows.

Bartov argued that while many European firms instinctively focus on governance—rules and guardrails—when adopting AI, Henning Larsen recognized early that culture is the more critical lever. Many aspects of AI use, such as how data informs design decisions or how outcomes are justified and communicated, are fundamentally cultural rather than regulatory. For instance, the ambiguity and subjectivity inherent in architectural language challenge the notion that “data-driven” design is inherently “good.” Bartov illustrated this with the example of asking an AI to generate a “beautiful building,” which exposes the limitations and biases of training data. Similarly, he noted that a purely statistical approach would neglect minority needs, such as accessibility features, which architecture as a discipline refuses to do.

Henning Larsen’s strategy is built on four core principles: literacy, transparency, agency, and accountability. Literacy goes beyond teaching employees how to use specific AI tools or prompts; instead, the firm educates staff on the mechanics of large language models and machine learning, fostering a deeper understanding that empowers experimentation and informed tool selection. Transparency ensures that AI use is openly discussed both internally and with clients, enabling proper evaluation and alignment. Agency emphasizes retaining human involvement and judgment in design, resisting the temptation to automate away meaningful engagement. Accountability reinforces that responsibility for outcomes always remains with the human designers, not the AI.

Emilian Niyere, head of digital adoption, detailed how these principles translate into practice. AI is not used to generate entire projects from a single prompt; rather, it accelerates and enhances many small, complex tasks. Everyday productivity tools like Copilot and Ramboll GPT (an internal ChatGPT variant) are widely used, but AI’s most transformative impact is in communication and ideation. For example, AI enables early-stage visualizations and scenario explorations with clients, facilitating alignment without the need for detailed 3D modeling. In projects like the Milan pavilion, AI-inspired ideation led to tangible architectural outcomes. AI also supports rapid simulation of spatial uses, environmental risks (such as flooding), and landscape complexities, making abstract risks and opportunities more concrete for stakeholders.

The firm leverages tools like NotebookLM to synthesize reports, regulations, and client data, streamlining research and brainstorming. AI assists in transforming client-provided databases, such as planting lists, into actionable design palettes. Importantly, these workflows are bottom-up: designers, not AI specialists, drive experimentation, often starting with familiar tools like Revit and layering AI-generated enhancements. AI also supports storytelling, enabling the creation of sketches and diagrams at varying levels of fidelity, from loose conceptual visuals to near-photorealistic renderings, often incorporating site-specific imagery.

Ashgan Arasteh, from the development department, framed tool development as a narrative process, beginning with a defined challenge—such as environmental analysis or material innovation—and mapping where digital and human agents contribute. This narrative-driven framework allows the integration of diverse expertise, from BIM management to noise simulation, into a cohesive ecosystem of tools. The goal is not to create an omniscient AI, but to embed human knowledge and agency into the digital environment, ensuring that tools reflect the collective intelligence of the firm.

Bartov concluded by highlighting the tangible cultural shift at Henning Larsen: widespread curiosity, experimentation, and skill development around AI. The voluntary adoption of an internal AI chat on Teams, with nearly half the company participating, exemplifies this shift. Early performance data indicates significant time savings (70–80%) and improved quality for AI-assisted tasks, with emerging evidence of cost savings. Most importantly, the firm recognizes that AI adoption is an ongoing process requiring continuous learning, not a one-time transformation. This commitment to cultural evolution, rather than static governance, is the central lesson Bartov offered to the audience.

title: Change Management and AI-Enabled Lighting Design at HLB
summary: This presentation focused on organizational change management for AI adoption in lighting design. Erik Stroemberg discussed tracking impact through metrics like time, effort, quality, and value alignment, supported by AI champions and internal structures, and showed how Copilot-generated lighting designs can combine efficiency with creative practice.
transcript:
Eric Stromberg: Let’s jump straight into relevance, and I want to make this personal for a moment.

Some of you may have noticed that I was limping slightly when I came on stage. I had minor hip surgery about a month ago. After surgery, I looked at my X-rays. As is usually the case, even if you trust the doctor and stay calm, your brain starts scanning the image for meaning without actually having the expertise to interpret it.

I looked at the X-rays and honestly could not tell what I was looking at or what the actual issue was.

After the appointment, I had that familiar combination of relief and worry. You meet with a doctor, get a 30-second summary, ask a couple of questions, then go home and realize you have two more.

So I uploaded the X-rays to an AI chatbot and asked, without additional context, whether it saw any abnormalities.

It gave me a clear explanation that aligned with what my doctor had told me. It was not a replacement for the doctor. It was reinforcement.

It helped remove some of the fear from the situation and gave me language I could use to think about it. In that moment, it mattered. My doctor made the diagnosis and treatment plan. AI was the partner that helped me process it at my own pace. It gave me insight beyond my expertise in terms I could understand, exactly when I needed it.

That is relevant, and I will come back to it.

For anyone curious, the issue was a small bump on the femur head that had to be removed. It was very hard to see with an untrained eye.

My name is Eric Stromberg. I’m an associate director and team manager at HLB Lighting Design. I’m an electrical engineer and an automation enthusiast almost by accident. These days, I spend most of my time helping design teams figure out how to use new technology in ways that make work better rather than more complicated.

I do not come from the perspective of having all the answers. I come from having tried a lot of things, broken a lot of workflows, and learned from very smart people. Today I want to share some of those lessons.

HLB Lighting Design is the world’s largest women-owned independent architectural lighting design firm. Founded in 1968, we have more than 50 years of experience in how people experience the built environment. We have 10 studios across North America and the Middle East and are growing in Europe. We have nearly 200 professionals serving 20 market sectors. Our services include architectural lighting design, sustainability, visualization, and custom product development.

There are three key insights I hope you take home from this talk:

Urgency comes from relevance, not fear.

Pilots don’t scale—recipes do.

Give ownership, not tasks.

Our industry shares a few defining traits: it is knowledge-based, deeply proud of its craft, and very fast-moving.

Nothing threatens professional identity faster than hearing someone say they can do the same task faster, with less stress, and with better results.

Think of a carpenter who has used a hammer for 20 years. Then someone arrives with a shiny screwdriver. The new person shows off; the experienced person feels the threat and grips the hammer more tightly. Over time, they will learn from each other—the new one will learn to use a hammer, and the experienced one will eventually incorporate the screwdriver—but the time it takes them to meet can vary greatly.

When we introduce AI using fear, we trigger resistance.

During our Copilot onboarding sessions early last year, after covering GenAI fundamentals, compliance, structured data, and reverse engineering, I showed the team an image and asked whether they thought it was AI-generated.

The responses were about 50/50, which was what I expected. But what was remarkable was not the split—it was the energy in the discussion when I asked why.

These were lighting designers. They argued passionately about balance between daylight and internal lighting, whether the structured surface in the back looked plausible, and whether the fixtures were positioned correctly.

Then I told them the image had been generated by Copilot.

That was when I realized that urgency does not come from fear. It comes from relevance. If someone sees why it matters to their craft, they engage.

The real leadership question is not, “How do we roll out AI?” It is, “How do we help each person hold on to the pride they already have?”

That means tuning the message carefully. If you are too quiet, people ignore it. If you are too loud, you create resistance. The leaders who succeed are the ones who tune the message to the specific craft.

A recent MIT-affiliated study showed that 95% of organizations across industries are not seeing measurable returns from AI. Only 5% are extracting meaningful value.

So if AI feels frustrating, you are not behind. You are normal.

Most firms are stuck not because they lack ambition or intelligence, but because they have run pilots as isolated events focused on tasks, instead of designing new systems and processes end to end.

Pilots prove that something can work, but a recipe makes sure it works again—with different people, different projects, and different data.

When we started our pilots in early 2024, we thought we were being smart. We wanted something scalable, measurable, and intuitive. So we created a pilot workflow: identify the task, prepare the data, develop the prompts, execute, evaluate, and document.

What we did not understand was that these steps are not equally weighted.

In reality, data preparation dominates everything. Not the tools. Not the prompts. Data.

AI readiness turned out to be less about intelligence and more about organization.

Let me give you a concrete example. I went looking for the best proposals from our best projects because proposals seem like a natural AI task. They are high effort, repeatable, and time-consuming.

But the reality of our folders and file structures showed we were not ready. Should I trust the latest modified date? Should I ask the project manager? Which file is actually the right one?

So we deliberately decided not to pursue proposals as one of our first AI tasks—not because AI could not handle them, but because we were not ready.

That insight matters. As many of you know, collecting and managing qualitative data is one of the hardest parts of starting and scaling AI.

During our pilots, we used a few key metrics. I tried to imagine what someone on the board would need to know before investing more money in the initiative.

We focused on three measures that most executives understand: time, effort, and quality, comparing non-AI and AI-assisted work.

These metrics were easy to measure, easy to communicate, and easy to revisit—not just at the end of a pilot, but later as tools evolved, which they obviously do.

As development sped up, we added a fourth measure: value alignment.

That is not a nice-to-have. Speed without value introduces risk.

For example, one of our core values at HLB is integrity. That means agentic AI can never be the sole decision-maker in talent selection.

Whether you buy tools, build them, or mix the two, what keeps progress from turning into chaos is a repeatable recipe for evaluating them.

What is interesting is that these metrics start to feel familiar, because they resemble the way you evaluate a person before hiring them: experience, knowledge, attention to detail, and cultural fit.

As we move toward autonomous agents, AI stops being a tool you use occasionally and starts to look more like a colleague or teammate that you manage.

If you hired 50 new employees, you would not simply assign them tasks. You would redesign management, training, review, and accountability.

That brings us to ownership.

With a traditional leadership style, if you assign tasks, you often get compliance. If you give ownership, you can get stewardship.

Because all of this is moving so quickly, we wanted to enable fast iterations by turning people into intrapreneurs.

Within predefined boundaries and with knowledge of our AI policy, we gave them enough room for individual experimentation, alongside frequent updates.

During our firmwide Copilot onboarding, we told everyone: “You are now a supervisor. This tool is your new colleague. What it produces, you are responsible for.”

Our AI champions—our guiding coalition—were set up with that in mind.

We defined their role as carrying updates from the strategic team back to their studios, and then feeding studio experience back to the broader champion group. They represent a group of people, not just themselves.

They are not merely prompt writers. They are feedback loops.

If you do not yet have an AI champions group, that is my recommendation. They are mostly volunteers, they speak honestly about both pros and cons, and they help protect others from pitfalls.

From my experience implementing software and processes—and especially now in the AI era—I believe a successful organizational structure needs three elements.

First, an executive sponsor: someone in the C-suite or management who understands risk, reputation, and long-term firm health.

Second, an AI council: ideally one representative from each department, but kept small—perhaps no more than 10 people. This group should prioritize processes, not just tools, and should be willing to be unpopular in the short term.

Third, and most importantly, an adoption owner in the center. The title matters less than the authority: AI transformation lead, head of AI enablement, digital change lead. What matters is that this person has clarity and authority.

This is the person who wakes up asking why pilots are not turning into habits, and what needs documentation, upskilling, or training.

The right profile for this role is hybrid: both technical and operational, deeply trusted internally, and a process thinker first and technologist second.

If that person has to ask permission for everything, the role is decorative. If they can make decisions, it is transformative.

So the sponsor sets the why, the council sets the what, and the adoption owner figures out the how.

To summarize the three key ideas:

Make AI meaningful for the people doing the work by tuning into relevance.

Make it repeatable by investing in a system for evaluation.

Empower teams to carry the work forward by giving ownership.

Leadership is not about having all the answers. It is about making it easier for others to win.

To close, if you had to approve next year’s budget and choose between more AI tools or better adoption systems, which would move ROI faster?

Most firms would probably choose more AI tools, because it sounds exciting and may come with fewer meetings. But our experience suggests the better strategic choice is adoption systems, because they are rare and because, as with my X-ray, the breakthrough was not that the technology existed. It was knowing when and how to use it.

Audience question: What role does leadership play in sustaining momentum after the initial excitement of an AI pilot fades? How do you keep adoption efforts from stalling?

Eric Stromberg: I think it comes back to the AI champions and the communication chain they create. You need champions who engage the people they work with and maintain a clear flow of communication between the strategic group and the people doing the work. Frequent updates matter more than large, infrequent updates. Again, the goal is to enable fast communication and fast iteration.
detailed_summary:
Eric Stromberg’s presentation at HLB Lighting Design centered on the thesis that successful AI adoption in knowledge-based, craft-driven industries hinges not on fear or novelty, but on making technology relevant, repeatable, and owned by those who use it. Drawing from both personal experience and organizational case studies, Stromberg argued that the urgency to adopt AI must be rooted in its direct relevance to practitioners’ work, rather than imposed through top-down mandates or fear of obsolescence.

Stromberg opened with a personal anecdote about using an AI chatbot to interpret his post-surgery X-rays. While the AI did not replace his doctor, it provided reinforcement and clarity, helping him process complex information in accessible terms. This experience illustrated his core point: AI’s value emerges when it augments human expertise at moments of need, not when it attempts to supplant it. He returned to this theme throughout, emphasizing that technology must be introduced in ways that respect and enhance professional identity.

HLB Lighting Design, as the world’s largest women-owned independent architectural lighting firm, operates in a sector defined by deep expertise, pride in craft, and rapid change. Stromberg noted that introducing AI into such environments can provoke resistance, especially if framed as a threat to established skills. He likened this to a seasoned carpenter confronted with a new tool—initial skepticism is natural, but meaningful engagement arises when the new tool’s relevance to the craft becomes clear.

To illustrate this, Stromberg recounted HLB’s onboarding sessions for Microsoft Copilot, where lighting designers debated whether an image was AI-generated. The ensuing discussion revealed that engagement spiked when the conversation focused on the craft-specific implications of AI, rather than abstract technicalities. This led to his first key insight: urgency for change comes from relevance, not fear.

Stromberg critiqued the widespread reliance on isolated AI pilots, referencing a recent MIT-affiliated study showing that only 5% of organizations achieve measurable returns from AI. He argued that pilots, while useful for proof-of-concept, do not scale unless they are transformed into repeatable “recipes”—systematic processes that work across projects, teams, and data sets. HLB’s own pilot workflow initially focused on task identification, data preparation, prompt development, execution, evaluation, and documentation. However, they quickly discovered that data preparation was the dominant factor in AI readiness, outweighing tool selection or prompt engineering. This realization led them to postpone automating proposal generation, not due to AI limitations, but because their data organization was insufficient—a common but underappreciated barrier in scaling AI.

In evaluating their pilots, HLB used metrics familiar to executives: time, effort, and quality, comparing AI-assisted and traditional workflows. As their initiatives matured, they added value alignment as a fourth metric, recognizing that speed without adherence to core values (such as integrity in talent selection) introduces risk. Stromberg stressed that these metrics mirror those used in evaluating human hires, underscoring the shift toward treating AI as a managed teammate rather than a mere tool.

Ownership emerged as Stromberg’s third pillar. He advocated for empowering teams as “intrapreneurs,” giving them autonomy within clear boundaries and policies. HLB’s AI champions—volunteer leaders embedded in each studio—acted as feedback loops, bridging strategic direction and on-the-ground experience. Stromberg recommended that organizations establish similar champions groups, supported by a three-part structure: an executive sponsor to set vision, a cross-departmental AI council to prioritize processes, and a central adoption owner with both authority and hybrid technical-operational expertise. This structure ensures that AI adoption is not decorative but transformative.

In conclusion, Stromberg advised that investment in robust adoption systems—rather than just more AI tools—yields greater ROI. The real breakthrough, as with his X-ray experience, lies not in the existence of technology, but in knowing when and how to use it. In response to an audience question about sustaining momentum, he reiterated the importance of AI champions and frequent, transparent communication to maintain engagement and iterative progress. Stromberg’s perspective is clear: leadership in AI is about enabling others to succeed by making change meaningful, systematic, and owned at every level.

title: Engineering Workflow Automation with AI at Proyectos and Macadamia Labs
summary: Proyectos Engineering and Macadamia Labs demonstrated practical AI deployment in engineering workflows, including quality assurance, vendor comparison, and technical documentation. The presentation covered user adoption metrics, the shift from hourly billing to fixed pricing, engineer-led workflow design for trust and usability, UX refinement of comparison tools, and Plant 3D integration for direct QA checks that reduce errors and streamline delivery.
transcript:
Joaquin Arakena: Thank you for being here before lunch. We’ll keep this concise. We want to talk about what we did together—the good, the bad, and the ugly.

It has been reassuring to hear other firms share many of the same problems. That tells me I’m at the right conference.

I’m Joaquin from Proyectos. I’m a partner there, and I’m here with Abel Embrechts from Macadamia Labs. We’re going to talk about what we built together over the past year and share some insights.

Proyectos is a multidisciplinary design firm of roughly 100 people, depending on how you define small versus midsize in your market. We are based in Uruguay and work across the Americas. We are engineers and architects, and we were smart enough to know that we could not do AI by ourselves.

So I went to LinkedIn, looked through my network, and found these two gentlemen—now friends—from Macadamia Labs.

Abel Embrechts: We are a Y Combinator-backed startup focused on speeding up engineering deliverables for engineering firms, particularly in industrial infrastructure, of which Proyectos is a great example.

We have been working with Proyectos for about a year and have implemented four workflows.

The first is quality-assurance checks on technical documentation, such as isometrics, verifying whether design files comply with the basis of design.

The second is chatting with technical standards—making it easy to navigate complex technical documentation.

The third is RFQ vendor comparison. There are many vendors and many files that need to be compared quickly, so we use AI to provide engineers with a clear overview.

The fourth gets deeper into a software platform Proyectos uses, called Plant 3D. We built small automations for repetitive workflows—spooling, tagging, and quality assurance checks directly within the design software.

For those less familiar with AI agents, these are systems that can read unstructured or technical documents and use existing tools such as AutoCAD and Excel.

At a very high level, our platform takes in your technical documents and the tools your engineers already use during design. We then create custom workflows with custom interfaces based on how the firm operates. The end goal is faster engineering deliverables, whether that means QA reports or more detailed design outputs.

Joaquin Arakena: Let me describe the problem we were trying to solve.

We do integrated design with all disciplines together in-house. Each discipline believes it is the most important, wants priority, and wants ownership of its BIM model.

Each discipline also has its own recipe—its own tools and ways of working—and each one is responsible for its deliverables. You cannot simply tell engineers exactly how to work.

We work through the full lifecycle, from early design to procurement to construction and commissioning. We are looking at AI across that full lifecycle, not just in design, even though design is the obvious low-hanging fruit.

Mistakes are very expensive. If something is wrong in a BIM model, you can change it. If something is wrong in the real world, someone can get hurt or killed. You cannot say, “Our new AI agent made a mistake.” So error checking and risk management are always present.

Every project manager and engineering designer also believes that their project is different. Anyone who has worked in integrated design has heard that before. That makes standardization difficult.

What we did was examine eight workflows and deploy four. We now have 54 users and 24 repeat users. We would like that repeat-user number to go up. That is where we stand today.

Let me share a few business insights—what worked, what did not, and what the results have been.

First: do not automate chaos.

We thought we were going to automate piping design processes. When we asked how piping design was actually being done, we discovered six different processes. We are not even that large a company—we probably have around 30 piping designers—but somehow we had six different ways of doing the same thing.

We learned that only when we tried to hand the work over to an agent.

So one major benefit was simply mapping our processes. That delivered value before the AI itself.

Second: error detection is part of the design process. You cannot just ask a system to produce something. It also needs to support checking whether that output is correct.

Third: the actual piping engineers need to help design the agent together with our external partner, because they are responsible for the result. You cannot hand a tool to a professional and simply say, “Please make sure you use this.”

Fourth: adoption is the hardest part. It is easy to say, “I want an agent—please build it.” It is much harder to get other people to use it consistently.

Another major insight was to start with the business case. At the beginning, our mindset was often, “AI can do that, so let’s do it.” Later we realized that some of those tasks only happened once every four years. We had to ask whether automation still made sense.

So we developed criteria to evaluate ideas properly.

Another big lesson was to get ready to change your pricing strategy. If you are selling hours, AI disrupts your effort model. That is a tricky situation.

But the benefits have been real.

We showed a graph of the last eight years at Proyectos. In 2017 onward, sales were rising, billable hours were rising, and EBITDA was rising—but the growth was tied to selling more hours.

Last year was the first year in which we sold more with fewer hours.

We did not fire anybody. We simply spent more hours working with our partners and our agents.

So what do we sell now?

We are not only selling hours anymore. Sometimes we still do, because some clients insist on that model. But increasingly we are selling results.

That is a major cultural shift—for us, for our clients, and for our people.

And the results are there. This is not just because of four agents. It is also because AI forced us to confront the weakness of an hours-based pricing model and move more of our work to fixed-price projects. That improved our results.

What do our people think?

We surveyed roughly 80% of our design team.

Reported productivity gains were strong.

One area that concerned me as a manager was risk perception. Engineers largely reported low perceived risk. That worries me. I would actually prefer a bit more caution. If my engineers are not perceiving risk, I need to work on that.

We also tracked whether people were worried about losing their jobs. About 14% expressed concern. This is the first year we have measured that, so we will see how it changes. My sense is that many engineers have already realized that the greater risk is not using AI, rather than using it.

Another interesting result was what people were using AI for. The most surprising use case was learning and research.

We work across industries—oil and gas, semiconductors, pulp and paper—and each has different standards, such as how far supports should be spaced in piping. AI helped electrical and piping engineers move into new sectors much more quickly. That was a very interesting finding.

Abel Embrechts: Let me share some technical insights. Over the past year, we learned a lot in four areas: creating trust, handling quality and noise, co-designing with engineers, and driving adoption.

First, trust. Everyone knows LLMs hallucinate. We therefore tried to design the product in a way that engineers could trust.

Two basic methods helped. One is citations to source material. If the system flags an error in an isometric drawing, it should point to the evidence. If it finds no errors, it should still show what area it investigated.

The other is transparency through reasoning traces. We want to be open about what the agent is doing and how it is thinking, so the engineer can judge whether the logic is sound.

Second, quality and noise. AI produces a lot of output. For example, if we do a quick QA check, the system might return 50 to 80 points. The challenge is how to help engineers review that quickly, rather than slowing them down.

One lesson is to build around state-of-the-art models. When we started working with Proyectos, we were using earlier-generation models; by now, the performance gains in construction-related tasks are significant. If you build custom systems on top of strong frontier models, your solutions improve as the models improve.

Another lesson is to use the company’s existing SOPs. Many firms already have strong standard procedures for reviewing drawings and generating reports—processes often used by interns. If you make that knowledge available to the agents, performance improves substantially.

UX design is also critical. We often build custom interfaces so that reviewing the outputs is straightforward.

Joaquin Arakena: The RFQ comparison interface we showed was probably the fourth version. Our project managers kept saying, “I don’t like it. Show me something else. I showed it to my client and they think it is too complex.” So we kept iterating.

That is one reason this partnership mattered. The version we are showing now is not the first iteration. A lot of lessons are embedded in it.

Abel Embrechts: We also observed that adoption is much higher when we integrate directly with the software engineers already use. For example, with Plant 3D, engineers do not want to export a PDF of an isometric just to check it. They want to run the check directly inside the software. That creates much more value.

Small things also matter for adoption, such as making it easy to export reports, share outputs internally, and access resources from one library.

Finally, the most-used use cases almost always came from close collaboration with engineers inside the firm. When an engineer from the organization proposes the idea, adoption is much stronger.

Joaquin Arakena: Exactly. If I go to an electrical engineer and say, “I have an idea for you,” the first question is likely to be, “How many electrical design projects have you done?” If the answer is zero, they are not going to care.

Sometimes you need to suggest ideas, but if someone originates the idea themselves, the chances of adoption are much higher.

Audience question: You mentioned using a state-of-the-art model like Gemini. How do you bridge the gap between very deep domain expertise and a general model?

Abel Embrechts: That is the key opportunity. Every company has deep internal knowledge that is not available to public LLMs because it lives behind closed doors. That is where the value lies.

As the models get better, your internal domain knowledge becomes even more powerful. If that knowledge exists in actual files—SOPs, PDFs, or other formats—it can be used to guide the models effectively.

We often describe these systems as increasingly smart interns. But what makes them useful is access to the firm’s proprietary expertise.

Also, because the design process is so complex, we break it down into many smaller building blocks and evaluate performance at that level. That helps us build the confidence needed before putting something in front of an engineer for verification.

Audience question: Your comments about user experience resonated with me. How long did it take to go from the first version of your design to the fourth, and how did you handle diverse expectations? Is the fourth version really what users wanted, or is this a never-ending journey?

Joaquin Arakena: The honest answer is that it is a never-ending journey.

We care most about quick iterations. Much of the collaboration has been remote—our design team is mostly in Uruguay, while our technical partners have been working from Silicon Valley and Europe. In fact, this conference is the first time we are meeting in person, which is interesting in itself.

What we tell engineers is: say what you want, but do not expect the first result to be exactly right. Get something out there and then give feedback.

So our priority is not perfection in the first version. Our priority is speed of iteration and making sure the output adds value to our clients.

That is the most important test—not whether one of our engineers likes it in isolation, but whether it solves a problem for the client.

You can easily end up with two engineers talking all day and iterating something that does not matter. The client must remain part of the conversation.

One final point: our average age at Proyectos is about 26. We used to describe ourselves as an infrastructure design company that uses technology. Now we increasingly think of ourselves as a technology company that does infrastructure design.

It used to be BIM. Now it is AI with BIM. People in their mid-20s are very excited about that. That makes some aspects easier, though of course it will not be the same for every firm.
detailed_summary:
Joaquin Arakena and Abel Embrechts presented a detailed account of their year-long collaboration between Proyectos, a multidisciplinary design firm based in Uruguay, and Macadamia Labs, a Y Combinator-backed startup specializing in AI-driven workflow automation for engineering firms. Their central thesis was that integrating AI agents into engineering workflows can drive significant productivity gains, but the true value emerges only when automation is grounded in well-mapped processes, co-designed with domain experts, and tightly integrated into existing tools and business models.

The partnership focused on automating four key workflows at Proyectos. The first was quality assurance (QA) checks on technical documentation, such as isometric drawings, to verify compliance with the basis of design. The second involved building a chat interface for navigating complex technical standards, making it easier for engineers to find and interpret relevant requirements. The third workflow automated the comparison of RFQ (Request for Quotation) vendor submissions, a process that previously required manual review of numerous documents to provide clear, actionable comparisons. The fourth and most technically integrated workflow involved embedding automations directly into Autodesk Plant 3D, the firm’s primary design software, to handle repetitive tasks like spooling, tagging, and QA checks without leaving the design environment.

Abel Embrechts explained that their AI agents are designed to read unstructured and technical documents and interact with familiar engineering tools such as AutoCAD and Excel. The Macadamia Labs platform ingests technical documents and integrates with the tools engineers already use, enabling the creation of custom workflows and interfaces tailored to the firm’s operational realities. The overarching goal is to accelerate engineering deliverables—whether QA reports or detailed design outputs—by reducing manual effort and error.

Joaquin Arakena emphasized the complexity of integrated design environments, where multiple engineering disciplines each have their own tools, standards, and processes. This diversity makes standardization and automation challenging, as even within a team of 30 piping designers, they discovered six distinct processes for the same task. Attempting to automate these workflows surfaced the need to first map and standardize processes—a step that delivered immediate value even before AI was deployed.

A key lesson was that error detection must be embedded in the design process, not treated as a separate or after-the-fact activity. Furthermore, successful automation requires that the engineers responsible for deliverables co-design the AI agents with external partners. Adoption proved to be the hardest challenge; while building an agent is straightforward, ensuring consistent use across the team requires ongoing engagement and iteration.

The business case for automation also evolved. Initially, the team was tempted to automate any task AI could handle, but soon realized that some tasks occurred too infrequently to justify the investment. This led to the development of criteria for evaluating automation opportunities based on frequency and impact. Another major shift was in pricing strategy: as AI reduced the hours needed for deliverables, Proyectos began moving from an hours-based billing model to fixed-price, results-oriented contracts, which improved both profitability and client satisfaction.

The impact was measurable. For the first time in eight years, Proyectos increased sales while reducing billable hours, without layoffs. Productivity gains were confirmed by internal surveys, though Arakena noted a need to raise awareness of risk among engineers, as low perceived risk could lead to complacency. Interestingly, AI’s most surprising use case was accelerating learning and research, enabling engineers to transition between industries—such as oil and gas, semiconductors, and pulp and paper—by quickly accessing and understanding new standards.

On the technical side, Embrechts highlighted the importance of building trust in AI outputs. This was achieved through features like citations to source material and transparency via reasoning traces, allowing engineers to verify the logic behind AI-generated results. Quality and noise management were addressed by leveraging state-of-the-art models (such as Google Gemini), integrating company-specific SOPs, and focusing on user experience with custom interfaces. Iterative design, driven by direct feedback from engineers and project managers, was essential; the RFQ comparison tool, for example, went through four major iterations before reaching its current form.

Integration with existing software, particularly Plant 3D, was critical for adoption, as was making outputs easy to export and share. The most successful use cases originated from within the engineering team, underscoring the need for close collaboration and user-driven innovation.

In conclusion, Arakena and Embrechts argued that AI-driven workflow automation delivers real business value when it is grounded in mapped processes, co-designed with domain experts, and aligned with evolving business models. The journey is ongoing, requiring rapid iteration, continuous feedback, and a focus on solving real client problems. The cultural shift—from selling hours to selling results, and from being a design firm that uses technology to a technology company that designs infrastructure—was as significant as the technical transformation itself.

title: AI-Assisted Structural Analysis in Autodesk Robot
summary: Alexey Serous demonstrated how Autodesk Robot Structural Analysis can be combined with AI to automate structural engineering tasks such as modifying trusses and analyzing load-bearing frames. He also explained technical concepts behind the AI experience, including token-based response times, attention layers in LLMs, and the need to manage context carefully to reduce hallucinations and improve reliability.
transcript:
Alexei, a project engineer in structural steel design and AI ambassador at Sweco Finland, presented a prototype that integrates AI into the daily workflow of structural engineers using Autodesk Robot Structural Analysis.

He explained that when he says “daily workflow,” he means something engineers can benefit from literally every day. In his experience, many AI solutions are text-oriented, while engineers often spend their day inside professional software performing actions such as copying, moving, mirroring, dragging, and trimming rather than writing text. His goal was to let AI “push the buttons” for engineers.

He described the technical approach as using tool calls: asking an AI model to respond in a specific format, then having software parse that output and make changes in the engineering application based on the response. He built this by taking Autodesk Robot Structural Analysis, coding tools for it, creating a small chat interface, and connecting it to AI.

A live demo was attempted, but repeated network and connection issues prevented it from running reliably. Alexei noted that the prepared presentation also contained GIFs of the intended demonstrations, so he continued using those instead.

Using the presentation examples, he showed that a user can send text prompts to the assistant and have the AI modify the structural model opened in Robot. One example was asking the system to make a steel truss steeper by changing its slope. He noted that response times are typically around 30 seconds to one minute because the model generates the solution token by token.

He then discussed where these AI-assisted engineering applications perform especially well. First, they are very strong at data analysis tasks such as “find me all…” queries. He attributed this to the attention mechanism in large language models. In simplified terms, when the model receives tokens in context, it evaluates their importance and compresses them into a weighted summary. When users ask it to locate or identify specific items, it is effectively using that attention mechanism to highlight the most relevant tokens.

Second, the applications also work well when generating content from scratch. He explained that large language models are trained on long token sequences where each next token makes sense relative to the previous summary, so they are naturally good at producing similar coherent sequences. He had planned to demonstrate generating a steel hall from scratch, but due to the failed live demo he relied on the GIFs in the slides instead.

Alexei then turned to the challenges. He emphasized that this is a fast-moving area and that some of the problems he encountered may already be less severe with newer models. He framed this section as more of a historical debrief based on his own implementation experience.

The first challenge was what he called “task understanding” hallucinations. He gave an example of a building with six load-bearing frames that he wanted to convert into a building with five load-bearing frames while keeping the same overall length. In the failed case, the AI understood the geometry in general but hallucinated intermediate bars that should not exist.

He attributed this to the nature of model training. Large language models are trained first on internet-scale data and then on a comparatively small set of instruction examples. There is far less engineering content online than general content, so the model’s weights are not naturally tuned for many engineering-specific transformations.

His solution was to change the context by breaking a complex instruction into a sequence of basic tasks. Instead of saying, “Convert this building to a five-frame building,” he would decompose the job into simpler operations: find all load-bearing frames, confirm there are six, delete the first frame, then move the remaining frames so the building keeps the same total length. He found that the model handled these basic operations—find, move, copy, create, delete—very well.

The second challenge was what he described as a “messed up output” problem, where no matter what the user asked, the model would corrupt the software model and still behave as if it had succeeded. He believed this was caused by overloaded context.

He explained that even if the user stays within the model’s allowed token limit, context quality still matters. Since all context is compressed into a weighted summary, too many low-value or irrelevant tokens can pollute that summary. He illustrated this with an experiment using a small local language model. When he asked it to draw a triangle with a simple prompt, it correctly produced three points. But when he added several pages of “useful” extra information about geometric shapes and coordinate systems, the model became confused and sometimes produced only two points or four points instead of three.

His conclusion was that the context must be kept clean. There is no need to expose thousands of tools, include full software manuals, or overwhelm the prompt with system instructions, tool definitions, output formatting rules, and other noise. If all of that is compressed into the same summary, the model may focus on JSON formatting or tool syntax rather than the substance of the user’s request.

In closing, Alexei argued that AI can deliver value in engineering for anyone who spends their day operating software to produce design packages under deadline pressure. He said there is work for multiple roles in making this practical:

- Developers need to write plugins and tools for the engineering software people already use and connect those tools to AI.
- AI ambassadors or AI integration leads need to educate colleagues, especially non-coders, because many still see AI as a black box.
- Engineers themselves need to stay open-minded and be willing to change how they work, much as people adapted when the internet became widespread.

He estimated that even relatively small assistants can save one to four days per month per employee, depending on how much they are used. Scaled to a division of 500 people, even if only 20–30% of employees use the tools, the savings could amount to thousands of hours—roughly 4,000 to 10,000 hours—which he said is a substantial amount of engineering effort in structural steel design.

During Q&A, one attendee asked whether the same outcome could have been achieved with copy and paste. Alexei replied that his target use case is not repetitive duplication but model modification. He described scenarios in which a customer asks to extend a building by a few meters, which in finite element software requires many manual adjustments: moving walls, fixing nodes that do not follow connected geometry, and updating detailed components. He said copy-paste helps when repeating geometry, but his prototype is aimed at the tedious modification work common in detailing and design development. Based on stopwatch measurements, he estimated time savings of about five to ten minutes per request, depending on complexity.

Another question asked whether the prototype was already being used within Sweco. Alexei said that this year’s target is broader introduction and adoption. The tool had been released in a testing phase, some colleagues had tried it, and at least one person was already using it in production. He said the goal for the year was to introduce this way of working across the business unit, and that Autodesk Robot was only one of several programs in which they hope to enable similar workflows.
detailed_summary:
Alexei, a project engineer specializing in structural steel design and serving as an AI ambassador at Sweco Finland, presented a prototype system that integrates artificial intelligence directly into the daily workflow of structural engineers using Autodesk Robot Structural Analysis. His central thesis was that AI can and should be leveraged to automate the routine, non-textual tasks that dominate engineering workflows—such as copying, moving, mirroring, and trimming elements within professional design software—rather than focusing solely on text-based solutions. His vision is for AI to effectively “push the buttons” for engineers, streamlining model modifications and freeing up valuable engineering time.

To realize this vision, Alexei developed a technical approach centered on tool calls. In this architecture, an AI model is prompted to respond in a structured format, which is then parsed by custom software to execute corresponding actions within Autodesk Robot. He built a chat interface that connects user prompts to the AI, which in turn manipulates the structural model based on the interpreted instructions. Although a live demonstration was hampered by network issues, Alexei used pre-recorded GIFs to illustrate the system’s capabilities. For example, he showed how a user could instruct the AI assistant to make a steel truss steeper by adjusting its slope, with the AI generating the necessary modifications in Robot. He noted that response times for such operations typically range from 30 seconds to a minute, as the AI model generates solutions incrementally.

Alexei highlighted two areas where AI-assisted engineering applications excel. First, they are particularly effective at data analysis tasks, such as responding to “find me all…” queries. He attributed this strength to the attention mechanism inherent in large language models (LLMs), which allows the model to weigh and summarize relevant tokens in context, making it adept at identifying and extracting specific information from complex models. Second, these applications are strong at generating new content from scratch, thanks to the sequential training of LLMs on long token sequences. He had planned to demonstrate the generation of a steel hall from scratch, but due to technical difficulties, relied on illustrative GIFs instead.

However, Alexei was candid about the challenges encountered during development. The first major issue was “task understanding” hallucinations, where the AI would misunderstand or invent elements during model transformations. For instance, when tasked with converting a building from six to five load-bearing frames while maintaining overall length, the AI sometimes hallucinated intermediate bars that should not exist. He traced this problem to the limited representation of engineering-specific tasks in the training data of LLMs, which are predominantly trained on general internet content. His solution was to decompose complex instructions into a sequence of basic operations—such as find, move, copy, create, and delete—which the model handled reliably.

A second challenge was the “messed up output” problem, where the AI would corrupt the model regardless of the user’s request, often due to overloaded or noisy context. Alexei demonstrated that even within token limits, the quality of context is crucial; extraneous or irrelevant information can pollute the model’s internal summary, leading to errors. He illustrated this with an experiment using a local language model, showing that excessive “helpful” context could confuse the model and degrade output quality. His key recommendation was to keep the context clean and focused, avoiding the temptation to overload prompts with tool definitions, manuals, or formatting rules.

In conclusion, Alexei argued that AI integration can deliver significant value for engineers working under deadline pressure to produce design packages. He emphasized the need for collaboration among developers (to build plugins and connect tools to AI), AI ambassadors (to educate and support non-coders), and engineers (to remain open-minded and adaptable). He estimated that even modest AI assistants could save one to four days per month per employee, and projected that, at scale, this could translate to thousands of hours saved annually in a large engineering division. During the Q&A, he clarified that the prototype targets model modification tasks rather than repetitive duplication, offering substantial time savings for common detailing and design changes. He also shared that the tool was in a testing phase at Sweco, with plans for broader rollout and extension to other engineering software platforms within the business unit.

title: Bentley Copilot and Python Assistant for Engineering Workflows
summary: Daniel Eng and LouisMartin Losier presented Bentley Copilot and Python Assistant as productivity tools for AEC workflows. Bentley Copilot helps users navigate software and comply with standards such as FDOT CAD guidelines, while Python Assistant lets engineers automate tasks through natural-language-driven scripting for dimensioning, drawing production, and onboarding.
transcript:
Dan Eng, a MicroStation product expert at Bentley, and Dr. Louis Martin Lozier, Senior Product Manager for AI in MicroStation, presented Bentley’s AI-driven workflows for accelerating infrastructure project delivery.

Dan opened with the broader market context. Citing a forecast from McKinsey, he said that by September 2025, around $106 trillion in investment was expected over the next 15 years to meet demand for new and upgraded infrastructure. He noted that everyone in the room is part of the ecosystem needed to deliver that work.

He argued that infrastructure projects are becoming larger, more complex, and subject to tighter deadlines, while traditional workflows are no longer sufficient to keep up. The challenge is compounded by rapid urbanization, aging infrastructure, climate resilience pressures, and a shortage of engineers in the workforce.

Bentley’s stated vision is to empower the next generation of infrastructure design by leveraging AI to accelerate project delivery without compromising quality. Their goals were to develop purpose-built AI solutions for infrastructure professionals and to create context-aware AI tools and features that address users’ most pressing problems.

Louis Martin emphasized that infrastructure generates huge amounts of data, but AI can only use that data effectively when it is standardized, connected, and contextualized. He said Bentley Infrastructure Cloud is intended to connect and contextualize data so that AI can produce meaningful and reliable outcomes. He also stressed that engineering requires trustworthy AI because precision and consequences matter. AI in infrastructure must remain human-in-the-loop.

He explained Bentley’s AI principles around data governance and trust. Unlike some software vendors, Bentley’s position is that users’ data remains their data. Their approach to data sharing is opt-in rather than opt-out. Users retain control over whether and how their data is used for AI, whether it contributes to model training that benefits others, and Bentley aims to be transparent about how AI models are created and what data was used. He acknowledged that this limits how fast Bentley can move, but said it is foundational to responsible AI development and user trust.

Dan then described Bentley’s continuous discovery process. Bentley held workshops in Kansas City and London with product managers, UX designers, developers, product experts, and users. Through interviews and user journey mapping, they focused on real pain points in infrastructure project delivery. They asked users where workflow bottlenecks occur, where delays happen in daily work, and which tasks consume the most time.

From those workshops, Bentley identified several recurring themes:

- Manual processes dominate and create long delays.
- Knowledge gaps between peers and colleagues slow decision-making.
- Drawing production and labeling remain major bottlenecks and often lead to costly rework.

Dan recalled one engineer summarizing the issue by saying, “This isn’t an engineering problem, it’s a time problem.”

From this discovery process, Bentley organized its work into three themes:

1. Automation
2. Learning and help
3. Drawing production

Under automation, Bentley introduced a Python editor and Python Assistant inside MicroStation. These are intended to enable scripting and workflow automation for manual tasks, freeing engineers to focus on higher-value work.

Under learning and help, Bentley introduced Bentley Copilot to support onboarding and contextual guidance. Under drawing production, Bentley developed AI-assisted annotation and labeling tools.

Louis Martin then walked through a series of demos.

First, he showed an example in which an engineer needs to place safety barriers along a complex roadway. Traditionally, this would involve hours of repetitive manual work and rework whenever the alignment changes. With the Python Assistant, the engineer describes the intent in natural language, and AI generates a script that places the barrier automatically and aligns it to the design geometry. If the road changes, the engineer can rerun the script in seconds. Louis Martin characterized this as democratizing automation because the user does not need to be a programmer; they can simply ask for the automation they need.

A second example focused on dimensioning, one of the most repetitive and time-consuming parts of drawing production. Engineers often spend hours placing and updating dimensions manually, especially as designs evolve. Using the Python Assistant, the engineer describes the desired dimensioning behavior and AI helps generate a script that applies dimensions automatically based on the model geometry. When the design changes, the dimensions can be updated consistently by rerunning the script. He noted that after that, the engineer can still manually adjust the dimensions if needed.

Another example addressed onboarding and software transition. He described an engineer familiar with AutoCAD joining a project in MicroStation. Even with experience, switching software can reduce productivity. With Bentley Copilot, the user provides their background and intent, and Copilot uses contextual memory to translate concepts, commands, and terminology into something familiar. It guides the user step by step within the active design context, including where the tools are located and how equivalent terminology differs between AutoCAD and MicroStation. The result is faster onboarding, less disruption, and more confident execution.

Louis Martin also demonstrated a compliance-oriented use case. An engineer working as a consultant for the Florida Department of Transportation needs to ensure that a design complies with FDOT standards. Using Bentley Copilot, the engineer can attach the FDOT manual as a PDF and ask Copilot to compare the project’s level naming conventions against the standard. Copilot reviews the file and highlights where the design is compliant and where it is not. Instead of manual checking, the engineer receives immediate, actionable feedback before submission, reducing downstream rework.

A drawing production example in OpenRoads Designer showed an intersection with standard annotations that were difficult to read and inconsistent. Adjusting labels manually would take time and likely create rework. Using an AI label optimizer, labels were automatically repositioned and refined for clarity based on a trained annotation model. The engineer then used Bentley Copilot to ask how to annotate specific geometry, run a geometry report, and query applicable design standards, such as minimum design speed based on traffic volume. The combination of AI-driven labeling and contextual assistance produced clearer, more standards-aligned drawings more quickly and with fewer manual edits.

A final example in OpenSite Plus focused on site civil design. An engineer designing a land development project needs to move quickly from concept to detailed design without switching tools or losing context. In OpenSite Plus, data can be brought in easily and geometry created contextually—for example, generating a pad site based on required size and slope, then positioning it precisely using northing and easting coordinates. In the demonstration, the terrain was re-optimized, which reduced earthwork and potentially saved project cost.

Dan concluded that Bentley’s AI-powered features provide value through:

- Automation with Python scripting and AI assistance using natural language
- AI-powered drawing production support
- Copilot assistance that reduces manual information retrieval and supports optimized analysis and layout decisions

He emphasized that these are purpose-built AI tools for infrastructure delivery, that AI-assisted design is not about replacing engineers, and that the industry is still only at the beginning of what can be unlocked.

In Q&A, an attendee asked how Bentley trains systems if customer data remains private and cannot be used freely for training. Louis Martin responded that Bentley relies on internal expertise and curated materials. Bentley has many subject matter experts and experienced engineers who create design examples and training content. The same applies to documentation and learning content: they create validated question-and-answer sets and train models from those. In some scenarios, users can also provide their own documents, such as PDFs, which the system can ingest and query without turning that into Bentley-owned training data.

Another attendee asked about distributing Python snippets or components created through this workflow. Dan replied that users can create their own Python scripts, and Bentley provides API access and a GitHub repository with approximately 170 examples to help users get started. Additional examples are added with MicroStation releases. If users want to contribute, they would likely do so through GitHub.
detailed_summary:
Dan Eng and Dr. Louis Martin Lozier’s presentation centered on Bentley’s vision for transforming infrastructure project delivery through AI-driven workflows, specifically via the integration of Bentley Copilot and a Python Assistant within MicroStation and related engineering tools. Their main thesis was that the scale, complexity, and urgency of modern infrastructure demands cannot be met by traditional workflows, and that purpose-built, trustworthy AI solutions are essential to empower engineers, accelerate delivery, and maintain quality.

Dan Eng began by framing the challenge: with a McKinsey forecast projecting $106 trillion in infrastructure investment over the next 15 years, the industry faces unprecedented pressure from urbanization, aging assets, climate resilience needs, and a shrinking engineering workforce. He argued that the bottleneck is not engineering capability but time—manual processes, knowledge silos, and repetitive drawing production tasks are the true obstacles. Bentley’s response is to leverage AI to automate routine work, bridge knowledge gaps, and streamline drawing production, all while keeping engineers at the center of the process.

Dr. Louis Martin Lozier elaborated on the foundational role of data in AI for infrastructure. He emphasized that AI’s value depends on standardized, connected, and contextualized data, which is the purpose of the Bentley Infrastructure Cloud. He also underscored Bentley’s commitment to data governance and user trust: users retain ownership of their data, participation in data sharing is opt-in, and Bentley is transparent about model training and data provenance. This approach, while slower than more aggressive data harvesting strategies, is positioned as essential for responsible AI in a safety- and precision-critical domain.

The presenters described Bentley’s continuous discovery process, which included workshops in Kansas City and London with stakeholders across product management, UX, development, and end users. Through interviews and journey mapping, Bentley identified three primary pain points: the dominance of manual processes, knowledge gaps that slow decisions, and persistent bottlenecks in drawing production and labeling. These insights shaped Bentley’s AI initiatives into three themes: automation, learning and help, and drawing production.

Under automation, Bentley introduced a Python editor and Python Assistant within MicroStation. These tools allow engineers to automate repetitive tasks by generating scripts from natural language descriptions, democratizing automation for users without programming backgrounds. For example, Louis Martin demonstrated how an engineer could use the Python Assistant to automatically place safety barriers along a complex roadway alignment—a task that would otherwise require hours of manual effort and be prone to errors if the design changed. The AI-generated script could be rerun instantly whenever the alignment was updated.

For drawing production, the Python Assistant was shown to automate dimensioning, another labor-intensive task. Engineers could describe their intent, and the AI would generate scripts to apply and update dimensions based on model geometry, with manual adjustments still possible as needed. In OpenRoads Designer, an AI label optimizer automatically repositioned and clarified annotations, reducing rework and improving drawing clarity. Bentley Copilot further enhanced drawing production by providing contextual assistance—answering queries about annotation, generating geometry reports, and referencing relevant design standards in real time.

In the realm of learning and help, Bentley Copilot was presented as a contextual AI assistant that supports onboarding and workflow guidance. A notable example involved an engineer transitioning from AutoCAD to MicroStation; Copilot translated commands and concepts, provided step-by-step guidance, and mapped terminology between platforms, reducing the learning curve and minimizing productivity loss. In compliance scenarios, Copilot could ingest standards documents (such as the FDOT manual) and compare project files against requirements, providing immediate feedback on compliance and reducing the risk of costly rework.

A final demo in OpenSite Plus illustrated how AI-powered tools enable rapid, context-aware site design. Engineers could generate and position site geometry based on project requirements, optimize terrain, and reduce earthwork—all within a single environment, streamlining the transition from concept to detailed design.

In conclusion, Dan Eng reiterated that Bentley’s AI-powered features—automation via Python scripting and natural language, AI-driven drawing production, and Copilot assistance—are designed to augment, not replace, engineers. These tools address real-world bottlenecks and knowledge gaps, and Bentley’s approach to responsible AI ensures user trust and data integrity. The presenters acknowledged that the industry is only beginning to realize AI’s potential in infrastructure, and Bentley’s roadmap is shaped by continuous user engagement and ethical AI principles.

During the Q&A, Louis Martin clarified that Bentley’s AI models are trained primarily on internally curated data and expertise, not on customer data unless explicitly permitted. Users can also provide documents for context-specific queries without contributing to Bentley’s training data. Dan Eng noted that Bentley supports community-driven script sharing via GitHub, with a growing repository of Python examples and open API access, encouraging user contributions and collaborative innovation.

title: VIKTOR App Store for Python-Based Engineering Automation
summary: Peter Madlener showcased VIKTOR as a platform for creating, connecting, and sharing Python-based engineering apps. The demo highlighted 3D geometry visualization, integration with local and cloud software, and workflow orchestration features aimed at improving transparency and collaboration across projects.
transcript:
Peter, CEO and co-founder of VIKTOR, presented how VIKTOR supports engineering automation through Python-based web apps and an app store model, and demonstrated how AI-assisted “vibe coding” can dramatically speed up development.

He introduced VIKTOR’s mission as empowering engineers to build, connect, and share their own tools. He emphasized that he is an engineer himself and that before founding VIKTOR with friends from Delft University of Technology, they had started a bridge-building company that manufactured fiber-reinforced plastic bridges and fully automated much of that process. That engineering background shaped the platform.

When VIKTOR started in 2017, the team believed the platform needed a few core building blocks:

- It should be Python-based.
- Engineers should be able to create web apps so tools are easy to share.
- Those apps should live in an app store so engineers can discover and use them.

Over roughly eight years, VIKTOR grew to around 100,000 apps on the platform and is used on major projects and by large engineering firms and contractors around the world.

Peter said the company learned that automation makes engineers much more productive, but there was a major bottleneck: skills and time. Python is powerful and effective, but only a small percentage of engineers in most organizations can write good Python code. He showed this as a persistent gap across experience levels.

That became the major opportunity for VIKTOR. Because Python is so widespread, it is well represented in the data used to train large language models. VIKTOR recognized early that AI could help bridge the skills gap. Around three years ago, shortly after ChatGPT launched, the company began experimenting with an internal initiative called “Hector” as a play on the company name.

To demonstrate why this matters, Peter returned to his background in bridge design. Designing one of their fiber-reinforced plastic bridges involved several activities:

- Structural analysis, often in Excel
- Reporting
- Production documentation

He used that workflow as the basis for a live demonstration.

Before building the app, he briefly showed the VIKTOR platform: a project structure, development environment, app store, user management, and integration layer for both local and cloud systems.

He then loaded a prompt into VIKTOR’s app builder instead of coding from scratch. He explained that, as an engineer, he thought about the design process step by step and encoded that logic in a prompt:

- Define a cross-section with top, bottom, sides, and webs.
- Visualize that geometry and make it parameterized.
- Determine stiffness, using material properties relevant to fiber-reinforced plastic.
- Combine the 2D geometry and the mechanical properties of each layer to derive the mechanical properties of the bridge as a whole.
- Determine bridge deformation according to a relevant standard.

At that moment, VIKTOR was using Anthropic Claude 4.6. Peter explained that the LLM receives not only the prompt but also all the context VIKTOR has built over the years: the software development kit, geometry tools, reporting tools, and other engineering-specific functionality. Within VIKTOR’s own instance, the model then generates Python code that is specific to the VIKTOR framework.

The result was a working app that let him define mechanical properties, calculate bending stiffness, and calculate bending deformation. He described this as effectively replacing an Excel sheet, but in a more powerful and shareable form.

Peter then extended the app further. He added new capabilities through additional prompting:

- Create a 3D geometry of the bridge
- Include an arch in the bridge geometry rather than a flat profile
- Add a person for scale
- Enable geometry download
- Generate reporting automatically
- Fix a bug

He said this transformed the tool from an Excel replacement into something much more capable.

He also demonstrated adding an LLM directly into the app as an agentic component. This is built into VIKTOR, so when a user logs in they can add an LLM to the app itself. One reason for doing so is trust and transparency. Users may want to understand what the app does rather than treat it as a black box. He showed the app being asked to explain what it does, what design guidelines it uses, and to list all thirty vectors. This illustrated how users can interact not only with the app interface but also with the app logic through natural language.

He noted that VIKTOR also supports collaborative prompting: one engineer can begin building an app and another can continue prompting and refining it.
