Third, Sharifan highlighted a significant blind spot: research on serial social housing at scale is almost absent, with most studies concentrating on monumental or individual heritage buildings. This neglect is problematic given the scale and urgency of the social housing challenge. Fourth, human factors—such as occupant behavior, social dynamics, and cultural value—are routinely omitted from current frameworks, despite their centrality to sustainable rehabilitation. Sharifan referenced the New European Bauhaus initiative, which calls for heritage that is sustainable, inclusive, and beautiful, underscoring the need to integrate social and political dimensions into technical solutions.

Mapping the research landscape, Sharifan showed that most work remains focused on documentation for individual heritage buildings, with little attention to stock-scale prediction and decision support for social housing. The conclusion is clear: current tools are inadequate for the strategic, territorial-scale rehabilitation needed. The review thus establishes a foundation and justification for developing new, integrated AI-HBIM frameworks capable of operating at both the building and stock scale, explicitly tailored to the unique conditions of 20th-century social housing.

Sharifan concluded with three key takeaways: the landscape is fragmented, with AI and HBIM still largely separate; research is misaligned, overemphasizing short-term metrics at the expense of long-term, holistic approaches; and there is an urgent need for integrated, scalable solutions. Future work will focus on developing and testing such frameworks in real case studies in Lille and other French cities, with the aim of creating adaptable models that can incorporate diverse data sources—including heritage value, architectural design, and resident input—and be applied across Europe. In the Q&A, Sharifan elaborated on the importance of stakeholder engagement and the potential for AI-enabled tools to facilitate data collection from residents, as well as the adaptability of the proposed framework to different national contexts.

title: Comparing CNNs and Vision-Language Models for Object Detection in 2D Construction Drawings
summary: Christopher Mouflard presented a comparative study of convolutional neural networks and vision-language models for detecting objects in 2D construction drawings. He explained how hybrid approaches using voting mechanisms and Intersection over Union (IOU) metrics can improve both accuracy and efficiency. The talk stressed that the construction industry's continued dependence on 2D drawings remains a major challenge and opportunity for AI adoption.
transcript:
Christopher Mulhart: Hello everybody. I am Christopher Mulhart. I have been in the BIM space since about 2008, and over the last three years I have been investigating convolutional neural networks and related methods in a more practical way.

This is not to say it is the death of BIM, but the reality is that construction happens in a 2D drawing. That is what we are going to talk about.

I am here on behalf of my colleagues Hammad and Khan, who could not be here today. We are going to talk about what I tried to make into the most boring title possible: “Agentic versus convolutional neural networks: a comparison for object detection in 2D geometry.”

The reason I bring this up is that architects and engineers ultimately have to deal with people like me—people who used to be carpenters and just wanted to know where to put the stuff exactly as intended. The result of that need is the 2D drawing. That is the thing that actually happens.

One example is a door schedule. This is what you would expect to see, and we will talk about why that is important.

The real title I thought would be more interesting is: “Why you cannot yet just use Claude Code to successfully read construction drawings.” That is really what we are going to investigate.

On the other side is another architectural drawing, another construction document. It has tiny tags everywhere, and that is the kind of material we are dealing with. I spent a couple of hours last week trying Claude Code, and even Opus 4.6, the latest version, cannot do it. I am not saying that pessimistically. I am actually very optimistic about this work.

So what is going on in 2D drawings?

According to Autodesk, there was $65 billion a year in rework because construction drawings were not coordinated and were missing information.

Why? Because owners ask people to build things, architects design them, then hand them to engineers who check them. The architects then get approval to send them to the general contractor to build. The GC checks the drawings, sends them to subcontractors, and they check the drawings too. The whole thing starts over again when the project gets greenlit. There are many points in the process where human brains have to check things—for compliance, spelling errors, and many small but critical issues.

Why does this matter?

Because semantically checking that tags are actually in a schedule is extremely boring work—but it is also extremely important. You need to know how many doors are there and understand the details associated with them.

A company called Revizto Check in the United States wrote a 270-page book on how to read construction drawings. They describe three broad categories of issues: surface-level issues, which are basically whether something is present or absent; subsurface issues, which require some inference; and root-level issues, which are true constructability problems, such as a structural wall conflicting with a mechanical duct. That was the promise of BIM.

I want to make this concrete with a story. Harry—who is my wife’s younger brother, but I love him like my own brother—was doing his university degree and got a chance to intern at a construction company. He was given too much scope for his experience and made a $100,000 error.

He ordered more windows than there were openings.

Why? Because he trusted that the schedule would be correct. He did not know to count the openings; he just trusted that the architect would have updated the schedule and that everything would be perfect. This is a real problem, and it is why we need technologies like this. We need to teach people how to read construction drawings, but we also need tools that help.

What am I really going to talk about? Convolutional neural networks—broadly known as computer vision. These systems are cheap, efficient, and accurate, but they are weak at learning on the fly. In our experiment, we used a YOLO model, a type of computer vision model. We trained it on construction data that took about three weeks to annotate, meaning we manually drew bounding boxes around objects and fed that into a pipeline. Then we asked it to find those objects in new data.

It is really cheap—less than a penny to look for this sort of thing—and the inference time was around 30 seconds. So the pipeline was drawing input, CNN/computer vision, and then post-processing.

Of course, there are also vision-language models. Because I am perpetually paranoid that AI is going to take my job, we also built a similar pipeline using Gemini 3.0, Claude 4, and related models. We looked at the whole page, then broke the page into 5-by-5 and 3-by-3 segments. We tried to improve results that way, but we were always struggling against the context window—that is, how many tokens you can spend relative to how much information the model can absorb and return. There are limitations not only in cost but also in the time required per class of object we asked it to find.

This is where it gets interesting. In construction and architecture, design intent is important, but the exact location and accuracy of things is critical. So we decided to play these approaches off each other. We asked whether we could integrate a voting system between the VLMs and the CNNs and see whether each could contribute to a more accurate result.

What we found relates to the key metric called intersection over union. This measures how well the predicted location of an object overlaps the true location. We found that the total accuracy improved dramatically when we combined the systems. This is a mixture-of-experts architecture: a more deterministic computer vision model and a more probabilistic VLM playing well together.

Breaking this down further, we studied the timing.

The YOLO models took about 30 seconds per page, making them by far the most responsive. What surprised me was the latency we introduced in the hybrid model when we played the systems off each other.

Then there was cost. I do not think most people are going to train their own YOLO model, so they probably will not get those dramatic cost efficiencies or build one from scratch. They are more likely to use a VLM. But those context windows compound costs quickly, and I will say more about that in a second. We did see a cost reduction in the hybrid system relative to a pure VLM workflow.

We also looked at precision and recall, which are what we really care about in machine learning. How well did the model recognize what it saw, and how well did it recall what it thought it saw?

Again, the CNN was way faster and more responsive, but the hybrid systems really did show a strong increase in total accuracy and especially recall, which is extremely important.

The LLMs and VLMs still had very low recall, and that is the critical measure. If you think of the brain, it is like recognizing someone’s face but not remembering their name. That is the thing we need the VLMs to do better. Even when testing the latest 4.6 models, that recall is not quite there yet.

That may be because only a tiny fraction of the internet consists of construction data. There is also a recent paper arguing that multimodal large language models are shape-blind, and I think that matters a lot for the construction industry. These models may be very good at analyzing text and fairly good at numbers, but we work in a world where everything is represented by some kind of shape with data inside it.

Now to the results.

What you are looking at here is a construction drawing. The little circles with triangle hats are elevation markers. The blue bounding boxes indicate what the model found. This CNN model was about 84% accurate. I went through and added red boxes to show what it missed.

In this case, the VLM was actually a little bit better for some reason, but it was extremely expensive. What cost me a penny in one workflow cost several dollars in the other. That is just one page. So if we are asking these systems to look through hundreds or thousands of pages, cost becomes a serious consideration.

The best results were definitely in the hybrid system, where we tried to leverage both models together.

I also want to step back and talk more about the VLMs and why they performed better when operating within the context of a CNN than when operating alone. If you imagine taking a sheet, breaking it into a 3-by-3 grid, and feeding those grids back to the LLM, or feeding the whole document with some assistance from the CNN, you can compare the outcomes.

In these examples, red highlights where the object actually is and blue shows what the LLM thought it saw. Again, there are highly geometric shapes everywhere in technical drawings, and the LLM by itself, even with a larger context window, was terrible.

Even when we added more prompting and used a voting system, it still was not very good on its own. But when we gave it just a little input—the intersection-over-union-based support from the CNN—the results improved.

So what happened?

The CNN still has the problem that it cannot learn on the fly very well. It struggles with imperfect shapes, rasterized tags, and all the realities of construction drawings, which are never perfect. Even within the same architecture firm, depending on the design team, you can get two entirely different types of PDF documents.

In the VLM case, many of the results were extremely ambiguous. It missed small tags, as you saw in the examples, even with an expanded context window. Keep in mind that these were VLMs from the previous generation, but I do not necessarily see them getting dramatically better for this specific use case very soon.

They hallucinated in highly complex areas. One important thing we found was that in the hybrid case, when the VLM was wrong and the CNN was right, the VLM could still override the CNN. That speaks to overconfidence. It was just too confident, and it would push the bounding box away from the correct location.

My final “why” is this: why does all of this matter?

Because we are at an engineering conference on AI. If AI technology is not extremely accurate in the output it gives you, then can it be trusted? Is it usable in its current form for anything beyond helping you create a visualization or write an email? In our domain, pinpoint accuracy has to be king.

I fundamentally believe we are in an age of staff augmentation, not team replacement.

And yes, I get it—BIM, and I love BIM—but PDF is still the way nearly all projects are permitted, contracted, and built. It all happens through the 2D drawing.

The prompt control I mentioned is extremely critical. You need very tight guardrails around how the prompt is managed. That is how you manage the confidence level of the LLM.

A simple example is that I like to call the LLM “OCR, but worse.” It had real difficulty distinguishing between a numeral 1, a lowercase l, and an uppercase I. Traditional OCR systems, which have existed for a long time, are very good at that. So we need to think about these tools in combination rather than assuming one of them can do everything.

That is my final point: these hybrid systems work well today to solve construction problems right now. In the future, perhaps world models or newer architectures will change the landscape, but for now we have immediate construction problems to solve.

Back in 2008, I thought that 10 years later everything would be in BIM. That was nearly 20 years ago. Yesterday I met with my old boss and joked that we should have built Vico for today—it probably would have worked.

Q&A

Audience member: From a data science perspective, accuracy is not always a sufficient metric. You also have to consider false positives, false negatives, and related rates. And I wondered why fine-tuning was not mentioned—hyperparameters like learning rate and batch size matter a lot, and people using these models may not have a data science background. How do you deal with that?

Christopher Mulhart: I do not expect a normal user—and by that I mean someone like a project engineer such as Harry—to care about batch sizes, workers, or any of that. That is why technology innovators and people building solutions are a critical part of this workflow. It goes back to my point that you cannot just use Claude Code to solve this; you will get terrible results.

On our side, this involved a lot of experimentation. We did some things that most people would consider slightly dangerous. I decided to build my own computer, so we built one, put it in my kitchen, and ran experiment after experiment after experiment.

That gave us the initial inputs to understand how to tune the models correctly—what batch sizes to use, where the memory caps out, and how to make these systems function properly on an AWS server. It is one thing to have large, memory-hungry models, but another to make them practical for field use.

So yes, it was a lot of experimentation to determine what was optimal for the result we wanted, so that we could build something meaningful for the end user who does not care about the internals. They just want to press a button and get a result. That is the beauty of the GPT ecosystem: you press a button and get an answer, and you assume it is right. In our case, we wanted to produce output that people could see visually, because construction is a visual industry.

Audience member: If you give more context to the VLM, will it affect the accuracy?

Christopher Mulhart: Absolutely. These things have to be very carefully containerized, and the way you structure the prompt matters a lot. Earlier in the conference someone mentioned that when they gave too broad a prompt, the outcome was terrible.

We are seeing that inconsistency in the output on the same document with the same prompt can already be too risky to use. That is a major consideration. This might work well for my own workflow, but how do I scale that to the next person or the next company? That is where the risk comes in, and maybe that is why LLMs are not always going to be the answer. That is really the whole point of this presentation.
detailed_summary:
Christopher Mulhart’s presentation addressed the persistent challenge of object detection in 2D construction drawings, comparing the effectiveness of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and vision-language models (VLMs) for this task. His central thesis is that, despite the promise of advanced AI, no single model or architecture currently solves the nuanced, accuracy-critical problems inherent in interpreting construction documents. Instead, hybrid approaches that combine the strengths of deterministic computer vision and probabilistic language models offer the most practical path forward for the construction industry today.

Mulhart began by contextualizing the enduring importance of 2D drawings in construction workflows, despite decades of Building Information Modeling (BIM) advocacy. He underscored that, regardless of digital advances, construction projects are still permitted, contracted, and built from 2D PDFs. The stakes are high: according to Autodesk, $65 billion is lost annually in rework due to uncoordinated or incomplete drawings. He illustrated the real-world consequences with a case study involving a novice project engineer who, trusting an inaccurate door schedule, ordered $100,000 worth of unnecessary windows—highlighting the critical need for reliable, automated drawing interpretation.

The technical core of the talk compared two main approaches. First, Mulhart described the use of CNNs, specifically the YOLO (You Only Look Once) model, for object detection. The workflow involved labor-intensive manual annotation—drawing bounding boxes around objects in construction drawings—followed by training the model to recognize these objects in new documents. The CNN approach proved highly efficient, with inference costs under a penny per page and processing times around 30 seconds. However, CNNs struggled with the variability and imperfections of real-world construction drawings, such as rasterized tags and inconsistent document formats, and lacked the ability to adapt or learn on the fly.

In parallel, Mulhart and his team experimented with VLMs, including Gemini 3.0 and Claude 4, leveraging their ability to process both visual and textual information. They attempted to mitigate the models’ context window limitations by segmenting pages into grids (5x5 and 3x3) and feeding these to the models. Despite these efforts, VLMs suffered from high costs—several dollars per page—and significant latency. More critically, their recall rates remained low, meaning they often failed to identify all relevant objects, even with the latest model versions (e.g., Claude Opus 4.6). Mulhart attributed this to the scarcity of construction-specific data in the training corpora and recent findings that multimodal LLMs are “shape-blind,” a significant handicap in a domain where geometric accuracy is paramount.

The most promising results emerged from a hybrid “mixture-of-experts” architecture, where CNNs and VLMs were combined in a voting system. This approach leveraged the speed and precision of CNNs with the contextual reasoning of VLMs, yielding substantial improvements in intersection-over-union (IoU) accuracy and especially recall. However, the hybrid system introduced additional latency and complexity, and Mulhart cautioned that VLMs could sometimes override correct CNN predictions due to overconfidence, leading to misplaced bounding boxes.

Throughout, Mulhart emphasized the importance of practical usability. He argued that end users—project engineers and field staff—should not be burdened with tuning hyperparameters or understanding model internals. Instead, technology innovators must shoulder the responsibility of experimentation and optimization, as his team did by building custom hardware and iteratively refining their models for real-world deployment on AWS.

In conclusion, Mulhart asserted that while neither CNNs nor VLMs alone are sufficient for the demands of construction document analysis, hybrid systems can deliver actionable results today. He stressed the necessity of tight prompt control and robust guardrails for VLMs, likening their current reliability to “OCR, but worse.” Looking ahead, he expressed optimism that future architectures—perhaps world models or more construction-aware multimodal systems—may eventually close the gap. For now, however, the industry must rely on carefully engineered combinations of existing tools to address the immediate, high-stakes challenges of construction drawing interpretation.

title: Building AI Capability in Construction Teams Through Structured Upskilling
summary: In this keynote, the speakers examined why AI adoption in construction lags behind individual experimentation, highlighting organizational barriers such as data security fears, cultural resistance, and lack of readiness for disruption. The presentation centered on a five-step AI capability uplift framework, an AI capability pyramid for segmenting users from novices to innovators, and practical enablement methods including literacy training, persona-based programs, office hours, community platforms like Viva Engage, and support for citizen developers. It also emphasized leadership buy-in, identifying power users, and closing the gap between rapid AI innovation and slower organizational adoption.
transcript:
Speaker: Bragan

We keep seeing headlines across popular media about how the AI future is already here. That reflects both the opportunity in front of us and the way work is changing through AI. What I find especially interesting is the second half of that idea: our AI future is already here, but it is not evenly distributed.

As we have heard throughout the day, the value of AI is being captured unevenly. A small proportion of firms are actually able to execute and implement AI effectively. One question I have always had is: why is that the case?

There is a real adoption gap in AI. In a report by Places Solutions on AI intelligence in construction, more than 400 people in our industry were surveyed. The results were surprising. On the ground, adoption is happening: 74% said they use ChatGPT or a similar model daily or weekly. But when asked whether their company is actually ready to use AI and withstand the coming disruption, 68%—about two-thirds—said their companies are not ready for the future.

Why are we seeing AI being used, while companies themselves are not ready? In our industry, AI is seen as high potential but also high risk. We see headlines repeatedly about people accidentally copying and pasting or uploading information they should not be putting into ChatGPT. More than one in ten people are seen to be doing this now.

In AEC, we work with confidential data: project data, owner and client data, and internal intellectual property. Many companies are rightly concerned about increasing exposure to these tools. A recent Dodge Construction Network survey found that one of the biggest barriers to adoption is concern about the security of company data. Data leakage through everyday use is a real issue.

So how do we resolve this? My view is that education is the way forward. We can implement learning and training programs that improve AI adoption in a safe and responsible way. Continuing on from the Dodge Construction Network survey, we are seeing this on the ground. Forty percent of teams are starting to allocate AI budgets, and 38% are creating implementation teams focused on rolling AI out safely and securely.

In this presentation, I want to show you exactly how to do that. I will walk through how to design, deliver, and operationalize an AI capability uplift and training program. My goal is that each of you walks away understanding how to map AI literacy in your organization and what training to provide to engineers and other people on the ground so they can start using AI and help address the adoption gap.

A quick background on me: I was a civil engineer at AECOM, working in pavements and aviation from both design and construction perspectives, including railway and aviation projects. I have also worked in innovation, embedded in a government infrastructure authority, where I worked on AI training and capability uplift programs. We designed and delivered workshops across the entire organization.

I also write a newsletter called Last Week in ConTech, which helps people stay up to date on construction tech news. I am currently on sabbatical, helping out as platform lead and head of content for Formwork Labs, an accelerator backed by Brick & Mortar Ventures.

My interest in education goes back even further. Before construction, I worked for a software agency called AirDev, where we built education programs to help people learn low-code systems. Ultimately, more than 10,000 people came through our bootcamps and were trained to work on client projects.

So how do we actually build an AI capability uplift program?

I want to take you back two years, when we recognized that AI was coming and starting to be used in our organization, but we did not know how to increase adoption. After brainstorming and workshops, we came to the conclusion that if we wanted to increase adoption in a safe and responsible way, we needed a structured training program.

This is especially important in AEC for a few reasons. First, there was no existing training tailored to our project context. Most of what we saw came from OpenAI, Claude, YouTube creators, and others, but none of it really understood the realities of projects. Second, as a government infrastructure authority, we held a lot of confidential data and had strong public-sector constraints. External providers we tried to work with did not really understand that context or the implications for AI guidelines.

So we developed a five-step framework for building the program.

Step one was assessing capability. We needed to understand where AI literacy and adoption were in the organization.

Step two was mapping personas. We grouped people into broad categories and then developed training specific to those categories so they could move up in their level of AI adoption.

Step three was training them in AI literacy.

Step four recognized that AI is constantly changing. In just the last few months, we have seen AI agents being deployed and new tools emerge. We needed a training approach that was not only structured and formalized, but also dynamic enough to adapt to new ways of working.

Step five was building citizen developers—our power users. How do you empower them? How do you help them feel safe and responsible in how they use AI?

Let me walk through step one: the survey.

The first question was simple: how often do you currently use AI? That gave us a quick sense of AI literacy and capability levels in the organization.

The second question was: do you use AI as part of your work today? Many people use AI tools personally, but do not feel comfortable using them in a work context, so we wanted to understand the difference.

The third question was: what is your biggest concern about using AI at work? People avoid using AI for many reasons, and any training program has to address those objections directly.

Finally, we asked: what topics would you most like to learn about? If you want adoption, the training must be relevant to project contexts and specific use cases.

The results were interesting. We saw concerns around job loss and data leakage. People were genuinely scared of using ChatGPT or similar tools at work because they feared making a mistake, having it become public, and losing their jobs. That was especially true in a public authority context, so we had to address those fears proactively.

We also realized that many people simply were not sure they were allowed to use these tools. Even though we had sent communications saying they could, people still did not understand how.

Another major challenge was knowing where to learn. Even if you care about AI, there are not many obvious places to go. There are a few people online, but you need to know who they are. Inside an organization, people often do not know whether to talk to innovation, IT, or someone else. Who actually owns AI, and who can help?

From that, we mapped an AI capability pyramid.

At the base, we found that about 90 to 95% of the organization were AI novices. These were people who had either never used AI before or were scared and apprehensive due to security and data risks. Many were not technical and did not understand the software behind it.

For them, we created an AI crash course: AI 101. We brought them into a room, got them to sign up for ChatGPT, and had them start using it through guided exercises so they could experience the value firsthand. The idea was to put the tools in their hands and let them use them safely and securely, reducing risk through supervised use.

Before that, we covered some quick theory. First: what is AI and how does it work? You put in a prompt, you get information back, and what you write changes the answer. People needed to understand that basic interaction.

Second, we addressed strengths and limitations. Hallucination is one of the biggest concerns people have. If AI hallucinates once, many people stop using it. So we explained how hallucination works, the importance of human-in-the-loop systems, and the fact that AI is just another tool. If people do not know when and where to use it, they will not adopt it.

Third, we shared company guidelines: how we approach AI, what safe and secure use looks like, what people are allowed to upload, and what they cannot upload.

We also mapped four capability areas and created guided tasks, demos, and exercises so every person in the workshop could start experimenting and learning. Our goal with this group was simple: help people understand AI, build confidence to experiment, give them a clear sense that they had permission to do so, and reassure them that they could use it safely without risking their jobs, as long as they followed the rules.

The next group was the AI explorers. These were people who had some experience with AI tools and understood the basic concepts, but had not yet gained real value from them. In other words, AI was not yet part of their daily workflow.

That matters, because until people are using AI on a daily basis, they are not realizing the productivity improvements we want. So the key question for this group was: how do we move from experimentation to habit?

We did that by responding directly to what people said they wanted AI for, and by creating use-case-specific training.

The first was AI for writing: how to use AI for emails, reports, and improving clarity. This was especially relevant because many employees used English as a second language. Helping them write faster, review AI-generated writing, and understand when and where to use it gave them confidence and practical value.

The second was AI for Excel. This was very popular. We taught them how to generate formulas and, critically, what they could and could not upload. Because we were using free tools rather than commercial licenses at that stage, it was essential to teach data anonymization before uploading anything.

This was also the stage where we introduced Prompting 101. For novices, we did not teach prompting frameworks because they were still learning what AI was and how experimentation worked. But once people started using AI and saw how output varied, they were ready to understand prompting. We developed our own framework, which we called the Stingray model, to help people work in a human-in-the-loop way and get faster outputs.

Our goal at this level was to turn occasional use into a consistent workflow habit, so that every time people opened Excel or started writing, they knew exactly how to use AI, when to use it, and how to do so safely without leaking data.

The next phase was the AI practitioner—what we thought of as an AI power user. These were people who used AI regularly and had embedded it into their workflows. Not everyone would get to this stage; moving from novice to explorer to practitioner happens quickly, and the people who reach it tend to be naturally curious and excited about AI.

For this group, we wanted to create power users, so we gave them more tailored training.

First, we moved to business-line-specific training. Many of these people sat in specific business teams, so we surveyed them and built process maps of how they worked today. Then we looked at how AI could fit into that process and reduce 20 steps to five or six using different tools. We trained whole teams in these new processes, using champions within those teams to demonstrate and validate the changes.

Second, we created communities. These were small groups of people excited by AI who often felt isolated in their teams. Power users are often the only one or two people in a team who really love AI, and they do not have many people to share that excitement with. Community helped us distribute information and maintain momentum.

Third, we ran AI office hours. We would sit in the lunchroom from 2:00 to 4:00 p.m. on a Wednesday, and people would come ask questions. We would spend 20 minutes jamming on their use cases and acting as AI coaches. Many people wanted to improve, but did not know where to go for help. By being accessible, we built stronger ties across the organization and helped power users build capability that they could then pass on to others.

At the top of the pyramid were AI innovators. These were typically in the IT team and were building bespoke AI projects. We were not really training them—they generally knew more than we did from a machine learning and technical standpoint—but it was valuable to recognize that they existed and that we could tap into them for support.

The goal of the pyramid was never to make everyone an AI expert. We simply wanted to increase the general capability of the organization. Someone who has never used AI can still gain a lot from experimenting and realizing how it could help. From there, we want them to become explorers and form habits. A smaller proportion will become power users, and we want to nurture them so they grow and start teaching others—much like Excel experts have traditionally done by building templates and sharing them across organizations.

To make that more concrete, over a 12-month program we started with the AI novice group and ran AI 101 workshops as quickly as possible to lift organizational capability.

After about three months, we had enough trained people that some began graduating into the explorer cohort. If 90 to 100 people were trained in the first three months, perhaps 20 to 30 would move into the next stage because they were using AI enough to be ready for more use-case-specific training.

By months five, six, and seven, we started to see demand from the business. Those first people who got excited by AI wanted the organization to use it more broadly. At the same time, we developed our power-user community.

That was really important because it became our distribution mechanism for new ideas and updates.

One thing I want to highlight is that AI capability continues to increase rapidly. While we were proud of our program, we realized very quickly that it was static. It could take months to develop a new training stream, pilot it, test it, and operationalize it. Meanwhile, AI capabilities were changing faster than our ability to train people.

OpenAI has used the term “capability overhang” to describe this. AI innovation is drastically outpacing our ability to adopt new tools and absorb capability inside our organizations. Graphically, AI capability is improving at an exponential rate, while our internal training capacity tends to improve only linearly, based on how many workshops and sessions we can deliver.

That creates a gap. A new tool or capability appears, and we simply do not have the ability to train people fast enough.

So how did we try to resolve that? By identifying power users in every workshop and pulling them into their own community.

In a workshop of 30 people, you start to notice the two or three who are most excited. They stay afterward to ask questions. They are naturally curious. It clicks for them quickly. They understand what AI is, they can see the value it brings, and they want to be more involved.

For that power-user group, we built communities around them. We used Viva Engage in Microsoft, set up a Teams community, and created a weekly AI bulletin.

The purpose of the bulletin was to capture the benefits of new AI technology and contextualize them to our roles, our company, and our industry. That was valuable because it exposed the workforce to new capability changes through the people most likely to experiment.

These power users would naturally try new tools. They would adapt interesting ideas to their teams. Because they were curious and enthusiastic, they would tell their teams when they saw something valuable—such as an AI agent doing something meaningful for their use case. And because we had already given them the scaffolding and foundation for safe and responsible AI use, they knew how to evaluate and apply those ideas. They would also come to office hours and ask us how something worked and how they could use it in their team.

Over time, we mapped these people across the organization. It was interesting to see that they did not sit in one team; they were spread cross-functionally. That gave us a more dynamic delivery mechanism. We could direct AI teachings and training toward the most engaged users and the most responsive parts of the organization. My hope was that, over time, this would improve organizational adoption by helping us see more quickly what resonated, what worked, and what was worth formalizing into training.

To finish where I started: our AI future is already here, and AI is already being applied on AEC projects and across organizations. The winners, in my view, will be the organizations that are actually able to adopt and utilize AI across their workforce.

I hope you can take something from this and use it to build programs that support the people in your organizations who are curious, excited, and ready to start using AI in their projects today.

Q&A

Audience member: Excellent talk. I meant to ask this in an earlier session, but it applies here too. How did you align incentives in the organization to encourage AI adoption? We talk about culture, but some people are creating valuable IP that is then owned by someone else. Was there consideration around that?

Bragan: It is a massive challenge. We also heard earlier about culture and how some people are excited to experiment. We were in a difficult position because we were a public-facing government organization, which traditionally adopts technology slowly. A major concern was data leakage. In previous years, we had incidents involving people speaking to the media, which reflected badly on the organization, so there was a lot of fear.

One of our biggest challenges was getting the organization aligned and backing us with a mandate to own AI training. We approached it in a very structured way: we created a SWOT analysis of key players and stakeholders and tried to figure out where we could partner with them or how our strengths could help solve their challenges.

We held individual meetings with all of these stakeholders and tried to get engagement. We had detractors, and in some cases we had to go over their heads to get executive buy-in. It was challenging because almost every team wanted some ownership. We literally mapped the people, what they cared about, what value we could provide to them, and what they were afraid of.

Some teams were worried that if we got the AI mandate, we would effectively take over their jobs. There were a lot of political dynamics to navigate. So we got as much buy-in as we could, ran pilots with people who were already supportive, and built strong grassroots evidence. Then we could go to executives and say, “We are the ones actually doing this.” That helped us secure the mandate to move forward.

It was a mix of top-down and bottom-up support. We had some top-down backing, but we had to build bottom-up support too. One cheeky trick that worked well was getting the executive assistants for the senior leaders into the training. They loved it, then told the executives how valuable it was. That became a very effective pathway to support.

Moderator: Here is a question from the remote audience. AI adoption is great, and your program shows that more people are becoming enthusiastic. But as that happens, more use cases emerge. How do you prioritize the use cases that come out of increased adoption?

Bragan: The innovation team in the organization was actually quite sophisticated. We had three teams. Our team focused on the AI mandate and idea generation. We also had an R&D team that worked with pilot solutions and startups directly on projects. Then there was a third team focused on scaling. Once we saw ROI on a particular project, that team could scale it across the organization.

What happens is that you give people the scaffolding to understand how to use AI, but then the question becomes: how do you identify what is working, what has ROI, and how to scale it? We already had that pathway because we had a history of working with startups. So if a promising use case emerged, we could hand it over to the other teams. They would build the process map, identify an AI champion, pilot it in other teams and projects, and eventually the scaling team would take over.

So for us it was a bit different because we already had that innovation structure in place. The project had had an innovation team for seven or eight years by that point.

Audience member: I want to confirm whether I understood your presentation correctly. Is there an assumption behind the adoption gap that the key barrier is people, rather than infrastructure or culture? In the company I work for, people are interested, the culture is supportive, and the data team is in place. The problem is infrastructure and data readiness, not people.

Bragan: I can give more framing for our specific context. Being in government, we had curiosity, but also a lot of fear. Culturally, we were slower to adopt and use technology. Importantly, we did not even have commercial licenses. We did not have a ChatGPT commercial license or a Copilot commercial license. Those were in the works, but government procurement cycles meant they were still six to twelve months away.

Because we were already seeing people use ChatGPT, we designed the training around free tools. No commercial licenses—just how to use free AI tools, which may use data for model training, in a safe and secure way. We covered ChatGPT, Copilot, and Claude, and we believed there were many valuable use cases—from writing emails to using Excel—that were not core engineering tasks but still delivered value.

Also, because we were a delivery infrastructure authority, other teams such as legal, finance, commercial, and marketing could benefit too.

Your infrastructure point is very important, and I have seen that in other companies. Questions like which hyperscaler to use are major discussions. But at the core, AI models like ChatGPT provide value to almost any professional. Engineers also have non-billable tasks outside their core technical work where AI can help.

We were essentially saying: we know this technology provides value. How do we contextualize it to our industry and our organization, and how do we deliver training so people can use it safely?

So yes, people were curious, but there was still a lot of resistance. In government, we had people come to AI office hours and challenge us directly, asking whether this was going to take their jobs. In the private sector, there is often more recognition that people need to improve. In government, some people were strongly against what we were doing.
detailed_summary:
Bragan’s presentation centers on the thesis that while artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming the construction industry, its adoption is highly uneven, with most organizations struggling to build the internal capability required for safe, effective, and widespread use. He argues that the primary barrier is not a lack of interest or awareness, but rather a gap in structured education and upskilling tailored to the unique needs and constraints of the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) sector. Drawing on his experience as a civil engineer and innovation lead within a government infrastructure authority, Bragan outlines a comprehensive, stepwise framework for building AI capability in construction teams, emphasizing the importance of contextualized training, organizational buy-in, and dynamic support structures.

Bragan begins by highlighting the paradox revealed in industry surveys: while a significant majority of construction professionals (74%) report using generative AI tools like ChatGPT on a daily or weekly basis, two-thirds believe their organizations are unprepared for the coming AI-driven disruption. This disconnect, he explains, stems from the high perceived risk of AI—particularly regarding data security and confidentiality—combined with a lack of clear guidance and training. He cites the Dodge Construction Network’s findings that data leakage is a top concern, especially in environments handling sensitive project and client information. These anxieties are compounded by incidents of improper data sharing and a general uncertainty about what constitutes safe AI usage.

To address these challenges, Bragan advocates for a structured, organization-wide AI capability uplift program, designed specifically for the AEC context. He details a five-step framework developed during his tenure at a government infrastructure authority. The first step is a thorough assessment of current AI literacy and adoption, conducted via targeted surveys that probe not only usage frequency but also comfort levels, perceived barriers, and desired learning topics. This diagnostic phase revealed widespread apprehension about job loss, data leakage, and a lack of clarity around organizational policies and support channels.

The second step involves mapping organizational personas—grouping staff by their familiarity and comfort with AI—and tailoring training content to each group’s needs. Bragan describes the creation of an “AI capability pyramid,” with the vast majority of staff categorized as novices, a smaller group as explorers, and a select few as practitioners or innovators. For novices, the program delivered hands-on “AI 101” workshops, demystifying basic concepts, addressing fears around hallucination and data misuse, and providing clear company guidelines. These sessions emphasized experiential learning, with guided exercises using free tools like ChatGPT, Copilot, and Claude, and a strong focus on safe, responsible experimentation.

For the explorer cohort—those with some AI experience but not yet integrating it into daily workflows—the training shifted to practical, use-case-driven modules. Bragan highlights the popularity of AI for writing (especially for non-native English speakers) and for Excel automation, with careful instruction on data anonymization and safe input practices. At this stage, participants were introduced to the “Stingray” prompting framework, developed in-house to foster effective, human-in-the-loop AI interactions.

Practitioners, or power users, received business-line-specific training, process mapping, and support in embedding AI into core workflows. Bragan underscores the importance of building communities of practice, such as Teams and Viva Engage groups, and holding regular “AI office hours” to provide ongoing coaching and peer support. These structures not only nurtured power users but also created a grassroots mechanism for disseminating new tools and best practices across the organization.

At the apex of the pyramid were AI innovators—typically IT specialists developing bespoke AI solutions—who, while not the primary focus of training, played a crucial role as internal experts and champions.

Bragan is candid about the challenges of keeping training programs current amid the exponential pace of AI innovation, referencing OpenAI’s concept of “capability overhang.” To mitigate this, his team identified and empowered power users in every workshop, integrating them into dynamic communities that could rapidly test, contextualize, and share new AI capabilities. This approach allowed the organization to bridge the gap between static training content and the evolving AI landscape.

In the Q&A, Bragan addresses the complexities of aligning incentives and securing organizational buy-in, particularly in risk-averse, public-sector environments. He describes a deliberate strategy of stakeholder mapping, pilot programs, and leveraging executive assistants to build both top-down and bottom-up support. He also discusses the importance of an innovation structure that can identify, pilot, and scale high-ROI use cases, ensuring that grassroots enthusiasm translates into organizational value.

Bragan concludes by reiterating that the future of AI in construction belongs to organizations that can systematically build capability, foster curiosity, and empower their workforce to adopt AI safely and effectively. His framework offers a replicable model for other firms seeking to close the adoption gap and realize the transformative potential of AI in the built environment.

title: Panel Discussion on Agentic AI Across the AEC Value Chain
summary: This panel examined how agentic AI is reshaping architecture, engineering, and construction, covering adoption maturity, data quality, governance, and secure proprietary tools such as Sweco GPT and Arcadis GPT. Panelists discussed practical AI applications including flood simulations, tendering, and groundwater calculations, along with future developments such as large vision models, proprietary AI systems, and energy-efficient infrastructure. The discussion also addressed challenges like disconnected data, legacy systems, agent sprawl, energy use, and workforce impact, while emphasizing collaboration across firms, startups, academia, and software vendors.
transcript:
Sarah Port and Carla Valca moderated a panel discussion on the current state and future impact of agentic AI across the AEC value chain. Panelists included Dr. Sam Zoharan, senior technology director Kimba from Trimble, Dr. Simon from Arcadis, and a chief strategy officer from Sweco.

After brief introductions, Carla framed the discussion in four sections, beginning with the present state of AI and moving toward near-term and longer-term future scenarios.

The moderators first asked each panelist how long they had worked with AI and to rate their AI enthusiasm.

Dr. Sam Zoharan said his first serious interaction with AI dated back to 2016. At that time, he was exploring how generative AI could be used in design—not in the current LLM sense, but in the design process itself—and how machine learning could be applied in manufacturing. Coming from an AEC background, he worked on using machine learning to determine material types and 3D-printing machine settings so that printed parts would achieve the intended tensile strength and performance. On enthusiasm, he said he would not give a number, but that he dreams about AI sometimes and feels excited to be part of a technological wave comparable to the industrial revolution or the internet.

Kimba said he became deeply engaged with AI in 2019 through machine learning, after having followed the field earlier. Over the last three years, he had been closely involved in developing AI tools both internally and externally and had tried to act as an ambassador for their adoption. He rated his enthusiasm at 101.

Simon said he also started in 2016 while co-authoring one of his first research papers. He trained a simple neural network for prediction, initially thought he had reached 100% accuracy, and then discovered the training and test data had been mixed. He rated his enthusiasm at 100, though he added that some mornings it is 100 and then drops to single digits after reading his inbox. Overall, he described it as a growing rollercoaster.

The Sweco representative said his first real experience with AI was around Christmas 2022, just after ChatGPT launched, when his brother-in-law showed it to him over Christmas dinner and they used it to make Christmas rhymes. He rated his enthusiasm at 50. Professionally, he finds the technology exciting and fun to experiment with, but personally he is concerned about fake news, crime, fraud, and wider effects on humanity. He said he does not worry about his own role disappearing, but he does worry about the social consequences if a large share of white-collar work vanishes quickly, since work gives people meaning, community, and structure.

The panel then moved into the present state of AI adoption in AEC.

Dr. Sam Zoharan described a maturity pyramid from the book Disrupted that maps how AEC firms are reacting to AI. At the bottom are firms that see AI as hype and remain reactive. Above them are “tinkerers,” companies experimenting with many tools and chasing shiny objects. He referenced a conversation about there being roughly 1,700 AI tools and noted how overwhelming it would be for employees to test even a fraction of them. Above that are “adopters,” companies that begin with an inside-out strategy: they assess their own business, clients, and ecosystem, then identify solutions that meet those needs. At the top are “strategic innovators,” which he said are still rare. These companies are not just looking for AI tools; they are rethinking how value is created and delivered, including workflows that are no longer centered entirely on humans but on what is now technically possible. He summarized the sector as spread across the full spectrum, from minimal response to strategic innovation.

Kimba then asked the audience for a show of hands of those using AI almost daily in their work. Nearly everyone raised a hand. He observed that two years earlier, even among early adopters, only a few hands would have gone up. While this audience was a particularly engaged group, he said it still showed that people are already experimenting and seeing daily benefits. At the same time, he stressed that the industry is only at the beginning of what is possible.

The discussion turned to where transformation in the value chain should begin. Carla asked whether the push must come from owners and developers, consultancies, software companies, or elsewhere.

Simon said the earlier pyramid described organizational maturity, but the industry can also be viewed as a workflow made up of requirements, concept, design, and building. What he sees today is isolated pilots running in each of those phases. Because the overall delivery system is still highly complex, he agreed that full workforce replacement is not near. The industry is still running fragmented pilots across the chain.

Asked who needs to “push the button” to make the leap happen, Simon argued that the industry should stop making itself smaller. AEC often describes itself as traditional rather than tech-first, but it needs to rethink how it operates and perhaps how it has been organized for decades. That is where differentiation lies: in connecting the dots and bringing people together across the fragmented system. He said there are champions across all functions, not just in enabling functions, but also in architecture, engineering, and project management.

The Sweco representative said Sweco decided early that the technology needed to be made available to everyone. In May 2023, the company launched a vendor-independent internal tool called SwecoGPT and then focused heavily on adoption. At the time of the panel, they had reached 70–80% regular usage if other tools were included. They were seeing a great deal of experimentation in the business, with people exploring how to change the way they work. He gave examples such as changes to groundwater calculations in Sweden and to tendering, though he said much of it was still at pilot stage. For Sweco, the next phase is to scale these efforts up and transform full disciplines and workflows. On the client side, he said there is strong interest. Clients want to know how AI is being used and how it benefits them, and some ask Sweco to help them with their own AI journeys. Sweco also sells some AI products, though he said the company is not “there yet.” Considerable progress has been made in three years, but more remains to be done.

Asked whether clients are using AI as a reason to demand lower prices, he said the more common issue is internal: consultants who are used to selling hours may save 10 hours with AI but still charge as if they had sold those hours. He said the industry has a deeply rooted habit of judging value by hours, and that behavior needs to change.

Simon added that the issue resembles a gear system. If one gear turns much faster than the others, the system breaks. If AI makes proposal writing much faster, but design teams, construction, or fabrication are not ready, then all that has happened is that the front end has accelerated while the rest of the pipeline remains constrained. The whole ecosystem must move in coordination.

The panel then addressed how organizations should handle internal enthusiasm for AI when resources are limited and many use cases must be rejected.

Simon said this is a major issue, but one that can be addressed by putting a process in place. Organizations need a way to collect use cases from employees, an initial review by someone who understands the relevant business line, and then a technical feasibility assessment. The good news today is that technical teams can implement pilot versions much faster than in the past, which also means ideas can fail faster. He recommended creating an innovation pipeline with clear criteria and short timelines. If no results appear within four weeks, he said, they likely will not appear in three months.

Kimba was asked how product teams are prioritizing amid the acceleration in AI development. He said product management has also sped up because teams can now create market requirements and product requirements documents much faster. The process of turning customer needs into something engineering can work with has been accelerated. He said it is now possible to move from zero to launching products in less than a year, which would have been unheard of for complex software only a few years ago. He also described a move away from many fragmented AI interfaces toward a more unified platform, with stronger plumbing underneath so individual experts can contribute domain-specific capabilities and create agents that help customers. At some point, he said, companies have to stop building seven different chat interfaces and unify them.

Still, he noted that every morning brings new developments. Depending on which “rabbit hole” you are in, five or ten significant new ideas can emerge over a weekend. That makes it extremely hard to keep up, especially given the gap between the pace of innovation and the pace of training.

An audience member then asked about data security, especially in Europe and in construction, where project data can be sensitive and even strategically important. The question focused specifically on where data is stored and how tools like SwecoGPT are secured.

The Sweco representative said he was not the technical expert on implementation, but his understanding was that no data is sent to OpenAI, Anthropic, or similar providers. Sweco hosts the solution itself in a contained environment. That concern about losing data was one of the main reasons Sweco built the tool in the first place.

Simon added that Arcadis had built a similar tool, Arcadis GPT, and had seen very similar adoption numbers. Arcadis uses a cloud governance model and an Azure-first stack. Proprietary LLM instances are hosted there, and the frontend connects only to those controlled environments. Around that is a broader governance layer that ensures no external access or data leakage. Access requires company devices and security controls. For highly sensitive work, including defense and security projects, Arcadis can create restricted instances for specific teams or projects so that even project data stays within project-specific boundaries. He said Arcadis launched its internal GPT in June 2023 and that the process of building it taught the organization, together with privacy and governance teams, how to structure its broader AI journey. Within three months, the company had developed its first AI policy, using the internal GPT deployment as the baseline.

Kimba added that this is less a matter of construction-specific policy and more a matter of general AI governance and company-level policies. In practice, he said, the setup is similar to other cloud solutions and common data environments, except using private instances in Microsoft, Google Cloud, or AWS, potentially located in a specific country or region depending on customer requirements.

The panel then shifted to 2026 and asked what needs to happen to make the market ready for a bigger leap.

The Sweco representative said one major change will be how business value is defined. If a company can do the same work in 10–20% of the time, then value can no longer be based in the same way on hours sold. AI should be thought of as a force multiplier that enables people to do much more. He argued that there are many tasks that are not done today simply because of time constraints, so AI creates an opportunity to improve quality, make designs more constructible, and allow firms to take on more work. He also pointed out that there are already innovators in the market who are not waiting for software vendors and are building their own capabilities.

He said the market is showing early signs in small chunks, but the bigger opportunities are still ahead.

Asked whether the market is truly ready, he said organizations still need to focus on adoption and learning. At the same time, it is time to start looking at core processes and how to transform them. One of Sweco’s assumptions is that they do not know exactly where innovation will happen: within their own business, with clients, among software providers, in foundational models, or through startups. For that reason, he said organizations need to remain very open, technology-agnostic, and willing to try everything.

Dr. Sam Zoharan agreed and pointed out that the audience itself included people from 36 countries, which already shows the industry is trying to understand what to do. He also noted that clients themselves are starting to use AI. If AEC firms do not adopt it, clients may use AI to evaluate the quality and consistency of their output. That creates a new pressure on firms to improve the quality of documentation, drawings, and designs so that they align with client expectations and standards. He cited HNTB’s research with DOTs in the United States as an example of clients trying to understand what AI means for them, and said this kind of pressure will expand across the ecosystem.

Carla then raised the issue of data. AI is often labeled onto everything, but what makes it different as a technology is its ability to automate and scale. Yet all of that depends on data—both documented data and tacit, undocumented knowledge. She asked what data in the AEC value chain is actually the most valuable.

Dr. Sam Zoharan said the answer depends on the opportunity. He used a grocery analogy: if you are cooking a particular meal, you do not buy the whole store. Likewise, companies should not try to “boil the ocean” by centralizing everything into a giant data lake if the use case does not require it. With AI agents, complete centralization may not be necessary. Instead, firms should identify the opportunities they want to tackle and determine what data those opportunities require. The valuable data is context-dependent.

Simon agreed that high-quality data is critical and noted that many stakeholders say, “Do the magic, all the data is there,” but when asked where it is, the answer is often simply “in SharePoint,” meaning an unstructured data dump. He warned against oversimplifying the challenge. For large organizations, getting data ready requires real priority, strategy, dedicated teams, and long-term investment. Large language models are trained on past data; they do not know a company’s business model, clients, or workforce. Those gaps have to be addressed intentionally.

Kimba offered several concrete examples of valuable data. One is data that machines can understand precisely, because robots, fabrication systems, and field automation require exact, unambiguous inputs. Another is simulation-ready data, a point also emphasized by Nvidia, meaning data rich enough to support many kinds of simulations. A third major area is maintenance, because that is where the majority of lifecycle cost in buildings lies; design is a relatively small portion, construction a larger one, but maintenance is the largest by far.

The final major section of the discussion focused on 2030 and beyond.

The Sweco representative separated the issue into “what” and “how.” On the “what,” he said many of the hours sold today will become worthless in the future, just as firms no longer sell the kind of paper-based drafting services they once did. Those lost billable hours must be replaced by something else. The easy answer would be to assume demand will simply expand enough to absorb the productivity gain, but he said that is unlikely. Firms will need to innovate and create new services, many of them digital and AI-powered.

On the “how,” he said the value chain historically used to be highly sequential: architects handed work to structural engineers, who handed work to MEP, and so on. Over time, those stages have already become more iterative, especially in complex industrial and sustainable projects. If each step becomes much more efficient through AI, then the entire chain may compress further, with more cross-functional teams working together in the same room, alongside the client, more like agile software development.

Simon agreed that delivery models are likely to shift away from selling hours and perhaps toward something closer to selling compute or API-based capability. He said organizations are also seeing more people across the board develop foundational understanding of AI. Two years earlier, there might have been only a handful of AI champions low in the organization; now, perhaps one in ten people has a strong basic understanding of the technology. He also took it as a positive sign that governance is already becoming a serious topic, because organizations would not focus on governance if they did not see real value in the technology. By 2030, he hopes organizations will have their own proprietary models and some degree of AI sovereignty. He sees a transition from humans being “in the loop” to humans orchestrating technology, and then selling that technology-enabled capability to clients. He said he does not fear that humans will disappear from the process.

Kimba said software development is already changing substantially. Talented developers are now spending much more of their time on architecture and code review while agents perform the coding. He called this a fact, though he noted that legacy software remains more difficult. He expects a similar shift in AEC, where construction workers and designers may effectively have seven or ten PhD-level assistants carrying out work under their direction. Any company that seizes that opportunity could potentially become ten times more productive. He added that AI today is likely the worst it will ever be; from here it will improve.

Simon pushed back slightly on the idea that LLMs alone will achieve full PhD-level general intelligence by 2030. He noted that models already outperform nearly all humans on certain narrow tasks, such as high-level mathematics Olympiad problems, but they can also behave like toddlers in ordinary interactions and make obviously poor decisions. That matters because in agentic systems, many autonomous decisions must be made, and even a few poor ones can undermine the entire output. He said he is not yet convinced that simply adding more data and more compute to current architectures will produce true continuous learning, creativity, and robust PhD-level reasoning across the board.

Dr. Sam Zoharan said he does not claim to predict the future, but he does see several trends by 2030. First, more AEC firms of all sizes will hire AI experts to work alongside domain experts. He pointed to examples from the day’s presentations in which firms had recently brought in dedicated AI talent. Second, knowledge itself is being democratized. Clients may gain access to levels of technical expertise that were once the exclusive domain of consultants. What will remain differentiating is perspective: the unique interpretation and judgment that professionals bring, beyond what a GPT can provide. That perspective may itself become a service. Third, he expects the industry to move beyond LLMs into other types of models, such as large vision models and eventually large domain-specific models. He suggested that by 2030 the industry may have an early but meaningful “large building model” that connects more of the system together. Finally, he warned about “agent sprawl.” Just as many firms currently struggle with data sprawl, the increasing ease of building agents could lead to proliferation of poorly managed agents. Organizations will need to address where agents live, who owns them, whether they remain accurate, and how they are governed.

Carla added another issue she felt had not been sufficiently discussed: infrastructure. If the future involves many AI assistants embedded across organizations, there is also the question of whether there will be enough energy and compute capacity.

Kimba replied that he is not overly worried about energy availability itself, though access and infrastructure matter. He also pointed out that Chinese developers, constrained by a lack of access to leading GPUs, have produced extremely efficient models optimized for older hardware. That research is published openly, and safe versions of those ideas can be replicated elsewhere. He expects continued gains in efficiency, lower compute requirements, and AI-assisted research that improves models further.

As the conversation drew toward collaboration, Carla referenced a prediction from Sequoia Capital that the next trillion-dollar company will be a software company masquerading as a service company. She suggested the reverse might also happen: service companies increasingly developing their own software. She asked whether the ecosystem—startups, academia, service firms, and major software vendors like Trimble—can build this future together, or whether competition will dominate.

The Sweco representative said he takes a collaborative stance. Customers are already multi-vendor and struggle with tool sprawl and data sprawl. Agentic AI could help bridge those gaps and make it easier for customers to work with their data. He said much depends on where one believes innovation will happen. If agents require many systems to interoperate, then engineering companies may be well positioned to drive value. If innovation is captured fully inside the design software itself, then engineering companies may have less leverage. His view is that it will require a mix of actors and happen on multiple levels.

Simon said that personally he is collaborative, but he is less convinced that the business world, shaped by profitability and competition, will behave that way in practice.

An audience member then asked whether compute should explicitly become part of the P&L and operating discussion at board level, given that software costs may rise significantly because of AI.

Simon said the tech function has historically often been seen as a cost center, but that mindset is slowly shifting. Cloud consumption is increasing month by month, and this is already visible in financial discussions. At the same time, AI growth creates new business opportunities—for example, in designing data centers and the infrastructure that supports them. He argued that organizations need to stay broad and agile in how they look for those opportunities.

The Sweco representative said software cost is already part of the P&L in practice, and AI is becoming a more strategic topic because digital is increasingly embedded in delivery. The key question should not be how to minimize the cost, but how to create return from it—how to get a 10x improvement out of employees.

An audience member challenged this framing by asking whether the industry might look back in 2030 and conclude that it simply hired a great many AI engineers, increased operating costs, and justified it by increasing revenue, without real proportional returns.

The Sweco representative responded that, at least so far, token spending has not become a major concern internally because the absolute cost remains relatively limited, and even if it were separated out as a line item, it would still be viewed as an investment worth making. He said there is no real alternative.

Dr. Sam Zoharan argued that return on investment should not be judged only in dollars. It should also be measured in quality and speed. If AI helps teams produce better work faster, that can justify the cost. He also noted that client expectations are changing. If clients can generate a rendering of a building in two minutes, even if it is not architecturally sound, they may still expect much faster response times than before. He added that job displacement is a real concern and one that governments will have to address. Some jobs may have been transitional roles that technology has now caught up with, much like elevator operators disappeared once elevator systems became automated. In some cases, companies may choose not to add headcount, and some of the money that would have gone to salaries may instead go toward compute and token costs, creating a new balance.

Simon offered a small analogy from one of his software development teams using GitHub Copilot. After three months, he asked them whether they would rather keep the Copilot licenses or receive a free lunch every week. They chose the license. To him, that was a strong signal that they were getting real value from the tool.

Kimba concluded that token spending is simply the price of entry now. Every company must do it. In software, any company not using AI today is unlikely to remain competitive for long unless it has an extraordinary moat. He said software was perhaps ironically the first domain where AI seemed to threaten workers directly, but what has actually emerged is the need for very good software engineers who can work effectively with AI and avoid low-quality output.

In closing, Carla thanked the audience for contributing questions on accountability, competitive advantage, governance, and more. She emphasized that the formal panel was ending but that the discussion should continue through the rest of the event.
detailed_summary:
The panel discussion, moderated by Sarah Port and Carla Valca, explored the transformative potential and current realities of agentic AI across the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) value chain. The central thesis, articulated through the panelists’ diverse perspectives, was that agentic AI is poised to fundamentally reshape the AEC industry—not merely by automating tasks, but by redefining value creation, workflows, and the very structure of organizations. The panelists—Dr. Sam Zoharan (senior technology director, Trimble), Kimba (Trimble), Dr. Simon (Arcadis), and a chief strategy officer from Sweco—brought a mix of technical, strategic, and operational insights to the discussion.

The conversation began with reflections on each panelist’s journey with AI. Dr. Zoharan traced his involvement to 2016, focusing on generative AI in design and machine learning for optimizing 3D printing in manufacturing. Kimba described his deep engagement since 2019, acting as an internal and external advocate for AI adoption at Trimble. Simon recounted his early research with neural networks in 2016, highlighting the learning curve and the volatility of enthusiasm in the face of rapid change. The Sweco representative, whose first real exposure to AI came with the launch of ChatGPT in late 2022, expressed both excitement and caution—enthusiastic about experimentation but wary of societal impacts such as job displacement and misinformation.

Dr. Zoharan introduced a maturity pyramid, adapted from the book *Disrupted*, to categorize AEC firms’ responses to AI: from reactive skeptics, to tinkerers overwhelmed by the proliferation of tools (noting over 1,700 AI tools in the market), to adopters with inside-out strategies, and finally to rare strategic innovators reimagining workflows beyond human-centric models. The panel agreed that the industry currently spans this entire spectrum, with most firms still in the early or experimental stages.

Kimba observed a dramatic shift in AI adoption, noting that nearly all audience members now use AI daily—a stark contrast to just two years prior. However, he cautioned that the industry is only scratching the surface of AI’s potential. Simon reframed the discussion around the AEC workflow—requirements, concept, design, build—emphasizing that AI pilots remain fragmented across these phases, and that systemic transformation requires connecting these isolated efforts.

Sweco’s approach exemplified a proactive, democratized model: the company launched SwecoGPT, a vendor-independent internal AI tool, achieving 70–80% regular usage when including other tools. Sweco’s next challenge is scaling from pilots to full workflow transformation, with strong client interest driving further adoption. The representative highlighted a persistent challenge: the industry’s entrenched habit of equating value with billable hours, even as AI delivers significant time savings.

Simon likened the transformation to a gear system: accelerating one part of the value chain (e.g., proposal writing) without synchronizing downstream processes risks systemic inefficiency. He advocated for coordinated ecosystem-wide change. On managing internal enthusiasm amid limited resources, Simon recommended structured innovation pipelines with rapid prototyping and clear go/no-go criteria, while Kimba described how product management cycles have accelerated, enabling faster translation of customer needs into deployable solutions.

Data security and governance emerged as critical themes, especially in Europe’s regulatory context.
