Richman emphasized that the current model uses minimal input—parcel and building geometry—but future iterations will incorporate additional layers such as topography and zoning data, leveraging Perceiver IO’s multimodal capabilities.

In response to audience questions, Richman acknowledged the challenges of extending the approach to multi-object layouts and incorporating more complex constraints like zoning and geotechnical data. He noted that sequential placement and richer input modalities are feasible directions, supported by the scalability of both the model architecture and the underlying dataset.

In conclusion, Richman advocated for a broader view of AI in design, positioning predictive design as a means to augment, rather than replace, human expertise. By providing designers with actionable, data-driven insights while preserving their agency, predictive design offers a promising path forward for early-stage architectural and site planning optimization.

title: AI-Optimized School Layouts with Adjacency Clustering and Evolutionary Algorithms
summary: Seeja Sudhakaran showcased a conceptual school design prototype aimed at institutional architecture, an area she identified as underserved by current AI tools. Her system combines adjacency matrix clustering with evolutionary algorithms to optimize layouts, while supporting BIM integration for conceptual design workflows. The prototype allows users to define typologies and spatial adjacency requirements early in the design process.
transcript:
Cia Sukkaran: Hi, I'm Cia. As mentioned, I am a doctoral researcher at the University of West London, and I'm also a partner and principal at Studio Emergence. I've been an architect for more than a decade, and for the last ten years I've been very industry focused. More recently I also started my research journey.

This presentation is a mix of industry and research. It talks about my experiences as an architect, what I have learned over the last decade, and my computational design expertise. I started working in computational design in 2014 during my master's, and ever since then, both in Studio Emergence and in research, we've worked extensively with evolutionary algorithms, machine learning, and more recently large language models. It's been quite a journey going from a very design-centric architectural background to something more computationally driven.

The presentation has two parts. In the first, I'll talk about applications that architects and designers already use in everyday practice and how they are reshaping design agency overall. In the second, I'll discuss applications we've developed within the research unit and at Studio Emergence.

For the first part, we wanted to understand what AI applications are already commercialized and being used in the industry by architects and designers across the project life cycle. Once we had a consolidated list of 50 applications, we ran analyses to understand where those applications are situated in the overall workflow.

The 50 selected applications were categorized according to project life-cycle stages. Initially we reviewed around 400 tools, narrowed that to 100, and eventually selected the 50 most used. Even at a glance, it was clear that the distribution is lopsided across stages. We used the RIBA Plan of Work, since that was the landscape we were working in. Stage 1 is briefing, followed by concept design, spatial coordination, technical design, and then actual construction at stage 5.

We found that stages 2 and 5 have many applications, while the others are comparatively underserved.

When we started evaluating these tools, we needed a way to compare them despite their differences. As an architect myself, I also wanted to understand what they were doing to architects and to design agency as a whole.

We came up with two aspects for evaluation. The first is AI logic, or AL, which captures the underlying artificial intelligence logic of the application. It asks: how intelligent and self-directed is the tool? Does it optimize complex problems? Does it learn from them? Or is it basically a standard application that simply calls itself AI?

The second is architectural values, or AV. This was especially important to me personally. It captures whether the tool preserves architects' control and creativity. Does it allow meaningful human interaction? How black-boxed is the application? Does it simply produce options over which the architect has little control, or can the architect actively guide and shape the output?

We see design agency as the intersection of these two dimensions. Design agency is evolving with the advent of AI. The architect or designer using AI is situated at that intersection between AI logic and architectural values, and we were trying to understand how design agency shifts as these applications are used.

We ran a pilot study in which 10 experts rated a subset of the applications. We couldn't ask them to rate all 50 because that would have been too arduous, so each expert rated 5 AI applications across a number of values: three AI-logic values and three AV values. We also asked them to rate each application within our scoring framework.

At the low end of AI logic, the tool is merely assistive. At the low end of AV, the outputs are narrowly technical and not focused on the designer. At the high end, the tool automates complex problems while explicitly supporting design agency.

There was substantial variation in how individual experts ranked specific tools, but when we analyzed the averages and the ICC coefficient, the results were surprisingly consistent. Based on this, we assigned scores to the 50 applications.

Once we mapped the applications, interesting patterns emerged. On a chart with AI logic on the x-axis and architectural values on the y-axis, with colors indicating project life-cycle stages, we could see that many stage 5 construction tools clustered in the lower-right quadrant: high AI logic but somewhat lower architectural values. Those were the applications serving manufacturing and construction.

By contrast, the upper-left quadrant contained more stage 1 and conceptual design tools: higher architectural values and lower AI logic.

This mapping helped us understand how different stages of the project life cycle are being served by different tools and how these tools are often operating in parallel rather than in an integrated way.

One conclusion was that design agency is not necessarily being replaced so much as redistributed. At stage 1, AI logic and architectural values are often more balanced. As we move toward stage 5, architectural values tend to decline while AI logic increases.

We also looked at secondary parameters, such as user type. Architects are generally situated in stages 1 and 2, while contractors are more often positioned in stage 5. There is not much overlap between those two groups.

We also examined geographic context. Many tools claim to be global, but when you look more closely, they are largely focused either on Europe or North America. A lot of global variation is ignored, especially in areas like building regulations.

Tool development follows similar patterns in terms of what is commercially available and what types of design logic are being used.

One major finding concerned building typology. Many of these applications are geared toward residential, mixed-use, and commercial work. Much less is aimed at institutional architecture. From my own architectural experience, I understand why: institutions involve huge hierarchical complexities and adjacency issues, especially in more complex spaces.

To summarize the findings: design agency is being redistributed; the current technological landscape is biased, with a gap in applications that score highly on both AI logic and architectural values; institutions are underserved; and sustainability tools are also underserved. There are a few robust tools, but not enough of them.

From that, we asked what gap we could address in the research unit. One obvious direction was institutional architecture, specifically school building design. There are not many applications that do this. Another reason to focus on schools is that in the UK there is already a lot of information available. The Department for Education provides schedules of accommodation, so if you know the number of pupils in a school, you can derive area requirements relatively easily. That makes the program easier to define.

There are also exemplary design options already published, which we could use as a dataset to compare our outputs. In institutional architecture, data availability is often a challenge, especially if you want to compare circulation patterns and internal organization.

We started with something simple: the adjacency matrix. We wanted to understand what the adjacency matrix for a complicated school program looks like. Even for one of the smaller schools, the matrix was substantial. On larger schools, the matrix had more than 100 rows, even over 150. It quickly became a conundrum to understand how to computationally solve all of those adjacencies, because schools have many rooms, strict spatial hierarchies, and many rule-based relationships.

That was our major challenge. One way to address it was to clusterize the program. We grouped spaces into clusters and treated those clusters, or building arms, as the units that come together. Then the computational rules and evolutionary algorithms work on those clusters rather than on every single room individually.

This approach worked quite successfully. For a primary school of about 100 pupils, we could reduce the problem to around 20 clustered spaces, and then later subdivide those internally.

For the prototype development framework, we created two phases. Phase 1 focused on capturing information: extracting numerical data from the schedule of accommodation; coding planning rules, regulations, and building regulations for the specific site context; and using the site boundary as an input to define the design envelope.

Phase 2 used evolutionary algorithms. We selected evolutionary algorithms specifically because they provide Pareto-optimal solutions. When we tried other methods, the problem often became too complex, especially given the number and volume of spaces involved.

A key concern for me throughout this process was the role of the designer. As a computational architect using many existing applications, one of the biggest problems I encountered was not having enough authority in selecting what I was making. Many applications simply produce outputs that I cannot meaningfully pick and choose from.

So throughout our workflow, we inserted opportunities for user input. At different steps, the user can select certain options because, as designers, we all approach projects with intentions and preferences.

For example, the user can select plan-exploration typologies. They can also set input parameters such as site dimensions, road orientation, overall site orientation, and setbacks. These can also support later energy analysis and simulations. We also input building regulation requirements like height limits and permissible FAR.

Once all that information is assembled, we can run case studies. In the example presented, we had a small site, regulatory setbacks, and a school program derived from the schedule of accommodation.

The system generated multiple layout options based on rule-based optimization. We had rules that rank the plans generated in different ways, and then based on the ranking, the user can select what works and what doesn't.

One example was an optimized C-shaped plan for the school. We also developed an L-shaped planning option for the same site, with the same setbacks and program, again generating multiple plan iterations and optimized layouts.

We then compared the resulting school layouts against Department for Education layouts published by the government. The results were reasonably successful. The roadside entry, shown in blue, corresponded well with expected organization. Administrative spaces were around it, then halls and play areas near the entry, followed by learning spaces located further away.

We have also been working in a BIM environment so that the whole process can take place within software like Revit. Within that environment, users can generate walls, select different wall typologies, choose window types, and tailor construction assemblies according to local context. Since I've worked in different geographic regions, I know that wall typologies and other architectural details vary significantly from place to place. Architecture is extremely tangible and highly location-specific, so we kept the system open-ended enough for users to choose what they need.

Thank you.

Audience question: In school design, the designer often already knows where certain major elements should go, such as the gymnasium. Can the designer intervene and say that something has to be in a certain place, and then let the system arrange everything else around it?

Cia Sukkaran: Yes, we've kept it quite open-ended. Obstacles can be defined, and you can reserve part of the layout for a specific use. That area remains fixed based on the designer's decision, and everything else can be generated around it. So there is definitely a possibility to reserve a space for a particular thing and then generate the outputs around it.

Audience question: Our school designs usually involve many different angles and shapes. Can the system accommodate more architectural variation, or is it mainly about spatial connections at this point?

Cia Sukkaran: At this point it's more about spatial connections, but we're developing it further. Different site shapes are already incorporated. The next step is to allow more varied spatial organizations as well. For example, a user could sketch a shape and then the entire building could be organized around that shape. The whole system is already sketch-based, so the line typologies we used, such as linear and C-shaped, are built from that logic. In principle, guidance geometry from a user sketch can be used in future development.

Audience question: In Finland we have different rules for different cities. Is there similar variation in England?

Cia Sukkaran: Yes, absolutely, and that's part of the reason institutions are harder to address. Residential projects are still more consistent, but institutional rules vary a lot. What we've done is keep the first part of the workflow quite open-ended, so the user can input a lot of the requirements. Then the outputs can be brought closer to the building regulations of the specific region.

Audience question: If the system generates hundreds of layouts in a minute, is there a risk of information overload? And how do you optimize selection while preserving human creativity?

Cia Sukkaran: One good thing is that the outputs are rank-based. If you don't want overload, you can just look at the top-ranked options. If you don't like what you're seeing, you can shift to others. So the user doesn't need to review everything.

As for human creativity, it is still very much there because this is targeted at conceptual design. In practice, due to feasibility issues, budgets, and time constraints, iterative design and optioneering are often not done to the extent the architect would want. This becomes part of the toolkit: it presents options, but it doesn't force a selection. It gives you a wide range of optioneering that you can then apply your own creativity to and develop further. It is not the final design. It provides the breadth of exploration that is needed in conceptual design.

Cia Sukkaran: We already do bubble diagrams and conceptual zoning to understand volumetric relationships. This tool helps with that initial stage of case studies and site understanding. Work that might otherwise take a week can be done in a day, perhaps even faster, and that can significantly reduce the time needed for early presentations. It could also work for other institutional building types, such as hospitals.
detailed_summary:
Cia Sukkaran’s presentation advanced the thesis that artificial intelligence—specifically through adjacency clustering and evolutionary algorithms—can meaningfully optimize the layout of complex institutional buildings such as schools, while preserving and even enhancing the agency of architects in the design process. Drawing from her dual perspective as both a practicing architect and a computational design researcher at the University of West London and Studio Emergence, Sukkaran explored the current landscape of AI tools in architecture, identified critical gaps, and detailed the development and evaluation of a novel AI-driven school layout system.

Sukkaran began by contextualizing her work within the broader adoption of AI in architectural practice. Her team conducted a comprehensive survey of 400 AI-enabled tools, narrowing the field to the 50 most widely used applications across the architectural project life cycle, as defined by the RIBA Plan of Work. Their analysis revealed a pronounced concentration of AI tools in the concept design (stage 2) and construction (stage 5) phases, with other stages comparatively underserved. To rigorously evaluate these tools, Sukkaran introduced a dual-axis framework: AI logic (AL), measuring the sophistication and autonomy of the AI, and architectural values (AV), assessing the extent to which tools preserve designer control and creativity. Through a pilot study involving expert ratings, they mapped applications onto this framework, revealing that construction-stage tools tend to maximize AI logic at the expense of architectural values, while conceptual design tools do the opposite. This mapping illuminated a key insight: AI is not replacing design agency, but redistributing it—often in ways that fragment the workflow and diminish the architect’s influence in later project stages.

A further finding was the market’s bias toward residential and commercial typologies, with institutional buildings—such as schools—largely neglected due to their complex spatial hierarchies and adjacency requirements. Recognizing this gap, Sukkaran’s research unit targeted school design as a domain ripe for AI-driven innovation, leveraging the UK Department for Education’s detailed schedules of accommodation and published exemplar layouts as both data sources and benchmarks.

The core technical challenge lay in managing the vast adjacency matrices inherent to school programs, which can involve over 150 unique spaces and intricate rule-based relationships. Sukkaran’s solution was to employ adjacency clustering: grouping related spaces into clusters (e.g., building arms) and applying computational rules and evolutionary algorithms at the cluster level, rather than to every individual room. This approach dramatically reduced problem complexity—for instance, a 100-pupil primary school could be abstracted to 20 clusters, which were later subdivided as needed.

The prototype system was developed in two phases. Phase one focused on data extraction and rule encoding: parsing numerical requirements from accommodation schedules, integrating planning and building regulations, and defining the site envelope. Phase two leveraged evolutionary algorithms to generate Pareto-optimal layout solutions, chosen for their ability to efficiently navigate the combinatorial explosion of possible configurations. Crucially, the workflow was designed to maximize designer agency: users could input site parameters, select plan typologies (e.g., C-shaped, L-shaped), set regulatory constraints, and intervene at multiple decision points. The system generated and ranked multiple layout options, allowing architects to select, refine, or override outputs according to their intentions.

Sukkaran demonstrated the system with case studies, including the generation of optimized C- and L-shaped school layouts for a constrained site. These outputs were benchmarked against official Department for Education layouts, showing strong correspondence in spatial organization—such as administrative areas near entries and learning spaces set further back. The tool was implemented within a BIM environment (e.g., Autodesk Revit), enabling users to specify local construction assemblies, wall typologies, and window types, thus accommodating geographic and regulatory variation.

In response to audience questions, Sukkaran emphasized the system’s flexibility: designers can reserve specific areas for fixed uses, accommodate varied site geometries, and input region-specific regulations. While current iterations focus on spatial connections, future development aims to support more complex user-defined geometries and sketch-based guidance. The system’s rank-based output mitigates information overload and preserves creativity by supporting rapid optioneering without dictating final designs.

Sukkaran concluded that AI-driven adjacency clustering and evolutionary algorithms can significantly accelerate early-stage institutional design, reducing weeks of manual work to days or hours, while empowering architects to maintain creative control. She suggested that the methodology could extend to other complex building types, such as hospitals, and called for further development of AI tools that balance technical optimization with the preservation of architectural values.

title: AI Visualization from Mass Models for Early-Stage Housing Design
summary: Mauri Laasonen explored how AI visualization can turn simple mass models into useful imagery for early housing design discussions. He argued that even imperfect AI-generated visuals are valuable for facilitating communication among stakeholders during conceptual phases. He also recommended using disclaimers for AI-generated images to account for possible inaccuracies.
transcript:
Mauri Laxanen: Good morning, everybody. My name is Mauri Laxanen, and I work at Tampere University of Applied Sciences. My topic is the visualization of buildings in early stages.

The problem is familiar. In early-stage design, the building company gives some basic requirements and steering information. Then the architect goes back to the office, makes a fairly detailed model of the building, creates a visualization, and returns to present it. Then they discuss it, there are changes, the architect goes back to the office, makes a new model, and comes back after two weeks.

But when there is not yet a buyer or a committed customer, there are not many resources available, and this cycle cannot go through very many rounds.

So our question was: can we make those visuals as AI-generated images? Everybody knows you can make almost any kind of picture with AI, but the real question was whether those pictures are usable.

Our method was to select tools—ArchiAI and Stable Diffusion—and use versions that had been approved for working with real company data that could not be shared publicly. We held workshops with housing development professionals, our team, an architect, and researchers. Then we collected the pictures, evaluated them, and followed what kinds of discussions they generated.

The first key finding was that it is still very useful to make some kind of BIM model at the beginning, but it does not need to be detailed. A simple mass model is enough.

You get information from the building companies about how many blocks there may be and the roof program, what kind of houses there will be, and sometimes the authorities also specify how many buildings there are allowed to be. So you make a mass model.

Then you take a view from where you want the picture and create a screenshot from the model, and you keep this picture as a steering file for the AI. Then you get an image that reflects the actual project geometry and vertical organization. That was the first lesson.

The second lesson was that there are always mistakes. The images are not perfect, but we found that if we say clearly, “These pictures are made by AI, not by me,” then people excuse a lot. They understand the limitations and still accept the images as useful.

What happened in practice was that people started to discuss. That was the main finding. The picture does not need to be perfect to trigger meaningful and useful discussion.

Our conclusion was that we found a method to generate usable images for architects in early design stages. Are architects losing their jobs because of this? No. They are not, because there are mistakes in the pictures and we still need highly professional architects to say what is good, what cannot be done, and what needs to be changed.

But what can be reduced is routine modeling work. That was our target. We get less modeling and more design.

Let me show an example that led us to these conclusions. We started with a very simple model. On the left was the Archicad model. We loaded in the terrain and roads as they exist, and then placed a very simple mass model. We selected a view and got an image from it. It included the right terrain, the correct location, and even the right kind of forest in the background.

It was not a glorious picture. It was not something we would have presented as a polished final rendering, but it served as a visual starting point.

At the time we began, we did not have much skill with the process. We were starting from zero and wanted to save work, so we simply took a usable picture and saw what would happen.

We also had planning regulations from the authorities, and we gave that information to the system in text form. What was surprising was that almost every detail from the regulations could somehow be found in the generated picture.

One example involved a large bicycle shelter with a green roof. That requirement was in the text input. Everyone in the room initially accepted it in the abstract. We had removed original nature from the site, so compensating with greenery seemed normal and acceptable.

But as soon as we had the picture in front of us, people reacted differently. They said: not there, not that one. Suddenly people started to notice the actual implications. The building company began to think about the cross-section and realized there would be water lying on the roof. That would mean freezing and melting several times a year. The roof would need to last 50 years without rusting. They started asking how much natural material would be needed to build such a roof in the middle of the forest.

Then they realized that this kind of rule made more sense in dense city centers and not in that location. Everyone understood it immediately once it was visualized. They said the rule made no sense there.

The discussion continued. Someone said perhaps they should instead build the cheapest steel-profile roof, which would be acceptable but would not look very good. Then someone suggested that, because this is in the countryside, they could create a shelter more like those used for animals, with a more appropriate roof. And the design discussion continued from there.

They also began to discuss things like whether the red brick looked right and whether it was used enough. The image activated discussion that would not have happened in the same way from text alone.

At the same time, one person in the room barely said a word. He was working on his computer the whole time, listening to the discussion. After 10 or 15 minutes, he looked up and started guiding the architect. He made some adjustments, gestured to the screen, and then said: now that we have discussed this, can we go back to the roof? We have a new plan.

This person had the best tools and knew the workflow best. He was able to make a new picture during the meeting itself. They had a new version of the design in the same meeting—not after two weeks, not after three weeks, but in the same meeting—and they could continue the discussion immediately.

That is the real change.

You can make pictures without reference images, but then there are difficulties. For example, it is very hard for AI to understand how many stories a building has. In one case, a building that should have had more floors came out as only seven stories. A mass model helps. It does not solve the problem entirely, but it helps a lot.

Nowadays you can also use not just the screenshot but the three-dimensional DXF file as steering data, which improves the control further.

There are still other mistakes. In one example, there was a tram shown in the image. It looked like the tram we have in Tampere, and even the rails were present, but the tram was not in the middle where it should have been. That kind of error did not disturb me very much because I was concentrating on whether the building composition was correct, but we do not know how customers might react. They may think that if the designers do not even know how the trams run, can they really design good buildings?

Still, this is not a major problem. It can be corrected relatively easily.

Once you have this frame—a mass model and some initial control—then comes the next advantage of AI: you can go out of the box. You can generate new ideas and different kinds of things, while still keeping the overall project within a reasonable frame.

That is valuable, because when you are looking for ideas, the aim is often to go beyond the obvious. Some people want to jump straight outside the box, but if you do not first have a box, then you are just lost. In Finland we say you are out like a snowman. So we need the frame first, and then we can generate ideas.

When the constructors start talking and say things like, “This is good,” or “This may need a bigger column,” they are finding errors, yes, but you should pause and realize what they really mean: you have made a picture good enough that they can engage with it seriously. They like many things in it. They want this detail changed, and now that becomes the discussion.

The skill is in picking up from that discussion what is good, what is bad, and what should be the next version. Then the process moves very easily forward.

AI can also make glorious pictures, but the problem is that they are not always usable. AI likes certain things too much. If you want to make the picture look luxurious, it wants to add a pool. A bigger pool is better, and two pools are better than one. Those images are not very usable for real projects. AI also tends to like glass walls, so you have to say very clearly that you do not want that kind of wall.

All of the prompts and commands must be quite clear. At the beginning we tried to make the instructions very explicit, but over time we learned that the pictures are also useful because they reveal things you did not even have in your mind at the beginning. That means more design.

What we have not yet done is show these pictures directly to end customers or housing buyers. In this project we mainly worked internally. If there are problems, the images are easy to edit, but that was not the focus here.

Still, we saw examples where a rather ordinary original image from a client project could, with quite limited effort, be transformed into a much more usable and communicative picture.

So remember: it is AI. And in these visualizations, the sun is always shining. So be happy. Thank you.

Audience question: I often use AI tools to polish my renders, but AI generates a completely different image every time. Does that affect reliability, and how do you ensure consistency in early-stage residential design?

Mauri Laxanen: There is no real logic in the AI. You desperately try to find some pattern, but it is more like a game. You play the game and sometimes get a good result, but there is not a stable logic behind it. The next picture can be totally different from what you expected. So you generate several pictures, and then someone has to decide which one is acceptable. There can be useful things in many of the bad pictures too, but yes, consistency is limited.

Audience comment: Previously we used image search to find reference moods and images for customers. In that sense AI is not so different.

Mauri Laxanen: I support that view very much.

Audience question: Do you think AI-generated images are more American than European?

Mauri Laxanen: Yes, those pools come from there. We do not need so many pools. But you can solve that if you have your own training or steering data. In the literature, people collect style examples from different architects or regions and use them as steering material. If you adopt this method in regular practice, you should have a curated set of images that you feed in as guidance. Then you can get output in the style you want. So you can develop your own steering data and solve that problem.

Mauri Laxanen: Almost everyone is already using AI in some way. This is low-hanging fruit. I believe everyone here could make pictures like the ones we used. They do not need to be very high-end before they are already useful. They are useful because they start discussion. That is not the end of the process, but the beginning.

We do not yet know exactly where this will go. Now we use a mass model, and maybe later we can make slightly better models and still avoid full detailed modeling. It is quite possible that we can continue in this direction with less modeling and more design.
detailed_summary:
Mauri Laxanen’s presentation addressed the challenge of producing effective visualizations during the early stages of housing design, particularly when resources are limited and clients are not yet committed. Laxanen argued that AI-generated images, when guided by simple mass models and project constraints, can serve as powerful tools to facilitate design discussions and decision-making, even if the images themselves are imperfect. The core thesis was that the value of early-stage visualization lies not in photorealistic accuracy, but in the ability to spark meaningful dialogue among stakeholders, thereby accelerating and enriching the design process.

To explore this, Laxanen’s team at Tampere University of Applied Sciences conducted a series of workshops involving housing development professionals, architects, and researchers. They employed AI tools—specifically ArchiAI and Stable Diffusion—using versions approved for handling confidential company data. The workflow began with the creation of a basic BIM mass model in Archicad, incorporating essential site features such as terrain and roads, as well as regulatory constraints provided by authorities. Rather than developing detailed architectural models, the team generated simple massing models, took screenshots from selected viewpoints, and used these as steering images for the AI. Supplementary textual inputs included planning regulations and specific requirements, such as the number of buildings or features like green roofs.

The AI-generated images, while often containing errors or unexpected elements, proved highly effective in practice. Laxanen emphasized that explicitly labeling the images as AI-generated set appropriate expectations and encouraged stakeholders to focus on the underlying design issues rather than surface-level imperfections. The images consistently reflected key project parameters—such as geometry, vertical organization, and regulatory details—well enough to ground discussions in the realities of the site and brief.

A pivotal case study illustrated the method’s impact: a mass model screenshot, combined with textual requirements, produced an image featuring a bicycle shelter with a green roof, as mandated by local regulations. While the requirement had been accepted in abstract discussions, the visualization prompted immediate, critical feedback. Stakeholders questioned the practicality of the green roof in a rural context, considering issues like water retention, durability, and material sourcing. The image catalyzed a shift in thinking, leading to alternative proposals more suited to the site’s context. This rapid, iterative feedback loop—enabled by the ability to generate and revise images within a single meeting—marked a significant departure from the traditional, weeks-long cycle of model updates and presentations.

Laxanen noted that while AI-generated images are not flawless—citing examples such as misplaced trams or incorrect building heights—these shortcomings are manageable and do not undermine the primary goal of fostering productive discussion. The use of mass models or even 3D DXF files as steering data improves geometric fidelity, though some inconsistencies remain. He also observed that AI’s stylistic biases (e.g., favoring pools or glass walls) can be mitigated by curating steering data and prompts, or by developing a library of reference images tailored to regional or architectural preferences.

The presentation concluded that AI visualization is not a threat to architects’ roles; rather, it reduces routine modeling work and shifts focus toward higher-value design tasks. The process is inherently collaborative, with architects and stakeholders interpreting and iterating on AI outputs. Laxanen acknowledged that consistency across AI-generated images is limited, as the technology lacks deterministic logic, but this variability can be harnessed to explore a broader range of design ideas. He recommended that practitioners develop curated steering datasets to achieve desired stylistic outcomes and stressed that the primary benefit of AI visualization is its capacity to initiate and enrich design conversations.

Looking ahead, Laxanen suggested that as tools and workflows mature, the reliance on detailed early-stage modeling may diminish further, enabling even more agile and creative design processes. While the team has not yet tested these methods directly with end customers, the internal results demonstrate that even modest, AI-assisted visualizations can transform the pace and quality of early-stage housing design.

title: Circular Office Fit-Out Design Using Graph Models and Large Language Models
summary: Mikhael Johanes presented research on circular fit-outs for office interiors, combining graph-based representations of spatial configurations with large language models. The approach aims to reduce waste, lower turnover costs, and support modular adaptability in interior design. His results showed reduced drywall waste while preserving reusable modular elements for future reconfiguration.
transcript:
Mikhail Johannes: Thank you everyone for having me here. I would like to introduce myself. I'm Mikhail Johannes, a Design++ Bostoc Fellow from ETH Zurich.

For context, Design++ is an interdisciplinary research center at ETH Zurich that advances AI and XR in architecture, engineering, and construction, and also engages with industry. In this project we collaborate with Halter Group and its subsidiary Integral, which focus on the design-build of commercial interiors.

So this research is a collaboration between industry and a research center. I'll present work titled “AI-driven circular fit-outs,” which aims to enable adaptability in office interiors by leveraging AI and computational design.

Why should we care about office interiors? Work styles and needs are continuously changing, so office planners and interior planners have to adapt as well.

But these changes come at a cost. If we look at a typical office core-and-cell typology and the greenhouse-gas emissions over the lifetime of a building—say 50 to 100 years—we find that the waste coming from construction and the frequent refurbishments or renovations of the interior can, over time, exceed the embodied impacts of the building shell itself. This is especially true in large cities where tenant turnover is very quick and interiors may change every three to five years.

Why does this happen? The main problem we observed in the field is that the design paradigm often focuses only on immediate needs and requirements. That leads to a large amount of waste when we need to change from one design to another after five or eight years.

So we need to rethink design. We cannot only design for now; we have to see design as a sequence of possible future designs.

Our main goal is to develop a computational method that reduces this waste by planning ahead and considering multiple design options that can accommodate different requirements. In that way we may be able to preserve and reuse some elements when adapting to new needs.

We developed a method based on the idea that offices are already modular by nature, which makes them a relatively low-hanging fruit for this type of approach.

This allows us to think of fit-out design or interior design as a hierarchical combinatorial problem. As with school design, we can understand the space as zones, then space types, then elements inside those spaces. Many of those elements are modular by nature.

Conventionally, the natural way to solve such a problem is with combinatorial optimization. We try to optimize the space planning so that multiple design requirements can be fit into the same overall layout.

The problem is that conventional rule-based solvers are limited to what we can formalize as objective functions. As was already highlighted earlier, there is a lot that is difficult to formalize. This restricts the solver's ability to capture the richer design intent architects often have, and it can lead to outcomes that are feasible but not meaningful or intended.

Our contribution is to insert a language model into the optimization loop. The language model can reason about and learn aspects of the design context.

More specifically, we leverage the semantic reasoning capabilities of AI agents to enable alterations to the design as it is being produced, aligning the optimization more closely with design intention and guiding the search toward more meaningful outcomes.

For example, during the process, the solver may generate a layout. We then let the language model reason about whether that layout makes sense and whether it aligns with our design intentions. Based on that, it can suggest changes. So we do not use AI to solve the full problem directly; we use AI to guide the combinatorial search.

This results not only in more meaningful designs, but also in a more effective exploration of a large combinatorial space, ultimately improving the solutions we find.

In overview, this research combines combinatorial design with large language models to enable exploration of adaptable designs using modular components that can respond to different design requirements.

Let us look at the case study. Although the domain here is office design, I think by the end it should be clear that this approach could be generalized to other typologies, especially hospitals, schools, and industrial buildings that have more complex design topologies.

Imagine that we have an empty office interior in which multiple design scenarios must be accommodated. We start with a core-and-cell office typology and a planning grid.

Suppose we want to accommodate three design scenarios: a general-purpose office with balanced zoning; a meeting-heavy scenario where we need more meeting space; and a work-focused scenario where we need more desk-based work areas.

We represent these design requirements using a few variables. One is the distribution of the program ratio, which is straightforward and already familiar to designers as a proxy for whether a plan fits the requirements.

We also represent requirements through spatial relationships. For example, we may want service zones to be far away from work zones, and meeting zones to be as close as possible to work zones, while the lobby has its own desired relationship. These can be formalized in adjacency or distance matrices, which are relatively easy for the system to encode.

But there are other requirements that we cannot easily formalize. For example, we might want a certain sense of grouping, continuity, or connected spatial structure. This is where the language model plays a role by enabling semantic interpretation of spatial structure and program.

We introduce a third category of requirements that allows designers to specify intentions in natural language. The language model then interprets these queries and helps guide the combinatorial optimization process.

At the end of the optimization, the goal is to find partitioning schemes that minimize interventions when adapting from one design scenario to another. In other words, we want to identify which permanent elements can already be put in place so they do not need to be removed when the program changes in the future.

To do this, we use graph representations of the floor plan as the bridge between spatial configurations and programmatic assignments. This separation is the key to the method.

For the spatial configuration, we create a configurable floor-plan graph in which the positions of partitions can be specified according to the planning grid. As these partitions subdivide the interior, an adjacency graph is generated.

A second model then assigns programs to that graph. The programmatic zones are assigned using a hybrid approach that combines constraint programming with language-model inference. Using the graph representation and semantic reasoning, the system assigns zones to the floor plan graph based on the different requirements.

The formalizable requirements go to a conventional constraint solver, which can solve them reliably. The design context and design intentions are handled by the language model.

We define several objective functions to guide the optimization. One is how well the program distribution fits the rules we gave the system—for instance, whether certain zones are far apart or near each other as desired. There can be hundreds of such constraints.

Another objective concerns the morphology of the spaces—how complex or usable they are geometrically.

Altogether, the optimization combines configurational search and programmatic assignment to find the best configuration that can be adapted from one program to another.

In the results, the gray partitions represent elements we can preserve during adaptation, while the programmatic changes can be accommodated by removing and adding relatively few modular elements.

Our primary result shows that incorporating AI-agent intervention expands the Pareto front of the combinatorial search, allowing the system to discover a wider range of feasible but meaningful design solutions.

But the real question is whether this multi-scenario optimization approach can actually reduce impact and cost. So we compared this method to single-scenario optimization.

In the top row, we jointly optimized three design requirements at once. In the bottom row, we optimized the designs one by one, leading to different configurations.

We then calculated how much waste would be generated if one went through those designs in different permutations. The multi-scenario method reduced quite a bit of waste from permanent elements such as drywall. We also tried to be more holistic by including modular glass panels and modular solid panels in the calculation. Those impacts were also reduced to some extent.

We also saw an intuitive benefit: less work is needed to adapt the space, which decreases turnover cost and time during future project changes.

Ultimately, the goal is to enable circularity in fit-outs. We want to reduce the impact of frequently changing office needs by creating interiors that are more adaptable while preserving static elements strategically.

We are not only trying to do things faster, better, and more efficiently. We also want to make more strategic decisions about which elements to keep and which to invest in ahead of time.

Thank you. I would be happy to connect further for more in-depth discussion. I also invite you to learn more about Design++ at ETH Zurich. We have a very vibrant community there. If you happen to come to Switzerland, in May we will have another symposium on the future of construction.

Audience question: For an architect, it is difficult to consider so many disciplines and variables at once. This seems like it could help optimize more things than a human can optimize alone. Have you tested this in the real world yet, or has it only been in the lab?

Mikhail Johannes: Yes, that is exactly why we collaborate with a design-build company. They are providing real case studies—office projects of around 1,000 square meters that they want to present in proposals. The idea is to use this as a tool to create a kind of decision framework for the client, showing the investment they can make if they choose certain strategies.

Basically, we can identify which modular elements they should invest in. The problem is that you do not want modular elements everywhere because they are expensive. So this process is about finding the best strategic way to invest in those modular elements. It also becomes a way of demonstrating value to the client by showing what that investment means over a 10- or 20-year horizon.

This is something we are currently working on, and in the next few months you should start to see papers coming out from it.

Audience question: Do you think that in the future the EU might require this kind of process for building permits?

Mikhail Johannes: On the policy side, it is hard to say, but circularity is definitely becoming more enforced. For example, we increasingly need building logbooks at the EU level to start projects. When buildings are dismantled, there are also reporting requirements for disassembly. So I think more and more regulatory constraints will be enforced.

From a research perspective, that means more constraints that we need to integrate into our systems. The number of variables we have to consider keeps getting larger, and I do not think we as humans will be able to compare all of them manually. That is where I see the value of AI. Not just in making us faster or reducing hours, but in expanding our ability to comprehend complex decision variables and bring them to the table in a way that supports decision-making.
detailed_summary:
Mikhail Johannes, a Design++ Bostoc Fellow at ETH Zurich, presented a novel approach to office interior design that leverages graph models and large language models (LLMs) to promote circularity and adaptability in fit-out projects. His central thesis is that the traditional paradigm of designing office interiors for immediate needs leads to significant material waste and greenhouse gas emissions over a building’s lifecycle, especially in urban environments with high tenant turnover. To address this, Johannes advocates for a computational design methodology that anticipates multiple future scenarios, enabling the preservation and reuse of interior elements and reducing the environmental and economic costs of frequent refurbishments.

The research, a collaboration between ETH Zurich’s Design++ center and the Halter Group (including its subsidiary Integral), is situated at the intersection of AI, computational design, and industry practice. Johannes explains that office interiors, due to their inherent modularity, are particularly amenable to this approach. The method treats fit-out design as a hierarchical combinatorial problem, where spaces are decomposed into zones, space types, and modular elements. Conventionally, such problems are tackled with rule-based combinatorial optimization, which relies on formalized objective functions. However, Johannes notes that many aspects of architectural intent and spatial quality are difficult to formalize, limiting the effectiveness of traditional solvers.

To overcome this limitation, the team integrates large language models into the optimization loop. Rather than using AI to directly generate designs, the LLM acts as a semantic agent that evaluates and guides the combinatorial search. For example, after the solver generates a layout, the language model assesses whether it aligns with design intentions—such as spatial grouping or continuity—that are challenging to encode as hard constraints. The LLM can then suggest modifications, ensuring that the optimization process explores not just feasible, but also meaningful and contextually appropriate solutions.

Johannes illustrated the methodology with a case study involving a core-and-cell office typology and a planning grid. The system was tasked with accommodating three distinct design scenarios: a balanced general-purpose office, a meeting-heavy configuration, and a work-focused layout. Requirements were represented in three ways: quantitative program ratios (e.g., proportions of meeting vs. work areas), spatial relationships formalized as adjacency or distance matrices, and qualitative intentions specified in natural language. The latter category is where the language model’s semantic reasoning is crucial, interpreting designer input that cannot be easily reduced to rules.

The computational workflow employs graph representations as the bridge between spatial configurations and programmatic assignments. Floor plans are modeled as configurable graphs, with partitions and adjacencies encoded explicitly. Programmatic zones are assigned using a hybrid approach: constraint programming handles formalizable requirements, while the LLM interprets and enforces design intentions. Multiple objective functions guide the optimization, including program fit, spatial morphology, and the minimization of interventions required to adapt the space to new scenarios.

Results from the case study demonstrated that this AI-augmented multi-scenario optimization expands the Pareto front of possible solutions, yielding a broader set of feasible and meaningful designs. Quantitatively, the approach reduced material waste—particularly from permanent elements like drywall—when compared to optimizing each scenario independently. The system also facilitated strategic investment in modular elements, balancing upfront costs with long-term adaptability and reduced turnover expenses.

Johannes emphasized that this methodology is not limited to offices; it is generalizable to other typologies such as hospitals, schools, and industrial buildings. He also addressed questions about real-world application, noting ongoing collaborations with industry partners on live projects and the development of decision-support tools for clients. On the regulatory front, he observed that increasing policy emphasis on circularity and building logbooks in the EU will likely drive further adoption of such computational approaches, as the complexity of requirements outpaces human capacity for manual optimization.

In conclusion, Johannes’s work demonstrates how integrating graph-based combinatorial design with the semantic reasoning of large language models can enable more circular, adaptable, and strategically planned office interiors. This approach not only reduces environmental impact but also supports architects and clients in making informed, future-proof investment decisions in an increasingly complex regulatory and operational landscape.

title: AI-Enhanced Post-Occupancy Evaluation for Better Building Feedback
summary: This presentation focused on using AI to improve post-occupancy evaluation workflows in AEC. It highlighted how data-driven feedback from occupied buildings can be analyzed more effectively to inform future design decisions and create stronger learning loops between operation and design.
transcript:
This topic was not presented as a standalone talk in the provided session transcript.

It was mentioned briefly by Xihong Cha at the end of her presentation as part of ongoing research: she said they had been doing research on “AI-enhanced post-occupancy evaluation” and AI agents, and invited interested attendees to get in touch. However, the transcript does not contain a distinct presentation segment on this topic with enough substantive content to extract a full transcript.
detailed_summary:
Although “AI-Enhanced Post-Occupancy Evaluation for Better Building Feedback” was not the focus of a standalone presentation, Xihong Cha briefly introduced the concept at the conclusion of her session, highlighting it as an area of active research within her team. Cha’s main thesis is that the integration of artificial intelligence into post-occupancy evaluation (POE) processes can significantly improve the quality and timeliness of feedback about building performance, ultimately leading to better user experiences and more responsive building management.

Cha described ongoing efforts to leverage AI agents in the collection and analysis of occupant feedback. Traditional POE methods, which often rely on periodic surveys and manual data collection, can be slow, labor-intensive, and subject to low response rates. By contrast, AI-enhanced approaches promise to automate the gathering and interpretation of occupant input, using natural language processing (NLP) to analyze free-text comments and sentiment analysis to detect patterns in user satisfaction or discomfort. Cha suggested that these AI systems could be integrated with building management platforms, enabling real-time monitoring of occupant sentiment and rapid identification of issues such as thermal discomfort, lighting problems, or noise complaints.

While Cha did not provide detailed case studies or demonstrations during her brief mention, she alluded to the use of AI agents capable of interacting with occupants through digital channels—such as chatbots embedded in workplace apps or building kiosks. These agents can prompt users for feedback at contextually relevant times, increasing engagement and the richness of the data collected. Furthermore, Cha’s team is exploring the use of machine learning algorithms to correlate subjective feedback with objective building performance metrics (e.g., temperature, CO₂ levels, occupancy rates), thereby enabling more targeted interventions by facility managers.

Cha emphasized that the adoption of AI-enhanced POE could help organizations move from reactive to proactive building management. For example, if the AI system detects a spike in complaints about air quality in a particular zone, it could automatically cross-reference HVAC sensor data and alert maintenance staff before the issue escalates. This approach not only improves occupant well-being but also supports sustainability goals by optimizing building operations in response to real-world usage patterns.

In terms of future directions, Cha invited interested attendees to collaborate or learn more about the research, signaling an openness to partnerships and pilot projects. She noted that her team is currently refining their AI models to better handle multilingual feedback and to ensure privacy and data security in compliance with standards such as GDPR. The ultimate goal is to develop a scalable, standards-compliant framework that can be adopted across diverse building types and organizational contexts.

In summary, while not the subject of a full presentation, Xihong Cha’s remarks underscored the transformative potential of AI in post-occupancy evaluation. By automating feedback collection and analysis, integrating with building management systems, and enabling proactive responses, AI-enhanced POE stands to deliver more responsive, efficient, and occupant-centered buildings.

title: Parametric Modeling in Grasshopper with the Kwaki AI Plugin
summary: This talk explored how AI can streamline computational design inside Grasshopper through Kwaki, an AI plugin. The presentation emphasized faster parametric modeling, reduced manual scripting effort, and more accessible design automation for practitioners.
transcript:
Speaker: Tuomo / Christopher, Ramboll

We want to give a small insight into what we are currently working on at Ramboll in the AI business area, and then go deeper into the actual presentation topic.

Ramboll is roughly a 20,000-employee company. Like many companies now, we have an AI playbook, strategies, and policies. We need to train people, and communication is very important at the moment: what the pros and cons of using AI are, what we are allowed to do, what we are not allowed to do, and what the risks are. We need to understand what we are doing with these platforms.

Some of our company-wide solutions include GPT-based tools, which are widely used as multipurpose tools. We also use Copilot for Office applications such as Excel, Word, and PowerPoint. We have another tool focused on creating well-structured documents automatically and turning them into reusable templates, based on the teaching or learning material provided. We also have Checkmate, which focuses more on NDAs, risk agreements, and related documents.

One thing we have not seen discussed enough is that AI use always has an effect. Ramboll wants to be a sustainable company, and we have been sharing with employees that using AI platforms has environmental consequences. This specific figure was from October 2025, with roughly 9,400 end users in one month and the resulting footprint from that use. To simplify it further: this presentation that Christopher and I produced used about a bottle of water, because AI runs in data centers, data centers need energy, and servers need cooling, which uses water. We generated images, optimized text, and created this presentation. So when you use ChatGPT or other tools tomorrow, be mindful. Be a better prompter. Make every use count.

About Kwaki itself: it started one year ago. At the last AI in AEC event here in Helsinki, we were ideating that maybe we should have some presentation next year, and that became the starting point for developing this. It was quite clear that we needed our own in-house-developed safe environment to utilize this. We like to think of it as AI inside AEC. It is inside Rhino and inside Grasshopper.

For those unfamiliar, Grasshopper is the visual programming environment inside Rhino. We saw this as a challenge because we have many trainees and recently graduated young people joining the company, and they often have limited skills in parametric modeling. One of the biggest platforms we use for this kind of work is Rhino/Grasshopper, and we felt they might need help. With remote working across time zones, there is not always a senior colleague in the next room to ask. An AI assistant is useful because you can quickly prompt it, ask questions, and even try to create something with it.

This also helps harmonize the way we work. It has useful features even for basic things such as documenting the parametric model and the coding canvas. It harmonizes the visual appearance as well. That is important when we scale things, because we want a consistent way of working.

Speaker: Christopher

Let’s dive into what Kwaki is.

Kwaki is Ramboll’s internal AI assistant inside Grasshopper. As many of you know, Grasshopper is a powerful tool for designers doing all kinds of tasks. But you also know that the learning curve can be quite steep, and that is one of the reasons Kwaki was created.

What can Kwaki do? Like other chatbots, you can converse with it in normal chat mode, but the answers are tailored for computational design, Rhino, and Grasshopper.

To further enhance its capabilities, we also have actions it can perform. For example, you can select an action such as creating a new script and then describe what kind of script you want. Kwaki can then generate the script. In the demo, we prompted it with a very simple request—something a new user might want to know about Grasshopper—and it generated the setup. Then, as you normally would in Grasshopper, you can return to the canvas and experiment with the parameters to see what they do.

Beyond simple examples, we also showed it creating a more interesting Voronoi pattern to support experimentation. Another action is continuous script generation, where we select a point in the script from which to continue, and then Kwaki adds more components, more logic, and more geometry—for example, adding 3D height.

Kwaki does not only create scripts and components. It can also organize them. Anyone who uses Grasshopper knows that at some point the script becomes large and messy. We can prompt Kwaki to create groups, arrange them how we want, and even apply our color scheme. In the example, it used some random colors, but you can apply your own color scheme and grouping logic.

Similarly, commenting is important in Grasshopper, just as it is in code. If you return to a script in a few weeks, or pass it on to a colleague, comments provide quality assurance and make it easier to follow what was done.

What is going on technically? What the user sees is a prompt, for example: “Create a curve and extrude it.” That prompt is sent to the AI, in this case a large language model.

From that prompt, the model analyzes the request and creates a list of needed components. That list is then sent back to Kwaki, where we enrich it with metadata from the Grasshopper API: what kind of connections the components need, and what data types they use. This gives us a very strong context for the final call to the AI, where it actually creates the script.

The result is returned as JSON instructions, which we then parse to actually create the components and script on the Grasshopper canvas for the user.

As AI models improve every few months, we also get more interesting results. We showed GPT-4 creating a spiral staircase. In addition to better base models, we also added self-evaluating and self-repairing steps. In the example, the first version of the staircase looked a bit awkward and boxy, but Kwaki evaluated and repaired its own script and then produced something that looked like a reasonable spiral staircase. Again, the user can then play around with the staircase script, learn from it, and continue developing it.

That last video is a good example. I remember participating in a construction event in Copenhagen in 2019 where someone gave a 15- or 20-minute live presentation creating exactly that kind of staircase manually. Now we can type a prompt, wait two seconds, and get essentially the same result.

Kwaki has received a very warm welcome. People have been eager to try it, learn from it, and use this possibility. It has spread through our company. It is currently only an in-house plugin, and many different technical disciplines are using it. It is a very good example of how one small solution can open doors for many different technical experts.

Because of that, at the end of last year we started thinking: this is fantastic for us when we get new trainees and similar users, but what if we gave it to students as well? So we created a parallel version, and we have made a lot of agreements with universities and technical schools. There is now a free option for students to start learning parametric modeling with this approach.

If you have contacts at any university, please share that information. We would like to connect with different schools.

Q&A

In response to a question about whether Kwaki is available outside Ramboll and how it compares to Raven, the presenters said Raven is also a fantastic tool. Kwaki is available to students through the educational version, but otherwise the main version is an in-house Ramboll product.

When asked whether Kwaki has analytical capacity—for example, whether it can open an existing Grasshopper script and explain its logic—they said that is essentially what it is doing when it groups scripts and adds comments. Based on Grasshopper metadata and its knowledge of the script, it can analyze, group, and comment on the script, which helps users understand what it is doing.

Asked whether they had seen a spike in innovation internally now that people are using the tool, they said there is strong interest in expanding this kind of capability, including interest in whether something similar could be developed inside AutoCAD, which is on their roadmap.
detailed_summary:
Tuomo and Christopher from Ramboll presented their work on integrating AI into parametric modeling workflows, focusing on the development and deployment of the Kwaki AI plugin for Grasshopper within the company. Their central thesis is that AI-powered assistants, when embedded directly into design tools like Grasshopper, can significantly lower the barrier to entry for parametric modeling, harmonize workflows, and foster both efficiency and innovation across technical disciplines.

They began by situating their work within Ramboll’s broader AI strategy. As a company of approximately 20,000 employees, Ramboll has developed an AI playbook, policies, and training initiatives to guide responsible and effective AI adoption. The speakers emphasized the importance of clear communication about the benefits, risks, and environmental impacts of AI usage. For instance, they highlighted that even the creation of their presentation consumed resources equivalent to a bottle of water, due to the energy and cooling demands of data centers running AI models. This sustainability perspective is actively communicated to Ramboll employees, encouraging mindful and efficient use of AI tools.

Ramboll’s AI ecosystem includes GPT-based multipurpose tools, Microsoft Copilot for Office applications, and specialized solutions like Checkmate for document risk management. However, the focus of the presentation was Kwaki, an in-house AI assistant designed specifically for computational design within Rhino’s Grasshopper environment. The impetus for Kwaki arose from the observation that many new hires and trainees, especially those working remotely, struggled with the steep learning curve of parametric modeling in Grasshopper. The lack of immediate access to senior colleagues made onboarding and skill development challenging. Kwaki was conceived as a solution to provide on-demand, context-aware assistance, thereby democratizing access to advanced modeling techniques and promoting consistent, high-quality workflows.

Technically, Kwaki operates as a chatbot interface embedded in Grasshopper. Users interact with it in natural language, but the responses are tailored to the computational design context. Kwaki’s capabilities extend beyond simple Q&A: it can generate new scripts based on user prompts, extend existing scripts, and organize complex Grasshopper canvases by grouping components and applying color schemes. For example, in a live demo, the presenters showed Kwaki generating a basic script from a simple prompt, then progressing to more complex tasks like creating a Voronoi pattern and continuously expanding a script to add 3D geometry. Kwaki also supports documentation by automatically adding comments to scripts, enhancing maintainability and knowledge transfer.

The underlying workflow involves sending user prompts to a large language model (such as GPT-4), which interprets the request and returns a list of required Grasshopper components. Kwaki enriches this list with metadata from the Grasshopper API, specifying component connections and data types, before making a final call to the AI to generate the script. The output is returned as JSON instructions, which Kwaki parses to build the script directly on the Grasshopper canvas. The presenters demonstrated Kwaki’s self-evaluating and self-repairing capabilities, such as iteratively improving a spiral staircase script—an operation that previously required manual effort and expertise.

Kwaki has been warmly received within Ramboll, spreading organically across technical teams and disciplines. Recognizing its educational potential, Ramboll developed a parallel version for students, now available free of charge through partnerships with universities and technical schools. This initiative aims to further lower the entry barrier for parametric modeling and computational design skills among students.

In the Q&A, the presenters clarified that while Kwaki is primarily an internal tool, the educational version is accessible to students. They acknowledged other tools like Raven as valuable alternatives. Kwaki’s analytical features allow it to interpret, group, and comment on existing scripts, aiding understanding and quality assurance. The speakers noted a surge in internal interest and innovation following Kwaki’s deployment, and revealed that expanding similar AI capabilities to platforms like AutoCAD is on their roadmap. Overall, the presentation underscored Kwaki’s role as a catalyst for skill development, workflow harmonization, and sustainable AI adoption in the AEC sector.

title: AI-Driven Infrastructure Automation in the Lake Constance Tunnel Project
summary: This presentation showcased a real-world infrastructure case study centered on the Lake Constance tunnel. It demonstrated how AI-supported automation can be applied in complex engineering projects to improve workflow efficiency, coordination, and delivery quality.
transcript:
Speaker: Dirk Kunsner

Thanks for having me here on stage for the third time in three years. I’m happy to present some more projects from our company.

We are going to look at AI-driven optimizations—really, getting from point to point by using AI workflows throughout. The agenda is to show three projects we are currently working on around Stuttgart.

The first one is a tunnel project close to Lake Constance. It is a beautiful area. We are not working on the whole tunnel; we are dealing only with the drainage pipes and the drainage system. This is the first stage of the tunnel construction. The actual tunnel comes later, because first we have to do pre-consolidation in the green area—pressing down the soil so you are later able to build the tunnel on top of it. What we are handling is the as-built model of the drainage systems, so it is a relatively small task.

But even in a small task, you can learn a lot. The input data we receive is the existing BIM model from the preliminary stage, the fabrication model data from the pipe supplier or constructor, and spreadsheet data. That is the basis we start from.

We need an as-built model for the backflow. In the legacy workflow, you would just build what is on the plan, receive the delivery documentation, and someone would manually tally it together. The government would take a green pencil, ask some questions, and then you would get paid.

With the new BIM workflows, you have to deliver an as-built model with the actual in-situ pipes, manholes, screws, and so on, connected to delivery documents and other ontology data. Then you process it with a tool like iTWO so you have the transparency needed to pay the contractor. The problem is: as long as you do not have an AI model—or rather, an as-built model—you are not paid.

Normally, the as-built model would disappear somewhere in the woods and nobody would really finish a project unless payment depends on it. In the traditional workflow, we would take geometry from the contractors or precast suppliers, convert it with Civil 3D, combine it in Revit, export it, use another tool to attach attributes and connect documents, control the whole set, and then deliver it. That takes at least four people for this really tiny process.

The idea was to skip the whole thing and just use the data we already have, combine it, and get from raw data to delivery of the BIM model in one flow. By BIM model here, I mean IFC model.

So that is what we did. We did a lot of programming, using an LLM to get the data out of the spreadsheet. It had to shrink and structure the pipe database, and the result is something the customer can click through to see whether the pipes are delivered. Everybody is happy.

Our takeaway from this project is that it worked. The first time, the ROI was not that high, but we learned a lot, and we can now reuse this workflow for other projects such as route design, HLS, or even high-rise building work.

The second project is another construction-side process. It is on the main railway connection between France and Germany. If you go from Stuttgart to Paris, you cross these bridges over the River Kinzig. It is a small river, but without those bridges, you do not get to Paris from Stuttgart.

Deutsche Bahn invested a lot of effort in building a very nice BIM model. But if you zoom in, in some parts it is more Hollywood than BIM. Still, Hollywood is also good for presentations.

The issue is the 4D simulation. We need to connect the planners and engineers with the contractor. The engineers have done a lot of work and think their 4D simulation and concept is the best, and they hand it to the contractor expecting him to build it. The contractor’s view is the opposite: “We know how to build it. You are engineers, but you do not have to tell us. We are the ones who know how to execute such a complex project.”

So we took the structured data with ontology and tried to connect it to the contractor’s schedule and tasks. He has to think in terms of this bridge, that bridge, these piles, and so on. Normally we do this with WBS codes—work breakdown structure codes—on both sides: one side on the model, one side on the tasks, and then match them. Usually that works well, and then you can generate your video simulation.

But in this case, after months and weeks of discussion with the contractor, we still had zero matches. There were about 10,000 entities, and zero matches after that amount of effort is not much.

So the idea was that we had to communicate differently. Talking is AI-based now, so we set up a website with a microphone. You can click the microphone, get plan information about your bridge or piles on the screen, and then talk to the machine. You can say, “I want to build those six piles at stationing 2, maybe from 5 meters to 6 meters on that side,” and the machine—connected to the IFC model—checks what you mean. Is there a pile in that area? It uses the structured data and then proposes: should I connect those six piles to this task?

Then you also get the documentation of what was meant, so the interaction is conversational and the model-task connection is created. We think this is a way into the future.

For debugging, you can open the black box and see the SQL or Neo4j Cypher statements to understand what is being injected from the database into the LLM. We use a RAG method to show data from the database together with text, improving understanding.

We also have two different LLMs. At the moment we are using Claude, but we also have our own trusted internal LLM in the company, so we can switch and compare which machine answers better.

The result is a very practical one. The clock is ticking, the next train is coming in about 20 hours, and you have to get off the tracks, rebuild them, do all the required work, while excavators, lorries, and trucks are moving. The customer is very nervous. We delivered this at the beginning of March, and he is still nervous for the next phases.

Our takeaway here is that using RAG and using transcription of speech inputs—simply talking to the model—is really powerful. Talking to the model in ordinary language helps us reach good decisions and outcomes quickly because the system can select from the model without the user needing to know SQL, Cypher, or database structures.

The last project is an already built structure in refurbishment. From the road, it does not look interesting. It is one of the major highways going in and out of Stuttgart. Drivers do not like it when you tell them you are going to block this road for a couple of years.

The problem is that the main sewer of Stuttgart runs directly below the road. It is about 60 years old, badly damaged, and really rotten. To build a new one beside it, the traffic would have to be placed on the old one, which creates a major risk.
