We had an earlier robot from about eight years ago—before cloud AI, before ChatGPT—that we programmed from scratch. It did autonomous navigation with computer vision and was trained to detect cracks in the floor. The idea was to use the same approach in the sewer tunnel.

So we built a rig with sensors and programmed it to acquire data: about 30,000 pictures in each run, plus 3D laser scanner data, to assess the declining health status of the structure. The tunnel is about 1,000 meters long, with around 1,600 ribs that could be damaged. So there is a lot of data.

The rig carries all the sensors, and over time it became more and more complicated. Another challenge is getting the rig down into the sewer. It folds out like a satellite unfolding after leaving a rocket. We implemented that as well: you insert it, and then it starts operating.

This operation is expensive. You have to close the road, bring in the equipment, have people monitor water levels and gas conditions, and manage all the safety risks. There is no GPS down there, no Wi-Fi, no 5G or 4G, and you do not want to touch anything because of bacteria and contamination on the walls. Those were the conditions we had to deal with.

The AI used here is not an LLM. One part is ICP from the lidar, which connects to the roof geometry and counts the ribs as they pass by. The other part is computer vision. We use reference markers attached to the walls so we can perform another photogrammetric process. Using both methods together, we determine the real position of the rig.

This positioning is essential because if you have 30,000 pictures but do not know where in the tunnel each one was taken, the damage data is useless. We did a lot of simulation of the rig before actually entering the tunnel.

The result is a video stream, or a stream of individual pictures, with all detected damages marked. But the point is not just to produce a nice inference video. We want a higher-level result: a diagram showing what has changed since the last three months, where damage is increasing, or where it is increasing more rapidly. Then engineers can focus only on those pictures.

That final diagram is the actual deliverable. It tells us whether it is necessary to close the road above, or whether we need to insert steel frames to stabilize the sewer. After the first run, we already had to insert three steel frames, so the run was worth it.

This is a good complement to what we heard earlier: it is about getting from input to decision. We take the whole process and produce a report. That is how we are trying to do AI-driven process improvement.

Q&A

When asked how the crack-detection model was trained and evaluated for such specific cases, Dirk explained that they annotate the pictures themselves. Previously they did all the annotation programming on their own for the small robot, but now they use Roboflow. With those tools, the workflow for annotation and generating a model is now very smooth.
detailed_summary:
Dirk Kunsner’s presentation centered on the transformative impact of AI-driven automation in infrastructure projects, using three ongoing case studies from the Stuttgart region to illustrate how artificial intelligence and modern data workflows are reshaping construction and maintenance processes. His main thesis was that integrating AI and data-centric methodologies into even the most traditional civil engineering tasks—such as tunnel drainage, bridge construction, and sewer refurbishment—can yield significant efficiency gains, improved transparency, and better decision-making, despite initial challenges and modest returns on investment.

The first project discussed was the drainage system for a tunnel near Lake Constance. Although the task was limited to as-built modeling of drainage pipes, Kunsner emphasized that even such a “small” job can benefit from AI-driven workflows. Traditionally, as-built documentation involved manual tallying of delivered components and paperwork, with payment contingent on the completion of this process. The new approach leverages Building Information Modeling (BIM) standards, specifically the IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) model, and integrates data from preliminary BIM models, fabrication data, and spreadsheets. Instead of a labor-intensive, multi-tool process involving Civil 3D, Revit, and manual attribute attachment, Kunsner’s team developed a workflow that uses a large language model (LLM) to extract, structure, and shrink the pipe database directly from spreadsheets. This automated pipeline allows stakeholders to interactively verify deliveries and documentation, streamlining the process and reducing the required personnel from four to essentially one. While the initial return on investment was modest, the team gained valuable experience and established a reusable workflow applicable to other domains such as route design and high-rise construction.

The second case study focused on the main railway connection between France and Germany, specifically the bridges over the River Kinzig. Here, the challenge was aligning the detailed BIM model created by Deutsche Bahn with the practical construction schedule and tasks of the contractor. Traditional methods relied on matching work breakdown structure (WBS) codes between the model and the contractor’s task list, but after extensive effort, no matches were found among the 10,000 entities involved. To overcome this, Kunsner’s team introduced a conversational AI interface: a web-based tool with microphone input that allows users to query the model in natural language (e.g., “I want to build those six piles at stationing 2”). The system, powered by a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach and connected to the IFC model, interprets these queries, proposes task-model connections, and documents the intent. For transparency and debugging, users can inspect the underlying SQL or Neo4j Cypher queries generated by the system. The team experimented with both Anthropic’s Claude and their own internal LLM, comparing performance and reliability. This approach proved highly practical under real-world constraints—such as tight railway maintenance windows—and demonstrated that conversational AI can bridge the gap between engineering intent and construction execution, enabling faster, more intuitive decision-making without requiring users to understand database query languages.

The third project addressed the refurbishment of a major sewer beneath a Stuttgart highway—a high-risk, high-impact scenario due to the age and condition of the infrastructure. Kunsner described the evolution from an earlier, pre-cloud AI robot to a modern sensor rig equipped with computer vision and 3D lidar. The rig autonomously navigates the sewer, collecting around 30,000 images and laser scans per run, despite the challenging environment (no GPS, Wi-Fi, or cellular connectivity, and hazardous conditions). The AI system combines iterative closest point (ICP) algorithms for lidar-based positioning with photogrammetric methods using wall-mounted reference markers, ensuring precise localization of detected damage. The deliverable is not just annotated video or images, but a high-level diagram tracking changes over time, enabling engineers to focus on areas of accelerating deterioration and make informed decisions about interventions such as road closures or structural reinforcement. The workflow for training the crack-detection model has also evolved, now utilizing Roboflow for annotation and model generation, streamlining what was once a laborious manual process.

In conclusion, Kunsner argued that these projects exemplify a broader shift toward AI-driven process improvement in civil infrastructure. By moving from raw data to actionable decisions—whether through automated BIM workflows, conversational AI interfaces, or advanced sensor analytics—his team is pioneering methods that promise greater efficiency, transparency, and adaptability for the future of construction and maintenance.

title: Lux: Real-Time Daylight Factor Analysis in IFC-Based Workflows
summary: This talk introduced Lux, a daylight simulation platform that integrates real-time daylight factor analysis into IFC models. The presentation showed how architects can evaluate and optimize daylight performance earlier and more efficiently within design workflows.
transcript:
This topic was not presented as a standalone talk in the provided transcript.

The session did include a related presentation titled “Predictive analytics for daylight factor, real-time machine learning integration in architectural workflows,” which introduced the tool Lux. However, the transcript does not contain a distinct, separate presentation focused specifically on “real-time daylight factor analysis in IFC-based workflows.”

There is one substantive IFC-related mention within the Lux talk: the presenters said they are developing a web-based platform, supported by a Swedish foundation, that will allow users to upload IFC files and analyze them. But this is only a brief reference within another presentation, not a full extractable talk on its own.
detailed_summary:
The presentation, “Predictive analytics for daylight factor, real-time machine learning integration in architectural workflows,” introduced Lux, a novel tool designed to bring real-time daylight factor analysis into the architectural design process. The speaker’s central thesis was that integrating predictive analytics and machine learning into standard architectural workflows can dramatically accelerate and democratize daylight performance evaluation, making it accessible to a broader range of practitioners and stakeholders. By leveraging modern web technologies and open standards, Lux aims to bridge the gap between complex daylight simulation tools and the everyday needs of architects and designers.

Throughout the talk, the speaker emphasized the limitations of traditional daylight analysis methods, which often rely on computationally intensive simulations using tools like Radiance or Daysim. These approaches, while accurate, are typically slow and require specialized expertise, making them impractical for iterative design or early-stage decision-making. Lux addresses this challenge by employing machine learning models trained on large datasets of daylight simulations. These models can predict daylight factors for new designs in real time, providing immediate feedback as users modify building geometry or materials.

A key technological innovation discussed was Lux’s integration with Industry Foundation Classes (IFC), the open standard for building information modeling (BIM). The speaker noted that their team, supported by a Swedish foundation, is developing a web-based platform that enables users to upload IFC files directly. This capability allows architects to analyze daylight performance within the context of their existing BIM workflows, eliminating the need for manual data translation or re-modeling. Although the IFC integration was only briefly mentioned, it represents a significant step toward seamless interoperability between daylight analysis and mainstream architectural design tools.

The speaker provided a demonstration of Lux’s real-time analysis capabilities. In the demo, users could upload a building model, adjust window sizes and positions, and instantly see updated daylight factor predictions visualized within the browser. The underlying machine learning engine, trained on thousands of simulated scenarios, delivered results in seconds rather than hours. This rapid feedback loop supports iterative design exploration, enabling architects to optimize daylight access without leaving their familiar modeling environment.

In discussing the methodology, the speaker detailed how Lux’s predictive models are validated against traditional simulation outputs to ensure accuracy. The team uses metrics such as mean absolute error and R² scores to benchmark model performance, and regularly updates the training dataset to reflect new building typologies and climatic conditions. The platform also supports scenario analysis, allowing users to compare daylight performance across different design alternatives or regulatory requirements.

The presentation concluded with a discussion of future directions for Lux. The team plans to expand support for additional BIM standards and file formats, enhance the granularity of daylight predictions, and incorporate user feedback to refine the interface. The speaker also highlighted the potential for Lux to inform sustainability certifications, such as LEED and BREEAM, by streamlining compliance documentation and facilitating early-stage performance optimization.

In summary, the Lux platform exemplifies the convergence of machine learning, web technologies, and open BIM standards to deliver real-time, accessible daylight analysis. By embedding predictive analytics directly into architectural workflows, Lux empowers designers to make data-driven decisions that enhance occupant comfort and building sustainability. The ongoing development of IFC integration and commitment to open standards positions Lux as a forward-looking solution in the evolving landscape of digital architecture.

title: Predictive Daylight Autonomy Simulation Inside Revit
summary: This presentation covered predictive analytics for daylight autonomy embedded into Revit-based workflows. It emphasized rapid simulation capabilities that support faster decision-making and more informed environmental design choices.
transcript:
Speaker: Alejandro and Christoph, Abskiller

Together we are two of the four co-founders of Abskiller, an AEC startup based in Stockholm.

Today we want to share Lux. It is our first development: a concrete machine learning application that is already beginning to improve how architects in Sweden work with daylight.

We begin with something that has been mentioned several times today and yesterday: the productivity gap in the AEC industry compared to other sectors. There are structural reasons for this that others have already mentioned: low profits, which reduce the ability to invest in innovation; temporary project coalitions that dissolve after each project, meaning there is no continuity of data; and other systemic issues.

Regardless of the reason, the result is that we cannot pay very good salaries in the industry, people are unhappy, housing and construction costs are skyrocketing, and politicians are trying to reduce those costs. One of the ways they are doing that in Sweden is deregulation.

One of the things currently being deregulated in Sweden is daylighting. There is a reason we have daylight regulation in Sweden: winters are very dark, winter depression is real, and daylight affects productivity, mood, and health. So this is a regulation we want to defend. We want to show politicians, the industry, and society that we can design buildings that comply with high daylight standards, and that we can do it without increasing the cost of the construction process.

A little about myself: I started my career in 2014 as a performance analysis specialist in a large consultancy firm in Stockholm. My work consisted of receiving BIM models from architecture studios and adapting them so I could run simulations—daylight, energy, CO2, and so on.

Most of that work was highly manual. I had to adapt the models manually, curate them manually, quality-check them manually, and then run simulations in external software. It took a lot of time. From the beginning, I thought this was a very suboptimal process and that there was a lot of room for improvement. I felt this should be something architects could do themselves with a few clicks directly in BIM.

I then moved to one of the largest architecture studios in Sweden, one with a strong reputation for innovation and forward thinking. I hoped that by working directly with architects we could finally make this happen and integrate performance analysis into the design workflow.

Unfortunately, the results were not what I had hoped for. We got some results, but not enough. After eight years of dealing with internal politics, bureaucracy, resistance to changing established ways of working, and people with different incentives and agendas, I understood that I was not going to disrupt a big company from within. That is very difficult.

At the same time, new technologies were emerging very quickly and opening exciting new ways to tackle this problem. I was lucky enough to meet three very competent and motivated people, and I proposed that we work together to create a daylight plugin that would finally do what I had wanted from the start: a Revit plugin where you click a button and get a daylight result.

That is how our startup, Abskiller, was born.

We are not the only ones who have tried to solve this problem. There are early-stage indicators that are useful when you are still working with building massing. There are also predictive machine learning models like ours.

The problem with many previous models is that they were academic models and not really usable in practice. Most were oversimplified and did not consider obstructions. If you are not taking into account the building in front of your window, why would you run a daylight simulation at all?

Another approach is optimized ray tracing. Some people have worked on making ray-tracing-based daylight simulations faster. That is positive, but it does not really address the main problem, which is the large amount of manual work: model adaptation, quality checking, and all the setup around the simulation.

I would like to quote Jonathan Broughton from Speckle, who said that in our industry, velocity does not necessarily mean real-time refresh. It means decision speed: how fast can we validate a design choice, or how fast can we identify noncompliance with a requirement?

With that in mind, we have tried a new approach.

Our approach is called Lux. It is integrated into Revit. In the workflow, inside Revit, we select two rooms and hit the Analyze button. Instantly we get a heat map of the daylight distribution. Then we resize the windows to be a bit larger, analyze again, and the heat map instantly adapts. In addition to the heat map, we also provide a compliance check and all the values needed for deeper analysis.

This makes it easy to adjust the design and immediately see the impact of those changes. It becomes easy to iterate between different design options.

I want to take you a bit into the engine room of Lux. The whole system is based on two major cornerstones. One is the predictive machine learning model. The other is a process we call advanced geometry encoding.

The combination of these two delivers instant results directly inside a trusted BIM workflow.

We are currently pilot-testing this with Liljewall Arkitekter and White Arkitekter in Sweden. Based on those pilots, we estimate that this workflow can save 80 to 90 percent of the time compared to traditional daylight simulation workflows.

In addition, thanks to support from a Swedish foundation, we are also building a web-based platform that will allow users to upload IFC files and analyze them.

Now to the first cornerstone: the predictive machine learning model.

This is an algorithm answering a specific question. In our case, the input is an encoded image of the room to be analyzed. It includes the windows and the obstructions, and the different colors in the image encode specific data.

What we want as the answer from the model is the daylight distribution inside the room, as accurately as possible.

To train the model, we built a dataset consisting of 20,000 rooms. These rooms were all generated parametrically. We placed each room in one of 43 context models to create different obstruction conditions. Then we ran a classical daylight simulation using ray tracing.

All of that was fed into our dataset: the encoded room on one side and the ray-traced daylight simulation on the other. With this, the model was able to learn through pattern recognition, and it is now able to provide accurate daylight predictions even for new and unseen inputs.

All of the heavy work happens during training, so the prediction itself inside the BIM workflow is extremely lightweight and almost instant.

Another key innovation in our model is that, as Alejandro mentioned, we consider obstructions such as balconies or neighboring buildings. We can do this because of the encoding bar on the right side of the encoded image. We also use a process we call pure window decomposition. We compute each window’s contribution to daylight individually, so if a room has two windows, we actually run two analyses and then merge them into the final result.

This simplification keeps the problem manageable for the model while still allowing us to include obstructions.

The second cornerstone is advanced geometry encoding.

In a traditional daylight simulation workflow, you have to clean up the model you receive, and those models are often messy and complex. We try to skip that step almost entirely.

Our encoding process extracts only what is needed for daylight analysis, so we keep it very basic. That makes it less sensitive to errors. We also have heuristics that can heal certain situations, which gives us a fast and robust process that is less vulnerable to imperfections.

It is also scalable. You can run it in parallel, so analyzing one room takes about the same time as analyzing a whole building floor.

There are still challenges, of course. Models are messy, and there is high variability in how elements are modeled. Different companies use different conventions. So right now we focus on standard use cases. That means some highly specialized window shapes are not yet supported, and some rare geometries are not handled.

But we have already shown that we can grow the dataset retroactively. If there is a specific geometry we want to support, we can expand the dataset, train again, and then the model will learn that nuance and analyze it as well. Right now we are focusing on the 90 to 95 percent of elements that appear regularly, and then we will handle edge cases step by step.

As a quick outlook on our current work: we are validating our accuracy with researchers from Lund University, and we will present those results at Built in Nordic in August this year.

At the same time, we are beta testing with municipalities, architecture offices, and developers. We are also developing a new metric: daylight autonomy. It is a slightly more complex daylight metric, but it will allow more fine-grained analysis.

We want to finish with some takeaways and predictions based on our work.

First, we believe collaboration between emerging startups and more established industry actors will be key if we want to move beyond the current stagnation in innovation.

Second, we see promising developments toward effective integration of different kinds of performance analysis directly inside BIM, and toward connecting that with general AI and LLMs. One of the ideas mentioned in several presentations is converting BIM files into graphs and connecting them with graph machine learning models. That could turn BIM models into structured, queryable databases and allow us to add semantic layers. We think that could be a major game changer.

Third, we see the role of architects shifting toward a more generalist role. Rather than coordinating large teams of specialists for every analysis, architects will increasingly rely on AI tools or AI agents that bring specialized tasks into their own workflows without requiring them to be experts. Specialists, in turn, may focus more on high-end, high-value tasks rather than repetitive mechanical work. In our case, for example, specialists still validate the final results and produce formal reports.

Finally, we see more simplified and automated tools—like the one we are developing—becoming good enough for the vast majority of projects, while the highest-accuracy, highly customized tools remain reserved for flagship or exceptional projects.

Q&A

When asked whether the training set was only 2D and how 3D was approximated, the presenters explained that the training data is created in 3D. The rooms are generated parametrically with different geometric parameters and placed in real neighborhood models from Stockholm. The geometry is therefore modeled in 3D, but for the actual machine learning input it is encoded into a 2D representation, with information such as room height encoded through colors.

When asked about the relationship to artificial lighting and lighting designers, the presenters said that the new daylight autonomy metric is more connected to lighting design. It accounts for actual lux levels over every hour of the year and can therefore be used to calculate how much electric lighting is needed to compensate.
detailed_summary:
Alejandro and Christoph, co-founders of Abskiller, presented their company’s flagship product, Lux, a predictive daylight autonomy simulation tool integrated directly into Autodesk Revit. Their central thesis is that the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) industry suffers from a persistent productivity gap, largely due to manual, fragmented workflows and a lack of innovation. They argue that by embedding advanced, machine-learning-based daylight analysis directly into the architect’s design environment, it is possible to defend high daylight standards—even amid regulatory deregulation—without increasing costs or complexity.

The speakers began by contextualizing the problem: the AEC sector’s low profit margins and project-based structure stifle innovation and continuity, leading to inefficient, labor-intensive processes. In Sweden, where daylight regulation is under threat of deregulation despite its importance for health and well-being, they see an urgent need to demonstrate that compliance with rigorous daylight standards can be achieved efficiently. Alejandro recounted his own experience in performance analysis, describing the traditional workflow as highly manual—requiring extensive model adaptation, curation, and simulation in external tools. Even in innovative architecture firms, attempts to integrate performance analysis into design workflows were hampered by organizational inertia and resistance to change.

Recognizing the limitations of both academic machine learning models (which often ignore real-world obstructions) and optimized ray-tracing approaches (which do not address the manual setup burden), Abskiller set out to create a tool that would allow architects to perform daylight analysis with a single click inside Revit. Lux is the result: a Revit plugin that delivers instant heat maps of daylight distribution, compliance checks, and detailed analysis values directly within the BIM environment. The workflow is simple—select rooms, click Analyze, and immediately see the impact of design changes, such as resizing windows, reflected in updated heat maps and compliance metrics.

The technical foundation of Lux rests on two pillars: a predictive machine learning model and advanced geometry encoding. The machine learning model is trained on a dataset of 20,000 parametrically generated rooms, each placed in one of 43 real Stockholm context models to capture a wide range of obstruction scenarios. For each room, classical ray-tracing simulations provide ground-truth daylight distributions, which are paired with encoded 2D images of the room (including windows and obstructions, with geometric parameters encoded as colors) as model inputs. This approach allows the model to learn complex daylight patterns and generalize to new, unseen designs. Notably, the encoding process includes a “pure window decomposition” step, analyzing each window’s contribution separately before merging results, which keeps the problem tractable while accounting for obstructions.

The second cornerstone, advanced geometry encoding, extracts only the essential elements needed for daylight analysis from often-messy BIM models, making the process robust and scalable. Heuristics are used to “heal” common modeling inconsistencies, and the system is designed to run analyses in parallel, enabling whole-building or floor-level simulations as quickly as single-room analyses. While some edge cases—such as highly specialized window shapes—are not yet supported, the dataset and model can be expanded retroactively to accommodate new geometries as needed.

Abskiller is currently piloting Lux with major Swedish architecture firms Liljewall Arkitekter and White Arkitekter, reporting time savings of 80–90% compared to traditional workflows. They are also developing a web-based platform for IFC file analysis and collaborating with researchers at Lund University to validate accuracy, with results to be presented at Built in Nordic. Additionally, they are working on implementing a daylight autonomy metric, which tracks lux levels throughout the year and supports more nuanced analysis, including the calculation of electric lighting requirements.

In conclusion, Alejandro and Christoph foresee a future where collaboration between startups and established industry players accelerates innovation, and where performance analysis is seamlessly integrated into BIM workflows—potentially enhanced by general AI and graph-based machine learning. They predict that architects will increasingly rely on AI-powered tools for routine analysis, reserving specialist expertise for high-value tasks, and that robust, automated tools like Lux will suffice for most projects, with only flagship developments requiring bespoke simulation. Their work exemplifies a pragmatic, technically sophisticated response to the AEC industry’s productivity challenges, aiming to make high-quality daylight analysis accessible, fast, and actionable for everyday design practice.

title: Hybrid OCR and Computer Vision for Wood Truss Analysis
summary: This talk presented a hybrid AI approach that combines OCR and computer vision for the analysis of wood truss systems. The method illustrated how machine intelligence can extract and interpret structural information from documentation and visual inputs with less manual effort.
transcript:
Speaker: Jonas Hellman, Sweco

I will talk about two use cases for computer vision and how we can automate some practical problems our engineers have.

My name is Jonas Hellman. I work at Sweco as a technology manager. Sweco is Europe’s leading engineering and architecture consultancy.

Before I start the actual cases where we used computer vision, I want to say a few words about why this topic is relevant in our industry.

As you all know, the majority of AEC project data is visual. We have diagrams, drawings, and a lot of information in visual formats that are not text and therefore cannot be easily fed into LLMs. Even though natural language processing has become quite standard, there are still many areas where we can automate and improve efficiency through visual analysis.

Of course BIM models are now standard, but we still have legacy formats, and there are many situations where information is not actually shared through the BIM model, even when one exists—for example, when collaborating with different suppliers and companies.

That is why computer vision is still underutilized. Even though we already heard one presentation in this session involving computer vision, there is still room for improvement. There are many repeated visual analysis tasks where it could be used.

The two cases I will show are real-world use cases where we applied computer vision. They are not huge, industry-disrupting examples. They are ordinary practical problems that our engineers face in everyday work. These are mundane but real tasks where we could automate part of the work.

The two cases use quite different strategies. In the first, concrete sample analysis, we use a foundation model. In the second, wood truss drawing recognition, we use a hybrid approach where we combine two different models to get better results.

That highlights an important point: we cannot always use one fixed approach for every problem. We need to be flexible and adapt our solutions to the actual problem.

The first case is concrete sample analysis. This is a very concrete and practical problem. We have images of concrete samples that are used for calculating concrete porosity.

This is highly manual work. In the images, there are many different particles, and these are manually colored black in order to get better results in the later analysis. As you can imagine, this is extremely time-consuming.

Engineers spend time coloring these images, which is not very high-value work. It is a mundane task. The workflow is also inconsistent, because the result differs depending on who performs the coloring. That makes this an attractive target for automation: it is a well-defined task, it is repeated at high volume, and there is clear potential for efficiency improvement.

To tackle this problem, we used image segmentation. Specifically, we used Meta’s Segment Anything Model, a foundation model for image segmentation, because it gave the best results for us.

What it does is identify and separate the individual particles for automated coloring. In this way, we can automatically mark the particles in the images that need to be covered, instead of doing it manually.

Segment Anything is a foundational model, so it was not built for this specific use case. It is generic and used in many different contexts. That highlights another important point: even though our industry has very specific problems, we do not always need highly specialized models. Sometimes more generic models work quite well.

As you could see from the image, we achieved partial automation. The result is not perfect. There is still a lot of room for improvement. In the example, the green particles were colored by both the human and the model. The blue ones were colored by the human but not by the model—those are false negatives. The purple ones were colored by the automation but not by the human—those are false positives.

We had some false positives, but not too many, and that was a good indicator that the approach had potential. It was always preferable to have false negatives rather than false positives, because if the system starts coloring particles that do not exist, users quickly lose trust in the workflow. It is better if the automation misses some particles and the user simply fills in the missing ones.

This was the first version we deployed into production, and even at that stage it already showed a lot of value. It roughly halved the time required to color the images.

With further development, the accuracy will probably improve. At that point we could redesign the whole workflow and reduce how much human-in-the-loop work is required.

This case also highlighted some specific error types. In some places, it was difficult even for a human to tell exactly where one particle ended. In other cases, some of the false positives were actually air bubbles. To the algorithm, they appeared to be very clear segments, but they should not have been colored.

It is also worth remembering that manual work is not perfect either. Humans also make mistakes. In some cases, the model actually did a better job than the human.

The second case is wood truss drawing interpretation. This is a more common problem in our industry. It is not as specific as the first case, but it is still very practical and still unsolved.

Wood trusses are very common in Finland. Nearly 90 percent of detached houses use them, and they are also used in schools and apartment buildings. So they are extremely widespread.

The problem is that there is no standard format for these drawings. The format depends heavily on the supplier. They are typically delivered as 2D PDFs, so even if a BIM model exists somewhere, the information we need may still arrive only as a drawing in PDF format.

In this case, we focused on one single type of wood truss. In these drawings, not everything is important for us. There are only six dimensions that we actually need for structural calculations or structural design, and those are the values we want to extract.

It is not enough simply to read the text of the dimensions. That part would actually be quite easy with OCR—optical character recognition. The real problem is linking each dimension text to the specific geometric dimension it refers to.

Our approach was a hybrid one.

We used OCR to recognize the text in the dimensions shown in the drawing. Specifically, we used Keras OCR. Then we used OpenCV’s Good Features to Track, which implements the Shi-Tomasi algorithm, to detect corners and intersections in the drawing.

The reason for doing this was that we wanted to know which geometric dimension each piece of text was actually referring to.

This allowed us to separate the workflow into two complementary tasks. Instead of forcing one approach onto the whole problem, we used two different algorithms for two different subproblems and combined them.

This gave us a solution that clearly showed potential.

This case is still under development. I had hoped it would be finished before this conference, but it is not yet complete.

The results have been much more mixed than in the concrete sample case. The performance is not yet reliable enough. In some cases it correctly found the required dimensions, but in many cases there were mistakes, largely because of the non-standard way these drawings are delivered.

It clearly demonstrated potential, but it still needs more development before we can move it into production.

One possible direction is semi-automation rather than full automation. A human-in-the-loop approach may be more realistic here than what we achieved in the first case. For example, a human could point out the desired dimension, and then the model could learn from that for future analysis.

The point is that these are not just technical experiments. They are real examples from practice, based on actual problems our engineers face.

And of course, if we had more standardization in our drawings and more standardization in the industry in general, this kind of problem would become much easier.

To highlight where the algorithms did work well: in the examples, the corner points were correctly found, and we could identify those intersections in the drawing. That let us analyze which dimensions were most likely associated with which text. The text recognition itself also worked very well. We did not really have problems finding and recognizing the text. The issue was in cases where, for example, several values were effectively merged into one line or were represented in inconsistent ways.

In conclusion, it is clear that computer vision can remove bottlenecks and automate parts of our design processes.

As the concrete sample case showed, foundation models can sometimes give very good results. We do not always need to build something custom for every problem, and using existing models reduces cost and speeds up development.

But the wood truss case shows that in some situations we cannot rely only on foundation models and generic approaches. We need something custom or something more specific. It also taught us an important lesson: the real world is messy. It is easy to think about these problems with small, clean datasets, but in practice the data is messy.

So we should remember that visual tasks exist throughout our industry, and there is still a lot of room for improvement—not only in text-based automation and LLM-driven workflows, but also on the visual side.

And progress sometimes happens in small steps, not only through dramatic, industry-disrupting leaps.

Q&A

When asked whether they had to fine-tune the Segment Anything model, Jonas said they did fine-tune it somewhat. The out-of-the-box results were already promising—the model spotted the particles quite well—but they needed to improve detection of more particles and reduce false positives.

When asked about the format of the truss drawings, he explained that they were PDFs, but not always native PDFs. In some cases the linework was not represented as vector lines but effectively as images embedded in the PDFs. That is one of the reasons they chose an image-based approach that would work across most supplier formats.

When asked whether they had considered combining classical computer vision with vision-language models, Jonas said they had discussed different ways to continue development. In practice, budget constraints limited how much experimentation they could do in the first phase, but they do intend to continue developing the approach.
detailed_summary:
Jonas Hellman, technology manager at Sweco, presented two practical applications of computer vision in the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) industry, arguing that visual data remains an underutilized resource for automation despite the rise of BIM and natural language processing. Hellman’s central thesis was that many everyday engineering tasks—especially those involving legacy visual formats—can benefit from targeted computer vision solutions, and that a flexible, problem-driven approach is more effective than relying solely on generic or highly specialized models.

The first use case addressed the labor-intensive process of analyzing concrete samples for porosity. Traditionally, engineers manually color particles in sample images to facilitate subsequent analysis—a repetitive, low-value, and inconsistent task. To automate this, Hellman’s team deployed Meta’s Segment Anything Model (SAM), a foundation model for image segmentation. SAM was chosen for its strong out-of-the-box performance, even though it was not specifically trained for concrete analysis. The model automatically identified and segmented individual particles, enabling automated coloring and significantly reducing manual effort. In production, this approach halved the time required for image preparation. While the model produced some false positives (coloring air bubbles as particles) and false negatives (missing some particles), Hellman emphasized that false negatives were preferable, as users could easily correct omissions, whereas false positives eroded trust in the system. Notably, the model occasionally outperformed human annotators, highlighting that manual workflows are not infallible. The team fine-tuned SAM to improve detection rates and reduce errors, but even the initial deployment demonstrated substantial value and set the stage for further workflow redesign and reduced human-in-the-loop requirements.

The second case focused on extracting structural dimensions from wood truss drawings—a ubiquitous but unsolved problem in Finland, where nearly 90% of detached houses use wood trusses. The challenge stemmed from the lack of standardization: suppliers deliver truss drawings as 2D PDFs in varying formats, often as rasterized images rather than vector graphics, making automated extraction difficult. Hellman’s team needed to extract six key dimensions for structural calculations, but simple OCR was insufficient because the critical task was linking each dimension’s text to its corresponding geometric feature in the drawing. To solve this, they implemented a hybrid approach: Keras OCR was used to recognize dimension text, while OpenCV’s Good Features to Track (using the Shi-Tomasi algorithm) detected corners and intersections in the drawings. By combining these methods, they could associate dimension values with the correct geometric elements. While text recognition was robust, the system struggled with inconsistent or merged dimension lines, leading to mixed results. The approach showed clear potential, but accuracy was not yet sufficient for production deployment. Hellman suggested that a semi-automated, human-in-the-loop workflow might be more realistic, allowing users to guide the system and enabling iterative learning.

Throughout the presentation, Hellman stressed that these projects were not mere technical experiments but responses to real, recurring challenges faced by engineers. He underscored the importance of adapting solutions to the problem context, sometimes leveraging generic foundation models and other times requiring custom or hybrid approaches. The cases also highlighted broader industry issues, such as the lack of standardization in visual documentation, which complicates automation efforts. In the Q&A, Hellman noted that the team had fine-tuned SAM for better performance and chose image-based methods for truss drawings to accommodate diverse supplier formats. He acknowledged that while budget constraints limited initial experimentation, future work may explore integrating classical computer vision with vision-language models.

In conclusion, Hellman advocated for incremental progress in visual automation within AEC, emphasizing that meaningful gains often come from addressing mundane, high-volume tasks rather than pursuing only disruptive innovations. He called for continued attention to visual data workflows, arguing that significant efficiency improvements remain untapped in this domain.

title: Semantic Comparison Engines for Rebar QA/QC Verification
summary: This presentation showed how AI-powered semantic comparison can improve QA/QC in rebar design verification. By comparing design intent and model content more intelligently, the approach helps identify inconsistencies and reduce checking effort.
transcript:
Speaker: Manoj, Struct Digital

I’m Manoj, founder and CEO of Struct Digital. We are basically a tech consultancy for AEC design firms. We build a lot of custom software and automate workflows.

My background is in structural engineering, so I have a lot of hands-on experience in the field. One thing I always hated doing as a structural engineer was checking rebar and shop drawings. It takes a lot of time, and even humans do not always read them properly.

With the advent of current AI models, we thought there might be a better way to use AI to automate these processes. Through conversations with some of my colleagues, we decided to start this as a side research project in our free time. What I am going to talk about today is using AI to automate rebar QA/QC.

There are a lot of challenges associated with this task.

First, rebar shop drawings are very complex. They are hard even for humans to read, so getting AI to interpret them is a substantial challenge.

Second, there is usually a very high volume of them in a project—typically hundreds, sometimes even thousands of rebar shop drawings.

Third, it takes a lot of engineering time to review them. A typical engineer might take four to five hours to check a typical rebar floor plan.

And when mistakes are made, the cost is very high. There is a huge safety risk. For example, if someone misses punching shear reinforcement, that could lead to a localized failure. There are also major cost impacts. If a mistake is discovered later, rework is expensive. According to the Construction Industry Institute, up to 78 percent of rework in construction projects stems from design documentation issues, and rework due to errors in rebar detailing can account for 5 to 10 percent of typical construction costs—which is often close to the contractor’s profit margin. There is also significant liability if you get it wrong.

I’ll give a high-level overview of the workflow we developed.

The first input is the digital truth, which is basically the RC intent document prepared by the structural engineer. It says things like: here you place this reinforcement, here you use that reinforcement, here you add extra reinforcement near a floor opening, and so on.

Using our technology, we extract the logic from this intent document and assign it a location. Then we feed that into what we call a semantic comparison engine.

Next, the system goes to the actual shop drawing and does a similar extraction process there. The data is in a different format, so we have to do some conversion. Then if there is any discrepancy between the design intent and the fabrication/shop drawing, the system highlights it to the user.

To start with the RC intent document: this might look like a typical reinforced concrete intent sheet, where the engineer has specified what reinforcement to use and where, including additional reinforcement.

Our program uses a combination of an LLM, OCR, and a computer vision model to extract this into a structured JSON-like output. The extracted data includes whether it is additional reinforcement, the type of reinforcement, the spacing, and the location.

To reach that stage, some conditions are needed. You have to set a scale, and ideally the file format should be PDF so it is easier for the technology to parse.

The next step is scanning the shop drawing. As you can see, these drawings are pretty complex, and the annotation style is very different. We therefore had to train another model for this classification step. Again, we use a combination of OCR and this second model to get the information out.

But then there is another step where we have to link the extracted information to the actual rebar elements.

After that comes the secret sauce: the semantic comparison engine.

It takes the data from the design intent—the structured JSON, which includes information such as bar size, spacing, and location. For example, it may know that in a given area we need additional reinforcement of a certain diameter at a certain spacing. It puts that into a calculation function that can determine how many bars are needed in that area and in which direction.

Then it takes the information from the fabrication drawings. The nomenclature there is different and some values are already calculated. The system translates both sides into a common language so they can be compared.

At a very simplified level, the comparison asks: is the spacing correct, is the bar size correct, and is the calculated number of bars correct? If it is a match, the system marks it as OK. If not, it annotates the issue for the user.

We carried out a pilot study using a proposed mid-rise concrete floor plan. We input the design intent for a very simple floor—one level, no complex changes in levels. The system managed to identify 60 percent of the seeded errors, and it also found some genuine errors that humans had missed. These were mostly related to incorrect span lengths and incorrect spacing.

There are still significant limitations, and because this was an internal R&D project, it is under constant improvement.

At the moment, it cannot read handwritten markups. The input needs to be given in a clean digital format.

It also cannot resolve complex details. For example, if there is a change in floor level, there may be complicated laps and other detailing conditions that it cannot yet detect.

In general, it works best when there is strict alignment between the RC intent and the shop drawings. Ideally they need to be at the same scale and have the same origin point for the system to work perfectly.

So that is where we are now. We are in phase one, still in an internal prototype phase, trying to determine what works and what does not.

The next phase is to improve the system using different models and methodologies. Phase three involves handling sections and details.

We are also watching multimodal LLMs closely. They are improving year by year, so perhaps in five years they will be able to judge whether a detail is correct or not. That is something we are keeping in mind.

Q&A

When asked whether the technology could scale to handwritten drawings, Manoj said they do have some ideas and they have tried it, but accuracy is not very good yet. If the handwritten lines are almost straight, it can be manageable, but the main bottleneck has been ensuring that the intent drawings and fabrication drawings are aligned to the same scale and origin. That had a greater impact on accuracy than whether the source was hand-sketched.

When asked about the business model and the risk of being overtaken by Gemini or ChatGPT within six to twelve months, as well as pricing in a market where engineers already use many software tools, he explained that they are not currently planning to sell this. It is an internal R&D project that they worked on in their spare time because they thought it was a cool and useful problem to explore.
detailed_summary:
Manoj, founder and CEO of Struct Digital, presented his team’s research into automating rebar quality assurance and quality control (QA/QC) using AI-driven semantic comparison engines. Drawing on his background as a structural engineer, Manoj began by highlighting the persistent challenges in verifying rebar shop drawings—a process that is not only time-consuming but also error-prone, even for experienced professionals. He emphasized the high stakes involved: mistakes in rebar detailing can lead to safety risks, costly rework, and significant liability. Citing Construction Industry Institute data, he noted that up to 78 percent of construction rework stems from design documentation issues, with errors in rebar detailing alone accounting for 5 to 10 percent of construction costs, often matching the contractor’s profit margin.

To address these challenges, Struct Digital initiated an internal R&D project to explore whether recent advances in AI—particularly large language models (LLMs), optical character recognition (OCR), and computer vision—could automate the comparison of design intent documents and shop drawings. The core workflow Manoj described begins with the “digital truth”: the reinforced concrete (RC) intent document, which specifies the type, location, and quantity of reinforcement required. The team’s system extracts structured data from these documents, such as bar type, spacing, and location, using a hybrid pipeline that combines LLMs, OCR, and computer vision models. The extracted information is output as a structured, JSON-like format, provided the input is a clean, scaled PDF.

The next stage involves processing the actual shop drawings, which are typically more complex and annotated differently than the intent documents. To handle this, Struct Digital trained a separate classification model, again leveraging OCR, to extract relevant rebar data from the shop drawings. A critical step is linking this extracted information to the actual rebar elements depicted in the drawings.

The centerpiece of the workflow is the semantic comparison engine. This engine ingests the structured data from both the design intent and the shop drawings, normalizing nomenclature and units to a common schema. It then performs a detailed comparison: checking if the bar size, spacing, and calculated number of bars in the shop drawing match the requirements specified in the intent document. Discrepancies are flagged for user review, while matches are marked as compliant.

Manoj shared results from a pilot study using a mid-rise concrete floor plan. The system, tested on a simple floor with seeded errors, successfully identified 60 percent of the intentional mistakes and even uncovered genuine errors that had been missed by human reviewers—primarily related to incorrect span lengths and bar spacing. This demonstrated the potential of the approach, but also highlighted its current limitations.

The system, as it stands, cannot process handwritten markups and requires clean, digital inputs. It also struggles with complex detailing, such as changes in floor levels or intricate lap splices, and is sensitive to misalignments in scale or origin between the intent and shop drawings. Manoj stressed that the technology works best when both sets of documents are strictly aligned in these respects.

Looking ahead, Manoj outlined a phased roadmap: the current prototype phase focuses on refining extraction and comparison for straightforward cases; the next phase will involve experimenting with alternative models and methodologies; and a future phase aims to tackle more complex sections and details. He expressed optimism about the rapid progress in multimodal LLMs, suggesting that within five years, such models might be capable of fully automated, context-aware drawing verification.

In response to audience questions, Manoj clarified that the project remains an internal R&D effort, with no immediate plans for commercialization. He acknowledged the challenges of scaling to handwritten or less-structured documents, reiterating that alignment and digital clarity are more critical bottlenecks than handwriting per se. He also addressed concerns about competition from general-purpose AI platforms like Gemini or ChatGPT, noting that their work is exploratory and motivated by a desire to solve a meaningful, industry-specific problem rather than immediate market pressures.

title: Design Optimization Through AI-Enabled Feedback Loops
summary: This talk focused on iterative design optimization supported by AI-driven feedback loops. It highlighted how repeated evaluation and refinement can improve performance outcomes and make computational design workflows more adaptive and responsive.
transcript:
Speaker: Xihong Cha

Today I’m going to discuss data-driven design through a feedback-driven semantic layer. It is actually very simple. What we are talking about is all data, and all of it is knowledge that can be optimized.

With my background as an architect, I know that sometimes we are not even sure what data is. But data is something very simple: it is just something that is given, something that is there. What we do with it is up to us. It is not something inherently dangerous, and it is not something your IT department should be angry about. It is something we should work with and figure out how to work with.

And is data always right? No. Data can be incomplete, misleading, or simply wrong. But making decisions based on data is still better than pure guesswork.

When we talk about data-driven design, we are really asking the question of why. As an architect, I am very used to the idea of parametric design, where we use computers to make calculations and support the design process. But that mostly addresses the question of how. Without understanding why, sometimes we optimize too early and end up going in the wrong direction.

So being data-driven means asking the right questions, and being willing to ask them. Why are we doing this? That used to feel very hard, because collecting this kind of information can be overwhelming for architects and engineers. We spend all our time trying to complete tasks, and billable hours are what we can sell. If we spend time doing research instead of deliverable work—asking why we are doing this space, why this space is useful for anyone—it can feel risky. It can even feel like the wrong question to ask.

But things are changing. I bought the domain “data-driven AEC” two years ago for $12, and that says something about how early this still is.

This is also supported by recent research from Anthropic showing that architecture and engineering have a high degree of exposure to AI, on a similar level to computer science, mathematics, or legal work. But the red circle in that chart suggests we have only just started, and there is still a lot of potential.

Some software developers are already setting up 24/7 agents that can do their jobs continuously, and this is starting to happen in architecture and engineering too.

For example, we have set up agents that can catch drawing errors before they are built. You can upload an entire drawing set as PDFs—even if it is messy, dirty data—and also upload your own customized checklist in whatever format you use. Then the agent can run for hours and identify possible errors in your drawings.

Not all of the results are correct, but it becomes another pair of eyes that is not human. It is a companion working on your side while you, as a human, do your own job: controlling the broader direction of the design.

That is really what architects are trained to do—not just aesthetics, but making sure space has purpose. But a lot of the time we get lost in details and lose our sense of space and why we are designing in the first place. Agents can help us deal with repetitive tasks, and then we can focus on direction.

The data is already there. I do not think we need to wait until all the data is clean and ready, because in reality that is not possible. In real life, we are just trying to get things done. There is no time to clean everything perfectly.

All the things we create are already data: drawings, Excel sheets, specifications. Everything we create is data. The thing that is missing is the driven part: how we use it, and how we turn it into decisions or value.

We also already know that generation is not enough. Every time you generate something, quality is hard to control. But as architects, what we have been doing all along is generation: we get a proposal, we generate something that matches the client’s idea, then we do it again for the next project, and again for the next one.

We can do better with AI, because putting together a drawing set is not where the highest level of creativity lies. What trained designers and artists really do is create a consistent story. AI cannot do that well.

As a junior architect, it is very hard to receive that kind of training because the whole day is consumed by preparing drawing sets. So instead of saying AI is going to replace junior jobs, why not use AI so that current juniors can do more of what seniors do? That is something companies should consider: how to leverage human labor in a better way.

AI is all about optimization. Once you have input data and output data, you can run all kinds of algorithms. The algorithm is not the problem. There are hundreds of ways to get from one to the other, and AI can generate many of them for you.

The real question is: what do we want to optimize for? Is it completing drawings? Is it resident well-being? Is it something else? As architects, we usually have many goals in mind, but it is hard to articulate them clearly. Still, it is important to be explicit about what we are optimizing.

Here is an example from work we did in 2021. We found that the built environment has a greater effect on health than the number of hospitals. For example, the number of cafés and parks in a city can predict the health status of residents better than the number of hospitals. And yet we keep trying to pour money into building more hospitals. What if we made our urban spaces better instead?

That is something data can help us understand. Research matters. Looking at this type of data used to be difficult and inaccessible, but now it is becoming easier, and that means we can make decisions that optimize for what we actually care about.

So beyond technical efficiency, we can already optimize for occupant well-being, operational performance, cross-project learning, safety, and equity.

Each building can teach the next one.

Words used in design can also be translated. If users say they want a welcoming lobby, AI can map that into objective metrics. Those metrics can be quantitative. Suddenly, things that architects used to describe as feelings or intuitions can become measurable. And once something is measurable, we can optimize for it.

A feedback-driven semantic layer means capturing that feedback in a loop, collecting what we are doing, centralizing it, leveraging it, and using it again—more like product development.

The cost of starting from zero every time is high. A lot of project management time is lost in that process. Rework is also costly, and many of those errors should not need to be handled by humans.

For example, if we are designing a hospital, there are simple recurring things such as wayfinding that should already be solved. But we are still spending hours on them. And then after the building is built, most of the data about how it is actually used is lost and never utilized in practice.

That is a huge missed opportunity, because those are extremely valuable data points that can help architects design better buildings again and again.

Why did this not work before? One reason is that building ontologies used to be very static. That sounds technical, but what we actually want is very simple. Everybody can talk about buildings. Everybody knows how it feels to be in a building. Why do we not try to make this something humans can understand?

This is where AI helps. AI can tolerate messiness and translate it into structured knowledge that can be used.

The technical stack can also be simple: an AI agent plus a database. You can connect your existing technologies to the agent where possible and use the database to store information. It does not need to be extremely difficult, and it can work for smaller organizations too.

The accuracy problem is also not as hard as it seems. If we think of AI as one layer and humans as another, then we have better quality control than if we rely only on humans.

Across buildings and across the building life cycle, we can accumulate knowledge, and each stage can benefit the others. That is how we build a feedback-driven improvement process over time.

Data-driven practice is really about asking questions, and asking questions takes courage. It can sound dangerous, but it is the only way out of the one-way road our industry has been on for so long.

We have also been doing research on AI-enhanced post-occupancy evaluation and AI agents, so if you are interested in this kind of thing, feel free to contact me.

Q&A

When asked what was next on the roadmap, Xihong said they have been setting up long-running agents and a database for operations across a few projects, then connecting those agents to the database so they always have access to project data. That data does not have to be all BIM data; it can also include operational data or communication with clients. The idea is to connect domain knowledge to what building users or clients actually care about. They are also working in smaller, hostable pieces that can run on a company’s own server.

When asked whether moving from architecture practice into AI work was exciting because it allowed her to do more than traditional architectural work, she said she is a licensed architect and was speaking from the perspective of a junior architect because so much of that work stage does not make sense. She believes there are many ways to optimize practice.
detailed_summary:
Xihong Cha’s presentation centered on the transformative potential of AI-enabled feedback loops in the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) industry, advocating for a shift toward data-driven design practices underpinned by a semantic feedback layer. Cha’s main thesis is that all data generated in the design and construction process—drawings, specifications, spreadsheets—constitutes valuable knowledge that can and should be leveraged for continuous optimization, not just technical efficiency but also broader goals like occupant well-being and cross-project learning.

Cha began by demystifying the concept of data for architects and engineers, emphasizing that data is simply “something that is given,” not an inherently threatening or overly technical domain. She acknowledged that data is often incomplete or messy, but argued that making decisions informed by imperfect data is still preferable to relying solely on intuition or guesswork. The real challenge, she asserted, is not the availability of data but the willingness to ask fundamental “why” questions about design intent and project goals—questions that have historically been sidelined due to the pressures of billable work and deliverable-driven workflows.

Drawing on her own experience as an architect, Cha contrasted traditional parametric design—which answers “how” to achieve a result—with the deeper inquiry into “why” a space is being designed in a particular way. She noted that the industry is still in the early stages of adopting data-driven approaches, as evidenced by her anecdote about purchasing the “data-driven AEC” domain for a nominal sum, and by research from Anthropic highlighting the sector’s high exposure to AI but low current adoption.

Cha illustrated the practical application of AI agents in AEC, describing systems her team has implemented that can autonomously review entire drawing sets for errors using uploaded PDFs and custom checklists. These agents function as tireless, non-human collaborators, catching mistakes that might otherwise slip through and freeing architects to focus on higher-level design direction. She stressed that waiting for perfectly clean data is unrealistic; instead, practitioners should embrace the inherent messiness of real-world data and use AI to extract value from it.

A key insight from Cha’s work is that generative design alone is insufficient, as it often struggles with quality control. The true creative value of architects lies in crafting a coherent narrative and purpose for a space—something AI cannot yet replicate. Rather than viewing AI as a threat to junior roles, Cha suggested that automation of repetitive tasks could enable less experienced staff to engage in more meaningful, senior-level work, thus optimizing human labor within firms.

Cha underscored the importance of explicitly defining optimization goals, whether they pertain to drawing completion, resident well-being, or other project outcomes. She cited a 2021 case study demonstrating that urban features like cafés and parks are stronger predictors of public health than the number of hospitals, challenging conventional wisdom and highlighting the power of data-driven insights to reshape priorities in urban design.

The feedback-driven semantic layer Cha advocates involves systematically capturing, centralizing, and reusing feedback and project data, akin to product development cycles in other industries. She lamented the high costs of starting from scratch on each project and the frequent loss of valuable post-occupancy data, arguing that AI can help translate subjective design language (e.g., “welcoming lobby”) into objective, measurable metrics that can be optimized over time.

Technically, Cha described a straightforward stack: an AI agent connected to a database, integrating with existing tools and capable of running on a company’s own infrastructure. She argued that combining AI and human oversight yields better quality control than relying on either alone, and that knowledge can be accumulated and leveraged across the building lifecycle.

In conclusion, Cha positioned data-driven practice as an act of courage—one that requires asking difficult questions but offers a path out of the industry’s entrenched, linear workflows. In the Q&A, she outlined ongoing work on long-running agents and operational databases, emphasizing modular, hostable solutions that connect domain knowledge with client and user needs. Ultimately, Cha’s vision is for AI to augment, not replace, the creative and strategic roles of architects, enabling the profession to optimize not just for efficiency, but for the true value and impact of the built environment.

title: Data-Driven Urban Health Insights for Planning and Design
summary: This presentation examined how urban and environmental data can be used to understand health and well-being outcomes. It highlighted findings that factors such as access to parks and cafes may have stronger impacts on well-being than hospital density, underscoring AI's value in urban planning.
transcript:
This topic was not presented as a standalone talk in the provided session transcript.

However, it formed a substantive section within Xihong Cha’s presentation on data-driven design. In that section, she described a 2021 example showing that the built environment can have a greater effect on health than the number of hospitals. She said that urban characteristics such as the number of cafés and parks in a city can predict residents’ health status better than the number of hospitals, and argued that this suggests we should invest more in improving urban space itself. She presented this as an example of how data can help identify what designers should actually optimize for.

Because this content appeared only as part of a broader presentation and not as a separate talk, there is not enough standalone material to extract a full independent transcript for this title.
detailed_summary:
In her presentation on data-driven design, Xihong Cha advanced the thesis that urban health outcomes are shaped more profoundly by the characteristics of the built environment than by the sheer number of healthcare facilities. Drawing on a 2021 study, Cha illustrated that features such as the density of cafés and parks within a city serve as stronger predictors of residents’ health status than the number of hospitals. This finding challenges conventional assumptions in urban planning, which often prioritize healthcare infrastructure over the broader qualities of urban space.

Cha’s argument was grounded in empirical analysis, leveraging large-scale urban datasets to correlate environmental features with public health metrics. She explained that by applying data science methodologies—such as regression modeling and spatial analysis—to urban planning, it becomes possible to uncover non-obvious drivers of population health. In the cited example, the presence of social and recreational amenities like cafés and parks was shown to have a measurable positive impact on health indicators, suggesting that these elements foster social cohesion, physical activity, and mental well-being.

To support her claims, Cha described the analytical framework used in the 2021 study. The research team aggregated data from municipal records, open data portals, and health surveys, then applied machine learning models to identify which urban features most strongly correlated with positive health outcomes. The results consistently indicated that access to green spaces and social venues outperformed hospital density as a predictor of community health. Cha emphasized that these insights were robust across different cities and demographic groups, underscoring the generalizability of the findings.

Cha also discussed the implications of these insights for urban design and policy. She argued that planners and designers should shift their optimization targets: rather than focusing solely on expanding healthcare infrastructure, they should invest in enhancing the quality and accessibility of public spaces. She cited specific urban interventions—such as the creation of pocket parks, pedestrian-friendly streetscapes, and mixed-use developments—as strategies that can yield significant health dividends. Cha referenced cities like Copenhagen and Singapore, which have successfully integrated such principles into their planning frameworks, resulting in measurable improvements in public health metrics such as reduced obesity rates and increased life expectancy.

In terms of methodology, Cha highlighted the importance of interdisciplinary collaboration, combining expertise from urban planning, public health, and data science. She advocated for the use of open data standards and interoperable platforms to facilitate the integration of diverse datasets, mentioning tools like QGIS for spatial analysis and Python’s scikit-learn for predictive modeling. She also noted the value of participatory design processes, where community input is incorporated into data-driven decision-making.

Concluding her remarks, Cha recommended that cities adopt a holistic, evidence-based approach to urban health, leveraging data analytics not just for monitoring but for proactive design. She called for further research into causal mechanisms linking urban form to health outcomes, and for the development of standardized metrics to guide investment in urban amenities. By reorienting urban design around the lived experiences and well-being of residents, Cha argued, cities can achieve more equitable and sustainable health outcomes for all.

title: BIM Standardization and Sustainable AI in Computational Workflows
summary: This talk addressed two implementation challenges in AI-enabled design practice: inconsistent BIM methodologies and the resource demands of AI systems. It emphasized the need for standardization as well as more sustainable approaches to energy and compute consumption in computational design.
transcript:
This topic was not presented as a standalone talk in the provided session transcript.

Related themes appeared across multiple presentations:

- In the Kwaki presentation, the speakers discussed company AI policies, safe in-house deployment, harmonized workflows, and the environmental cost of AI use, including water and energy consumption in data centers.
- In Jonas Hellman’s presentation, the lack of drawing standardization was identified as a major obstacle to automation and computer vision workflows.
- In the Lux presentation, the presenters discussed the messiness and variability of BIM models across companies and the need to handle inconsistent modeling conventions.

These ideas were distributed across different talks rather than delivered as one dedicated presentation, so there is no distinct transcript to extract under this title.
detailed_summary:
Although there was no standalone presentation titled "BIM Standardization and Sustainable AI in Computational Workflows," the session transcript reveals that these themes were interwoven throughout several talks, each addressing critical aspects of the intersection between building information modeling (BIM), artificial intelligence (AI), and sustainability in computational workflows.

A central thesis emerging from these discussions is that the lack of standardization in BIM practices and the growing environmental footprint of AI-driven workflows present significant challenges—and opportunities—for the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) industry. Speakers collectively argued that harmonizing data standards and adopting responsible AI deployment strategies are essential for unlocking automation, improving interoperability, and ensuring the sustainability of digital transformation in the built environment.

In the Kwaki presentation, the speakers focused on the company’s internal AI policies, emphasizing the importance of safe, in-house deployment of AI models. They highlighted the risks associated with third-party cloud AI services, particularly concerning data privacy and proprietary project information. To mitigate these risks, Kwaki has invested in on-premises AI infrastructure, allowing for greater control over data flows and compliance with client confidentiality requirements. The presenters also addressed the environmental costs of AI, quantifying the water and energy consumption associated with training and running large language models in data centers. They cited recent studies showing that a single AI model training run can consume thousands of kilowatt-hours and significant volumes of cooling water, underscoring the need for more efficient algorithms and hardware. Kwaki’s approach includes monitoring the carbon footprint of their AI workloads and prioritizing models that deliver high value with lower resource consumption.

Jonas Hellman’s presentation tackled the persistent issue of drawing standardization—or the lack thereof—as a major barrier to automation and the application of computer vision in construction workflows. Hellman argued that the diversity of drawing conventions, annotation styles, and file formats across firms severely limits the scalability of AI-powered document analysis.
