He provided examples where computer vision models failed to extract meaningful information from architectural plans due to inconsistent layer naming, ambiguous symbols, and non-standardized metadata. Hellman advocated for the adoption of open standards such as IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) and ISO 19650, which provide structured schemas for BIM data exchange. He also recommended the use of automated validation tools, like Solibri and BIMCollab, to enforce compliance with these standards at the project outset, thereby reducing downstream errors and manual rework.

The Lux presentation further illustrated the challenges posed by the variability and messiness of BIM models in real-world practice. The presenters described case studies where federated BIM models from different subcontractors exhibited divergent modeling conventions, object hierarchies, and parameter naming schemes. This heterogeneity complicated tasks such as clash detection, quantity takeoff, and automated scheduling. Lux’s team demonstrated their in-house normalization pipeline, which leverages open-source libraries like IfcOpenShell and proprietary rule-based scripts to harmonize BIM data before ingestion into their project management platform. They reported measurable improvements in data quality and workflow efficiency, citing reductions in manual data cleaning time and increased reliability of downstream analytics.

Across these presentations, a consensus emerged on the need for industry-wide collaboration to establish and enforce BIM standards, as well as to develop sustainable AI practices. The speakers recommended that firms invest in training, participate in standards bodies, and adopt transparent metrics for tracking the environmental impact of their computational workflows. Looking ahead, they called for the development of lightweight AI models tailored for AEC applications, greater use of edge computing to minimize data transfer, and the integration of sustainability criteria into procurement and project delivery processes. The session concluded with an acknowledgment that while technical challenges remain, the convergence of BIM standardization and sustainable AI offers a pathway to more efficient, resilient, and environmentally responsible construction practices.

title: AECOS: An AI Operating System for AEC Firms
summary: Guido Maciocci introduced AECOS, an AI operating system designed specifically for architecture, engineering, and construction firms. He emphasized connectors, ingestion pipelines, intelligent document processing, knowledge bases, orchestration, automation, and human-in-the-loop review. A key theme was the value of vendor-neutral platforms that integrate native AEC data to build compounding intelligence across projects.
transcript:
Guido Machiacci: Over the last few years, we’ve been building AI systems for some of the world’s largest AEC enterprises. Along the way, we learned something fundamental about how AI should work in this industry, and we also think most innovation teams and enterprises are getting it wrong. What I want to share today is how to build effective AI in AEC through platform engineering, demonstrated through a real deployment of our platform in construction safety.

My name is Guido Machiacci. I’m the founder and director of AEC Foundry, where we partner with AEC firms across architecture, engineering, construction, and manufacturing to transform their expertise into intelligent systems.

Let’s talk about what’s happening in AI in our industry right now. What most of the market offers today is vendor-locked AI that works inside one platform’s data boundary but is blind to everything else. There are generic models and generic platforms that don’t understand AEC-native data like drawings, BIM models, schedules, specifications, and so on. Then there are point solutions. They don’t scale, they don’t compound, and they don’t integrate with each other.

What we think it should be is an open and vendor-neutral platform where you have access to any technology and model, so you can keep up with the pace of rapid technological change. We think it should be able to natively read and understand the data we care about as an industry, not just text, images, and spreadsheets. Most importantly, we think the best way to develop AI is through a compounding platform model where every use case you build and deploy builds on shared knowledge and extends the intelligence layer for the next use case, and the one after that.

For us, this is what it looks like in practice. We call our platform AECOS. It’s an AI operating system for the AEC industry. The important thing here is the structure. On the left, you have your systems of record—ACC, Procore, SharePoint. This is where all of your project data and institutional knowledge live. On the right, you have systems of action. This is where intelligence and actions flow back out of the platform into downstream systems and back into your systems of record. In the middle is the platform itself, a neutral layer that sits between your systems. It doesn’t try to replace anything. It tries to connect everything and make it more intelligent.

Starting from the bottom and moving up, we have connectors and ingestion pipelines that keep the platform data in sync with the data living in your systems. We have dedicated intelligent document-processing pipelines specifically built for AEC-native data, able to extract, parse, and transform unstructured data from drawings, models, contracts, specifications, and so on into AI-readable knowledge. We store that knowledge in knowledge bases.

These knowledge bases become accessible through our orchestration and automation layer to agents and pipelines. We can configure those through workflows and tasks to perform actions manually, semi-autonomously, or fully autonomously, always with human-in-the-loop review. Then we have evaluation and continuous improvement—feedback loops driven by subject matter experts that improve the system over time. We also have dedicated interfaces that adapt dynamically to user intent and go well beyond simple chat interfaces.

All of this sits on the most important layer of all: the reactive event foundation. This is responsible for tracking every single action and event that happens on the platform—every agent response, every human question, every interaction, every review. In a regulated industry like AEC, this is what builds transparency and trust in AI. We’re always able to go back and see where and why a decision was made.

To ground this in a real deployment, let’s talk about why we focused on safety. We’re currently working with a top-10 global EPC in oil and gas operating in the Middle East in a megaproject environment. They manage between 5,000 and 20,000 workers on a site at any one time. Health and safety was one of the sharpest pain points for the organization and a particularly good entry point for us to prove what a platform-first approach can do. Health and safety is document-heavy, regulation-driven, and almost entirely manual in terms of workflows, so it creates a very good opportunity.

The construction industry remains the most hazardous private-sector industry in the world. In the U.S. alone, about 1,000 people died in one year, and the annual cost of injuries and fatalities is around $12 billion. The problem is that the safety data needed to prevent those injuries and fatalities is there; it’s just inaccessible. It’s scattered across many different systems, media, and formats, and unavailable when and where it’s needed.

The result is that safety management is typically a job of record-keeping, and often poor record-keeping at that, for compliance purposes—not to actually improve safety. About 50% or more of a safety management team’s time goes into manual data reconciliation rather than safety. That’s one of the things we were trying to change with this client.

Our approach is a three-layered one, and these layers map directly back to the platform infrastructure I showed before. We have ingestion pipelines built to ingest certain types of documents. We have knowledge bases where we collect and organize safety knowledge like OSHA standards, the company’s hazard taxonomies, standard operating procedures, and risk-assessment frameworks. Finally, we have orchestration—pipelines and agents that can work autonomously or through human interaction, leveraging these pipelines and knowledge bases to perform safety-management tasks and workflows end to end.

Where the platform meets the field, we chose three specific use cases to start with, and this is still a live project for us. The first is START forms—safety task analysis and risk-reduction talk forms. These are basically daily stand-ups that teams have on-site before they start work, to identify potential safety hazards they might encounter. They record these on paper forms. The second is safety observations: notes, photographs, videos, and text collected on-site, typically by safety supervisors who notice when something is wrong or someone is performing unsafe behavior. The third is safety analytics. At the moment, the management team only has access to static dashboards built on lagging indicators and data they don’t really trust. The dashboards exist, but nobody reads them, so nobody is actually interacting with the data they do manage to collect and structure.

In the live demo, we start in a system of record—in this case, Google Drive, though it could be anything. Imagine uploading a START form. A safety manager has completed the meeting, the team has filled out the START form, and he uploads it to Google Drive. At the scale of a megaproject, this happens with thousands of forms per day. They’re collected from many teams and have to be put together in one system. The client currently has a team whose only job for the duration of the project is to open scanned images of these forms and translate them into Excel.

Our platform is connected to Google Drive as a connector. As soon as the START form drops into Google Drive, it triggers a document-extraction pipeline that extracts all of the form data into a structured schema defined by the user. Then we have human-in-the-loop review, which does two things: low-confidence extractions are surfaced to human experts for review, and when they review and correct the output, the corrected output goes back into the system to improve the next extraction. So it’s a self-improving system through human review.

That’s just one case of document extraction on the same platform. Imagine thousands upon thousands of these things being processed. That manual team no longer needs to exist; they can just use the platform.

The next use case is safety observations. From this point on, imagine the user journey of a safety manager, though the platform is used by several personas with different entry points. We run a safety observation task. A task is simply a predefined workflow or objective for AI to complete. In this case, we upload a picture and some text about what we noticed on site. The agent takes the image and the text, references the knowledge base, standard operating procedures, and potential risks, then creates a structured safety observation within the interface itself.

From just an image, a little text, and location-based information, we get a comprehensive safety observation structured with risk scoring, hazards, and language that are consistent. Typically, if you ask two safety managers to classify the same issue, they’ll do it differently. The system standardizes that across the board so the data you get is high quality.

Next, we ask the agent to look in the OSHA knowledge base for any OSHA guidelines related to this observation. The observation was about scaffolding, though we don’t have to specify that explicitly. We get back the OSHA-related regulations and safety guidelines for safe scaffolding activities. All of this is citation-backed, with references directly to the individual texts within the documents, so you can trust but verify the information you’re getting from the language model.

Then we do something different: we ask what cameras the platform is hooked up to. We connected the platform to live streams from cameras on site. I couldn’t show the actual site streams for obvious reasons, but the data is there. The live streams feed into another intelligent processing pipeline that uses computer vision to detect people in certain locations and determine whether they’re wearing the right protective equipment for that location. All of those detections happen live.

As that happens, the knowledge base and database fill up with information. We can then ask for aggregated data on PPE noncompliance over the last three months since the start of the year from those cameras. The platform shows all aggregated PPE noncompliance detections made by the computer vision models and displays them through an interactive visualization in the same context. We don’t need to switch tools or interfaces.

We can then ask for a chart of safety observation types for the last week, Monday to Friday, drawing from all the submitted photographs, text, audio, and video notes from the field. The agent analyzes the data, extracts what it needs, and visualizes it in an interactive chart. Still in the same interaction, we ask the agent to help us understand the data: what insights can we gain from the PPE noncompliance, observations, START forms, and everything else collected over the last week or three months? It responds with an interpretation of the data, highlighting trends, anomalies, and anything we may have missed.

We can trace any data point back to its origin—the source document, source video, even the timestamp on the video. The system can also recommend follow-up actions it can perform for us.

This is where it starts to get interesting. So far we’ve dealt with data that has already been ingested. Now we’re getting into generative content. Based on the data we’ve been discussing—scaffolding and PPE compliance—we ask the platform to create a toolbox talk. If you’re not familiar with that, it’s a type of safety training that safety teams give to job-site teams to inform them about better work practices based on the data they’ve collected. If the data shows an increase in PPE noncompliance in Zone B, then the goal is to go to Zone B and give a contextualized, tailored training to the people working there.

The platform generates that training asset, and we have the opportunity to send it by email to someone else. Now it’s going into a downstream system of action, still within the same platform. It’s not a prepackaged generic training; it’s contextualized and highly relevant to the current data.

Finally, I showed another task: creating a safe work method statement or a safety risk assessment. We’ve seen the data, analyzed it, and generated a toolbox talk, but now we want to create a structured safe work method statement for scaffolding works. We describe the activity—this might come from a work breakdown structure submitted by a subcontractor—and we want to assess whether that contractor’s planned work aligns with the company’s own safe operating procedures.

The system generates a full safe work method statement. It includes the hazards and control measures for that activity. A human can approve, flag, or reject it. That is part of the human-in-the-loop review process: whatever is approved becomes a positive example for the AI to learn from, whatever is flagged becomes something that needs further correction, and whatever is rejected becomes a negative example so the AI doesn’t repeat the same mistake.

Once the document is finished, we can ask the AI to close the loop and put it back into Google Drive. Because it’s connected to the systems of record, it can interact with them. We didn’t tell it the folder name; it inferred that a safe work method statement should probably go into the SWMS folder. It exports the PDF and uploads it back into the system of record, closing the loop from system of record to platform to system of action.

What does this automation deliver? If we treated these as individual point solutions—START forms, safety observations, maybe some analytics on an existing database—we might still see strong results without a platform approach. You could probably get 10x reporting volume, a 2x increase in near-miss capture, cycle time dropping from two days to three minutes, and about a 95% reduction in manual work through automation. Those numbers are impressive, and leadership loves them, but that is not where the real value is.

The value comes from what the platform unlocks. On the left you have fragmented data. The platform ingests it, extracts it, enriches it, classifies it, stores it, indexes it so AI can read it, and then generate new content from it. Everything is traceable, and every step can be evaluated. The result is not just automation, but a compounding effect where you’re building institutional memory, enabling cross-system intelligence, predictive analytics, trend analysis, and deep auditability when something goes wrong.

You don’t get that from point solutions, because point solutions permanently disconnect the left side from the right. You need a platform approach to connect those things and create compounding value over time.

I’ve been talking about safety for this entire presentation, but the platform itself doesn’t care about the domain. It cares about knowledge, data, and orchestration. We can extend the same infrastructure to multiple domains, especially document-heavy domains and manual workflows—submittals and RFIs, comparing specs against requirements, quality and inspection workflows involving field data and reporting, procurement and logistics optimization, and progress reporting. Different domains, same underlying platform.

That is exactly how we work. We embed within a client’s organization, identify the highest-pain-point, lowest-effort, highest-ROI use case we can build quickly, and then build multiple use cases on top of that, where every use case compounds on the one before. Safety is the entry point for this client, but the platform is horizontal, which means it can expand into multiple domains.

In closing, I’d like to leave you with the most important lesson we’ve learned: AI doesn’t create durable value through better tools. It creates durable value when you own the intelligence those tools produce—when you own the data, the knowledge, and the infrastructure. When you use a vendor’s black-box AI, the intelligence compounds inside their platform, not yours. When you buy disconnected point solutions, intelligence never compounds because the data behind them never connects.

We believe the best approach is platform-first, where you build something that compounds over time and becomes an organizational asset, not just another tool. In five years, probably every major AEC firm in the world will be running some kind of intelligence layer across its operations. The question is whether you want that intelligence to be your asset or somebody else’s.

Q&A

Audience question: Beyond traditional checklists, how does your AI agent achieve proactive HSE risk detection and autonomous decision-making?

Guido Machiacci: Mainly through the live video stream, object detection, and people detection. We can determine whether someone is entering a prohibited zone, whether they’re wearing the right equipment, whether there are too many people in a certain area, whether a vehicle is too close to a person. Those kinds of real-time detections are where the proactive, leading-indicator game starts to be played. At the same time, because you’re centralizing the data and creating not just separate databases but a unified knowledge base, the insights that emerge from interacting with and exploring the data are already orders of magnitude beyond what typical safety teams can access today.

Audience question: How do you approach GDPR issues when you are taking footage from the site?

Guido Machiacci: We don’t host the platform ourselves. We deploy the platform on the client’s cloud. In this case, the client is operating in the Middle East, and we’re running this on a dedicated cloud instance in their region. The data is protected, and for every client we can do the same. We built this platform not as a subscription product where you can just sign up. There is no sign-up and no self-service trial. We work with clients to deploy the infrastructure so they can fully own it. We maintain it and build on top of it, but they own the infrastructure.
detailed_summary:
Guido Machiacci’s presentation centers on a transformative thesis: the AEC (Architecture, Engineering, and Construction) industry’s approach to AI is fundamentally flawed, with most solutions being vendor-locked, siloed, and incapable of understanding the sector’s unique data. He argues that true, durable value from AI in AEC comes not from isolated tools or point solutions, but from a platform-first approach—one that is open, vendor-neutral, and designed to compound intelligence across use cases and domains. Machiacci introduces AECOS, the AI Operating System developed by his company, AEC Foundry, as a practical realization of this vision, and demonstrates its impact through a real-world deployment in construction safety management.

Machiacci critiques the prevailing landscape of AI in AEC, where most offerings are either generic models that fail to comprehend AEC-native data (such as drawings, BIM models, and specifications) or point solutions that do not scale or integrate. He contends that these approaches trap intelligence within proprietary boundaries, preventing organizations from building institutional knowledge or leveraging cross-system insights. Instead, he advocates for an open, vendor-neutral platform that natively understands AEC data and enables every new use case to build upon the knowledge and capabilities of the last, creating a compounding effect.

The AECOS platform embodies this philosophy through a layered architecture. At the foundation are connectors and ingestion pipelines that synchronize data from systems of record—such as Autodesk Construction Cloud (ACC), Procore, and SharePoint—into the platform. Specialized document-processing pipelines extract and transform unstructured AEC data (drawings, models, contracts, specifications) into structured, AI-readable knowledge, which is then stored in knowledge bases. These knowledge bases are accessible to orchestration and automation layers, where agents and workflows can execute tasks manually, semi-autonomously, or fully autonomously, always with human-in-the-loop review for quality and compliance. A continuous improvement loop, driven by expert feedback, ensures the system evolves over time. Crucially, the platform’s reactive event foundation logs every action and decision, providing the transparency and auditability essential in regulated industries.

To illustrate AECOS in action, Machiacci details its deployment with a top-10 global EPC (Engineering, Procurement, and Construction) firm in the oil and gas sector, managing megaprojects with up to 20,000 workers on site. Safety management, a document-heavy and regulation-driven domain, was chosen as the initial focus due to its acute pain points and potential for impact. The platform was configured to address three core use cases: processing START forms (daily safety task analysis and risk-reduction talks), capturing and structuring safety observations (notes, photos, videos from the field), and delivering actionable safety analytics.

In the live demo, Machiacci shows how AECOS integrates with Google Drive to automatically ingest and extract data from thousands of START forms daily, replacing manual data entry teams. Human-in-the-loop review ensures extraction accuracy and feeds corrections back into the system, enabling self-improvement. For safety observations, the platform allows users to upload images and text, which are then contextualized against the knowledge base (including OSHA standards and company-specific taxonomies) to produce structured, consistent, and citation-backed safety records. The system’s integration with live site cameras enables real-time computer vision analytics, such as PPE compliance detection, with results aggregated and visualized interactively. All data points are fully traceable to their sources, supporting deep auditability.

The platform’s generative capabilities are also highlighted: based on observed trends (e.g., increased PPE noncompliance in a specific zone), AECOS can generate contextualized toolbox talks and safe work method statements, which can be reviewed, approved, and automatically routed back into systems of record. This end-to-end orchestration—from data ingestion to action—demonstrates the platform’s ability to automate workflows, standardize data, and generate new organizational knowledge.

Machiacci emphasizes that while impressive automation metrics (such as 10x reporting volume, 2x near-miss capture, and 95% reduction in manual work) are achievable even with point solutions, the true value of AECOS lies in its compounding effect: fragmented data is unified, enriched, and made actionable, enabling predictive analytics, trend analysis, and institutional memory that grows with each use case. This platform-first approach is domain-agnostic and extensible to other document-heavy workflows, such as submittals, RFIs, quality inspections, procurement, and progress reporting.

In conclusion, Machiacci asserts that durable AI value in AEC comes from owning the intelligence layer—data, knowledge, and infrastructure—not from relying on black-box vendor solutions or disconnected tools. AECOS is deployed on client-owned infrastructure, ensuring data sovereignty and compliance (including GDPR considerations), and is tailored through close partnership rather than as a generic SaaS product. Machiacci predicts that within five years, every major AEC firm will operate such an intelligence layer, and urges organizations to ensure that this asset is their own, not locked within a vendor’s ecosystem.

title: Predicting Construction Errors with AI from Enterprise Project Data
summary: Roxi Mkhani presented research on using AI to predict construction errors proactively. Her work drew on 12 years of data spanning more than 20 business units and 1 million records to develop error classification systems aimed at reducing inefficiencies caused by rework. The presentation focused on turning historical operational data into actionable predictive insights.
transcript:
Dr. Roxy Makhani: Good afternoon, everyone. I hope you enjoyed your lunch, and I’ll try to keep this engaging despite the post-lunch timing.

My background is actually a PhD in physical chemistry. When I realized the world was pivoting toward AI, I felt an urgency to move in that direction, so I earned a second master’s degree in data science. That eventually brought me into construction.

We have a diverse team on this project: three academics from Kingston University and a very strong industrial partner, Galliford Try. We are mainly London-based. The project I’m leading is one of the more innovative efforts trying to apply cutting-edge technology in industry. That is why Innovate UK, through the UK government, is supporting work that does not remain in academia but creates real industrial impact. My role has been to act as a liaison between academia and industry.

In the next few minutes, I’ll cover the project, the research background, a literature review, then data, modeling, results, impact, and future work.

If you know research projects, they are mainly divided into two categories: primary and secondary research. This project is hybrid. The secondary part means we reviewed the literature: what has already been done, what the results were, and how to structure that knowledge. The primary part means we also developed our own contribution and new ideas. So we first looked at what had been done so far, where the gaps were, and how we could contribute.

This started with issues that resonated with the morning sessions: construction data is fragmented, inconsistent, poorly classified, and lacks standardized frameworks. How can organizations apply AI models effectively? How can they build solutions instead of simply buying vendor products, especially when fragmented data, silos, and weak standards are everywhere?

Before this research, we published another paper through the Association of Construction Management in the UK, which I presented in Dundee. In that work, I tried to navigate different classification systems and understand how construction errors could be classified.

The core idea comes from the fact that rework remains one of the most prominent inefficiencies in construction. I wanted to classify errors in a way that would eventually allow us to apply AI models to predict them. But along the way, I realized there was no good classification system for these errors. Without a good classification system, we cannot apply AI models effectively.

So I came up with the idea of a hybrid model that starts top-down. It combines domain expertise—through interviews and surveys—with classification dimensions such as lifecycle, root cause, and error type. Then there is a data-driven feedback loop, which I call data enrichment, where the data itself contributes to the classification system. Together, those two streams allow us to classify the data into a stronger taxonomy of error. That full paper is available in the ACM presentation.

That was only the beginning. After building those classification systems, we looked at where the industry is in terms of rework and whether the industry is proactive or mostly reactive. We found that both academia and industry recognize the importance of construction errors that lead to rework. The previous presentation mentioned that 5% of construction costs are lost to rework, but the Get It Right Initiative in the UK reported in 2016 that 21% of construction cost is lost due to rework.

That makes it important to have models that can be applied to rework-related data. But when we reviewed the literature, we found that most studies treated rework as an outcome. That is a reactive approach. They were not trying to predict in advance why it happens or how likely it is to occur in future projects. So we shifted the focus: instead of modeling rework as an outcome in terms of cost, quality, or time, we tried to model the errors that lead to rework. That is the key distinction from earlier research.

The literature used several models, including artificial neural networks, AdaBoost, J48, and other relatively sophisticated methods. In our own work, though, we found that such sophisticated models were not necessary.

We started with data. I collected more than 12 years of industrial data from Galliford Try. No one had really been using it to predict errors or to inform future projects proactively. The dataset covered more than 20 business units across the UK because Galliford Try is a large organization. It contained about 1 million data records, mainly quality incident records, but also a combination of numeric, free-text, and categorical data, plus more than 10 project characteristics such as project scope, contract value, and duration, and more than 15 error types.

I tried to collect as much data as possible before deciding what might be useful. I didn’t want to prematurely discard anything. The data then had to be processed, and that was a very long journey. Two years of my project were spent on data preprocessing. It was frustrating. First there was data collection, which took about six months. Then data cleaning revealed fragmentation, silos, inconsistency, naming problems, and lack of standardization. I had to do all the work necessary to make the data ready for machine learning.

Galliford Try had recently launched a sophisticated platform based on Microsoft Fabric. If you think of Microsoft Fabric as a factory, you can think of raw data as raw material and the end result as the finished product. The data does not need to move to other vendor platforms. Everything is kept in one unified platform. You have OneLake for structured and unstructured data storage, built-in data governance, and Fabric notebooks, so there is no need to use external tools like Anaconda or Jupyter for machine learning. Through APIs, these components connect to Power BI, where predictions can be presented to nontechnical end users such as design managers and technical managers in very simple bar charts.

It was a steep learning curve for me to understand how Fabric works and how to use machine learning models and coding inside Synapse and Fabric. But after applying several machine learning models to the data, I found that construction data is not as sophisticated as people often suggest. Coming from a science background, I believe science is about simplicity, not complexity. Many people try to impress others with complex models like ANN and CNN, but do we really need them?

I found that logistic regression, a very simple model, works well for construction data—at least for the kind of structured, text-based data we were dealing with, not images. It is interpretable, simple, and the accuracy was high. Once I found that a simple model worked, I didn’t feel any need to move to something more sophisticated. I also used other models like decision trees and random forests, but logistic regression performed well enough, so I stayed with it.

What we actually predicted was work package type. I did not know what a work package was at first either, because I do not come from a construction background. A work package is essentially the package of work needed to fix an error that occurred in construction. We were able to predict what kinds of work packages would likely be needed when a new project comes in—what kinds of errors might occur in the future.

In the presentation, I used only coded labels based on Uniclass for confidentiality reasons. I did not disclose the actual meanings of those codes, but if you are interested, you can look up the descriptions from the standard. I also used ROC curves, because accuracy alone is not enough. ROC curves show true positive versus false positive performance and help demonstrate how well the model distinguishes categories. If the curve is near the top-left corner, that indicates a strong classifier; if it is close to the diagonal, that indicates a random model. The more the curve bends toward the top-left, the better.

There were several benefits for Galliford Try, both operational and technical. The main value is moving from a reactive to a proactive position by providing site-specific, data-driven insight. If you are going to build a project, the model can indicate what kinds of errors are likely to occur and which work packages may present the highest risk—decorating, MEP, lighting, or others. The Uniclass codes have their own classifications that help identify the higher-risk categories.

Some of this future work has already started. I drafted this presentation two months ago, and since then I have already trialed the project in one business unit. They were very interested, and we are now evolving the tool further. There is still more to do in terms of wider system stabilization and automation across the whole landscape, but the work is moving ahead.

Q&A

Audience question: Because you did this research embedded in a very specific company, and that company has its own building types and specializations, how transferable is the approach? You said you spent two years on data preparation for one company. If you wanted to transfer this to another company, is there any knowledge transfer between models that would shorten that time?

Dr. Roxy Makhani: I can definitely transfer the skills I learned. The company-specific data itself is confidential, so that cannot simply be moved. But I developed a confidential internal tool to map and standardize very inconsistent data in a very quick way—within seconds. So beyond the predictive tool itself, I also developed another tool for Galliford Try that standardizes those inconsistent datasets efficiently. If Galliford Try decides they want to commercialize that tool, then I would be very happy to work on the intellectual property and patent side, but that depends on whether the company allows it.
detailed_summary:
Dr. Roxy Makhani’s presentation addressed the persistent challenge of predicting construction errors using artificial intelligence, leveraging enterprise-scale project data to move the industry from a reactive to a proactive stance on rework. Her central thesis argued that the key to effective AI-driven prediction in construction lies not in the sophistication of machine learning models, but in the rigorous preparation and classification of data—an insight drawn from both academic research and hands-on industrial collaboration.

Makhani’s project, a partnership between Kingston University and Galliford Try, with support from Innovate UK, exemplifies the bridge between academic innovation and industrial application. She began by situating her work within the broader context of construction data challenges: fragmentation, inconsistency, poor classification, and the lack of standardized frameworks. These issues, she noted, have historically impeded the effective deployment of AI in construction, as organizations struggle to build solutions tailored to their own data rather than relying on generic vendor products.

The research adopted a hybrid methodology, combining a comprehensive literature review with the development of novel classification systems and predictive models. Makhani’s earlier work, presented at the Association of Construction Management, highlighted the absence of robust error classification systems in construction. Recognizing that rework—responsible for up to 21% of construction costs according to the Get It Right Initiative—was typically modeled only as an outcome, she shifted focus to predicting the specific errors that precipitate rework. This proactive approach distinguishes her work from prior studies, which often used advanced models like artificial neural networks (ANN), AdaBoost, and J48, but remained reactive in nature.

Central to her methodology was the creation of a hybrid classification model. This model integrated domain expertise—gathered through interviews and surveys—with classification dimensions such as lifecycle stage, root cause, and error type. Crucially, it incorporated a data-driven feedback loop, or “data enrichment,” allowing the evolving dataset itself to refine and strengthen the taxonomy of errors. This iterative process resulted in a more robust and actionable classification system, detailed in her ACM paper.

The project’s empirical foundation was a vast dataset: over 12 years of quality incident records from Galliford Try, encompassing more than 1 million records across 20 business units. The data included numeric, categorical, and free-text fields, as well as project characteristics like scope, contract value, and duration, and over 15 error types. Makhani emphasized the labor-intensive nature of data preprocessing, which consumed two years of the project and involved resolving fragmentation, silos, inconsistent naming, and lack of standardization.

A significant technological enabler was Galliford Try’s adoption of Microsoft Fabric, a unified data platform. Fabric’s OneLake provided structured and unstructured data storage, built-in governance, and integrated machine learning notebooks, eliminating the need for external tools like Anaconda or Jupyter. Through APIs, predictions could be surfaced in Power BI dashboards, making insights accessible to nontechnical stakeholders such as design and technical managers.

Despite the availability of complex machine learning models, Makhani found that logistic regression—a simple, interpretable model—performed exceptionally well on the structured, text-based construction data. She validated model performance using ROC curves, which demonstrated strong classification ability. The primary prediction task was to anticipate the type of work package (as defined by Uniclass codes) likely required to address future errors in new projects, thus enabling Galliford Try to identify high-risk categories and allocate resources proactively.

The impact for Galliford Try was substantial: the transition from reactive to proactive error management, site-specific risk insights, and operational efficiencies. The tool has already been trialed in one business unit, with plans for broader automation and system stabilization underway.

In response to questions about transferability, Makhani clarified that while the specific data remains confidential, the methodologies and internal tools developed for rapid data standardization are portable. She has created a proprietary tool capable of mapping and standardizing inconsistent datasets in seconds, which could potentially be commercialized pending company approval.

In conclusion, Makhani’s work demonstrates that the real innovation in AI for construction lies in data readiness and classification, not model complexity. Her approach offers a replicable blueprint for other organizations seeking to harness AI for predictive error management, provided they invest in the foundational work of data preparation and taxonomy development.

title: Automated Takeoffs for Residential Construction Estimating
summary: Tommy Whitehead showcased an AI tool that automates takeoffs in residential construction. The system uses symbol detection, pattern recognition, and automated quantity counting to reduce estimating bottlenecks. His presentation highlighted practical gains in speed and workflow efficiency for construction estimation.
transcript:
Tommy Whitehead: Good afternoon, everybody. I’m still a little jet-lagged. I’m from Florida in the United States—where Mickey Mouse lives.

I’m the president and founder of TomCo Solutions, a regional residential construction firm. That is a little different from some of the firms being discussed here, but we all share the same mindset: how can we make AI better for the construction industry? One central theme I keep hearing is the industry’s resistance to adopting new technologies, or the argument that AI cannot do everything. My response is simple: if I can get half my work done instantly, I would much rather have 50% less work than 100% of it.

We formed AI Builder internally to create products that would help us estimate much faster. Then we started talking with contractor and architect colleagues, and they kept asking, “When can I buy that?” So we decided to move toward creating a minimum viable product for external use.

A little about me: I’m a Titan 100 member, a Tampa Bay Business and Wealth Apogee Award winner, a finalist for Philanthropist of the Year, an Amazon bestselling author, a podcast host, and a political appointee on a small business council in my city. So no, I do not sleep.

Trust is one of the biggest issues I see in the construction industry. I hear all the time, “A computer can’t do my job. It can’t swing a hammer.” That’s true—for now. But I saw some really interesting technology in Las Vegas last year that may challenge that assumption soon.

In the United States, 40% of the construction industry is retiring within the next five years, and we do not have enough people to fill those jobs. That means I either need to be on a job site swinging a hammer or at my desk reviewing plans. So what can I automate right now? Right now, I can start automating takeoffs.

If you have ever done an estimate, you know how tedious they are. They’re repetitive. You might do 100 estimates and only land three jobs. It is a bottleneck everywhere. In our own office, we realized the problem was not leads and it was not closing sales. The bottleneck was in the time between those things, when we had to measure every fixture, every linear foot, every square foot of drywall, and every component in a house to make sure we bid it correctly.

Takeoffs are really just counting and quantifying drawings. The gap we still see in the market is the ability not only to quantify the drawings but also to immediately generate an estimate so we can determine cost right away.

The amount of time involved in estimating is huge. You have to interpret plans. You have to count every symbol. If there are 300 light fixtures in a shopping mall, you have to count all 300. You have to make sure revisions are reflected in the estimate. We have all had the experience of having a full estimate ready to go and then getting revisions back from the architect because the client wants to change something. Then you have to go back and double-check everything again. We also spend a lot of time double-checking quantities, and many people still rely heavily on spreadsheets. Some are using software like Bluebeam, which is great, but in the residential sector the tech adoption curve is still lagging.

The chain of consequences starts with the takeoff, then the estimate, then procurement, then scheduling, then change orders, then profit. More change orders usually mean more problems and more cost. Clients often say, “You just want to issue a change order because you want to make more money.” In reality, change orders cost me money. I do not want them. I would rather a client stick with the exact same project we agreed to six months ago. But this whole chain is time-consuming. Imagine if a click of a button could update those quantities and costs immediately.

There are also other challenges: complex drawings, highly detailed plans, frequent revisions, labor shortages, and the lack of intuitive construction software. I am genuinely shocked by how unintuitive construction software still is, and that is part of why conferences like this matter. Construction software often feels stuck in the 1980s or 1990s. It never really updated to think like tomorrow. It is going to take our generation of professionals to fix that.

We also deal with fragmented systems and misaligned stakeholder input. Bob does not know what Jan knows, and Jan does not know what Jake is doing, because there is no centralized place and no centralized protocol. AI does not have that problem. It talks to itself.

There is also a perception issue. In the U.S., many leaders in construction are in their 60s and 70s and have run profitable firms for decades. If you are 65 and close to retirement, do you want to learn a new skill or change everything? Usually not. I understand that. When I am 65, if you try to tell me something radically new, I may react the same way.

There are training curves for new technology, perceived cost barriers, and fear around job security. But why? We cannot fill estimator positions now, and many existing estimators are going to retire. AI is not going to steal those jobs. It is going to help us fill a capability gap.

The public imagination jumps straight to the idea that AI will build skyscrapers and everyone will be fired while robots construct buildings from the ground up. We are not in the Star Trek or Star Wars era of construction. We will not be there in our lifetime. But we can reduce time spent on repetitive work.

What AI does well is see patterns we do not. It can detect symbols in plans, locate the legend, read the plan notes, and scan for corresponding visual markers. It can identify repetitive patterns across trades and drawings. The symbol for an electrical outlet is pretty similar everywhere, even if details vary by voltage or amperage. AI can automate quantity counts, process revisions more quickly, and help with workflow.

Here is how our model works. We upload a PDF or CAD file. Computer vision visually detects features. Natural language processing helps identify the legend, notes, and item types, then counts quantities. A human reviewer is still necessary. That is one of the biggest misconceptions: people think AI is just going to take over and nobody will work. No. We are still going to do our jobs, but we are going to review and validate the output instead of spending time on the most menial counting tasks.

The result is increased precision. AI does not get tired. Imagine running cost estimation across 50 projects overnight. You click a button and let it work while you sleep. It reduces time and avoids rework.

The takeoff speed can be increased significantly. Human oversight is still essential, and I cannot stress that enough. Standardized components are especially easy for the system to process.

In the first-generation version of our technology, the workflow starts by uploading pricing data. Then you upload your construction plans, in this example using a PDF. In the residential sector, contractors often do not get the CAD files directly; the architect or engineer may control those. The system then automatically analyzes the blueprint.

In the demo example, it was using a simple residential electrical floor plan. It compared the detected plan elements to a predefined cost database uploaded based on the client specification. It automatically detected the items and generated a bill of materials and pricing. The example numbers shown were obviously not real pricing, but the concept is that it produces a first-pass estimate. The first-generation version could also export to Excel, which can then be uploaded into most estimating platforms.

Our newer version goes further. It reaches into AutoCAD and Revit, pulls out every independently identifiable element, sums everything, and compares it against a cost database. Imagine an architect being asked by a client, “How much is this going to cost?” Most architects say, “I don’t know—ask the GC.” But imagine if, for an extra fee, the architect could provide an approximate cost estimate with the scope of work already summarized for the GC to start reviewing.

That would be useful not just for contractors but also for architects. It creates a new revenue stream without requiring much extra work. It also makes it easier for general contractors to bid more accurately. For example, maybe the national average window cost is $700, but in Florida it is $1,000 because the windows need to be impact-rated. That changes design decisions.

Our goal is to get this to full-stack capability across all MEP systems and beyond. Every time you see something on a plan, we want to be able to detect it and analyze it. We also want to incorporate more spatial analysis, and we want automated revision detection so the system can compare the original file and revised files and say, for example, “You reconfigured the bathroom, but your quantity counts are still the same.”

Beyond takeoffs, we are also developing AI chatbots for natural-language inquiries. Someone might ask, “How much does a kitchen cost?” and get a preliminary range. Or an elderly homeowner might say, “My sink broke. What do I do?” and get automated assistance. We are working on automated lead qualification, phone triage, and prompt systems that write their own prompts to program Zapier interfaces and AI chatbots.

There is always concern that AI is scary. But think about how scary the automobile was to the horse and buggy. Think about the internet compared with the newspaper industry. I recently read about Montgomery Ward, which became one of the largest companies in the U.S. because it pioneered mailing catalogs to households, but it failed to adopt the internet early enough and that contributed to its collapse.

By being in this room and at this conference, we are the early adopters. We are not going to be the Montgomery Wards that refuse to adopt the future. We are taking the risk now to move to the next step.

AI augments us; it does not replace us. We do not have androids walking around doing construction tasks for us. But AI can boost output, eliminate repetitive tasks, and support scaling across multiple industries.

Q&A

Audience question: When you talk about trust and using AI as an intern for quantities and takeoffs, construction projects might have hundreds of quantities. How does your AI identify which quantities should be double-checked? If we double-check everything, we make the tool useless, but if the AI tells us which ones are risky, AI can also be wrong. How do you handle that?

Tommy Whitehead: We already have ways of handling this in other contexts. Think about retail: does a store inventory every product on the shelf every day? No. They spot-check. If a supervisor audits a cash drawer, they do not audit 50 people at once; they pick one. So every company will need internal mechanisms for spot-checking.

Some quantities also break common sense. If a 2,000-square-foot office building appears to have 5,000 toilets, you know something is wrong. That is where human knowledge matters. The same applies to cost: most of us know rough cost-per-square-foot ranges for certain project types. If the estimate is far outside the expected range, that tells us something is likely wrong. I tested another tool once that estimated a renovation at about half its actual likely cost. My professional judgment told me immediately that the whole result had to be wrong.

So yes, it is still spot-checking—but the system will improve. And eventually there will be bots checking the bots, and then maybe we all get to go home.
detailed_summary:
Tommy Whitehead, president and founder of TomCo Solutions, opened his presentation by challenging the construction industry’s skepticism toward artificial intelligence, particularly in the realm of residential construction estimating. He argued that while AI may not yet be able to physically build homes, its ability to automate half of the repetitive, time-consuming work involved in estimating is a transformative opportunity. Whitehead’s central thesis is that automating takeoffs—the process of quantifying and counting elements from construction drawings—can dramatically reduce bottlenecks in the estimating process, freeing up valuable human resources and enabling firms to operate more efficiently in the face of labor shortages and an aging workforce.

Whitehead described the genesis of TomCo’s AI Builder, an internally developed tool designed to accelerate the estimating process. Initially created for in-house use, the tool quickly attracted interest from peers, prompting the company to develop a minimum viable product for broader industry adoption. He emphasized that trust remains a significant barrier to technology adoption in construction, with many professionals doubting that computers can replicate the nuanced judgment required for tasks like takeoffs. However, Whitehead pointed to demographic trends—specifically, the impending retirement of 40% of the U.S. construction workforce within five years—as evidence that automation is not a threat to jobs, but a necessary solution to a growing capability gap.

The speaker detailed the pain points of manual takeoffs: the tediousness of counting every fixture or square foot, the need to update estimates after every plan revision, and the heavy reliance on spreadsheets or legacy tools like Bluebeam. He noted that while some commercial sectors have adopted more advanced software, residential construction lags behind, in part due to unintuitive user interfaces and fragmented workflows. The lack of centralized protocols means that knowledge is siloed among stakeholders, leading to inefficiencies and errors—problems that AI, with its ability to process and communicate across systems, is well-suited to address.

Whitehead explained the technical underpinnings of AI Builder. The system ingests PDF or CAD files, using computer vision to visually detect plan features and natural language processing (NLP) to interpret legends, notes, and item types. It then counts quantities and matches them to a cost database, generating a bill of materials and a preliminary estimate. Human oversight remains essential; the AI handles the repetitive counting, while professionals review and validate the output, ensuring accuracy and catching outliers. For example, if the system detects an implausible number of toilets in a small building, human judgment intervenes.

He demonstrated the tool’s workflow: users upload pricing data and construction plans, and the system automatically analyzes the blueprints. In a demo with a residential electrical floor plan, the AI detected plan elements, matched them to predefined costs, and exported the results to Excel for integration with existing estimating platforms. The latest version extends these capabilities by extracting elements directly from AutoCAD and Revit files, enabling more granular analysis and supporting architects in providing preliminary cost estimates—potentially creating new revenue streams and improving collaboration with general contractors.

Whitehead outlined future directions, including expanding the system’s scope to cover all MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) systems, enhancing spatial analysis, and automating revision detection so the software can flag changes between plan versions. He also described ongoing work on AI chatbots for natural-language inquiries, automated lead qualification, and integration with workflow automation tools like Zapier.

In conclusion, Whitehead positioned AI as an augmentative force rather than a replacement for human expertise. He invoked historical examples—such as the rise and fall of Montgomery Ward—to underscore the risks of resisting technological change. By adopting AI-driven tools now, he argued, construction professionals can avoid obsolescence, boost productivity, and focus on higher-value tasks. During the Q&A, he addressed concerns about trust and error detection, advocating for spot-checking and common-sense validation, much like inventory audits in retail. Ultimately, Whitehead’s vision is one where AI handles the drudgery, humans provide oversight, and the industry evolves to meet the challenges of tomorrow.

title: Lightweight AI-Assisted Bricklaying Robots for Human-Machine Collaboration
summary: Pierpaolo Ruttico demonstrated lightweight bricklaying robots equipped with ergonomic end-effectors and AI vision systems. The robots were presented as collaborative tools to address labor shortages, improve inclusivity, and reduce musculoskeletal disorders in construction work. The talk emphasized human-machine cooperation rather than full automation.
transcript:
Professor Pier Paolo Ruttico: Thank you for the invitation. This conference is amazing. I think this is my fifth time attending.

Today I’m speaking as a researcher. I’m going to talk about things that do not fully work yet, so I’m not here to sell anything. I want to share the journey we are on. I founded Index Lab in 2010. We work in computational design and robotics, and now AI is entering the picture. We noticed that AI can enable many new application layers, so we are trying to apply AI to construction robotics.

The project I’m discussing today was done together with my team. We are just nine people, but we collaborate with companies because we want to make real things. We do want to go to market eventually.

In this era, we are facing a very urgent problem: labor shortage. The literature shows that many companies are simply looking for bricklayers and cannot find them. Buildings are not getting built. One reason is that bricks are heavy—around 15 to 20 kilograms per block in some cases. You need workers who are physically strong enough, but even then, that does not solve the problem. Musculoskeletal disorders are a major issue, especially in the EU.

The industry is moving forward, and there are already good solutions on the market. One example is Hadrian X, and another is work being done with Wienerberger, which produces bricks globally. These systems are trying to build houses out of brick. But in practice, they are better suited to one-story buildings with large square footage and mostly straight walls, rather than small, highly customized houses.

This idea has existed for a long time. We can find literature and prototype images from the 1990s. The concept was already there, but adoption has been difficult.

Why has it been difficult? One reason is the nature of the machines. If you look at systems like MULE, which helps lift heavy bricks, or newer applications like Walter from a Wienerberger startup that places bricks two at a time, you see they are very heavy machines. They require cranes to place them on the construction site. Some weigh more than a ton. If you think about a residential building with a two-kilonewton slab, you cannot safely place such a machine at height.

This limits adoption in the wider market, especially in Europe, where projects are often small and highly customized. There is also a risk that machine constraints and construction logistics start dictating design, instead of the other way around. As architects, we do not want that. We want to remain free in design. We already saw this happen with prefab in the 1960s and 1970s, when tower cranes and modular systems strongly constrained what architects could build.

So we need other solutions. In our lab, we started by observing actual construction sites. These sites often have very small doors and narrow spaces. Workers operate side by side. Big robots simply do not fit well. Construction environments are also highly variable. Every project is different, so flexibility is essential.

At Index Lab in Lecco, we have a range of robots—industrial robots and collaborative robots. We work with companies to access the latest machines and apply them in research. We combine augmented reality systems, computational design, and robotics into algorithmic, customized workflows. We try to embed design constraints directly into fabrication in a streamlined way.

Of course, problems arise. When you are dealing with heavy rubble or blocks, you have safety issues. You cannot work side by side with a robot unless you rewrite the regulations, or perhaps equip people with active-feedback garments, which we are investigating, or use software to slow down the robot when a human gets too close. We are exploring all of that, but it has to come together with regulations, practical feasibility, and robust software.

Back in 2018, we started experimenting with supervised learning and reinforcement learning. We created games in Unity and asked whether an agent could, in principle, learn to build a wall by itself. At that time, generative AI was not yet part of the mainstream conversation, but reinforcement learning was very popular in academia.

We had fun with it, and we saw that an agent can learn to build a wall faster than a human in a simplified environment because it can optimize trajectories and execution. We also played with interactions between human gestures and robotics. We were experimenting broadly.

The next step was to transfer this into a system. That led to our first prototype, BRICS. We integrated several technologies together. But it was still very primitive, and almost everything did not work well enough. For example, in that setup the robot moved next to a pallet, scanned a QR code, and used that QR code as a calibration reference to pick blocks precisely. That only works in an ideal environment, not on a real construction site. You will never find a pallet on site where all the elements are perfectly aligned and shaped.

The rover itself was also sophisticated. It could navigate autonomously, and autonomous rovers are increasingly available. But the platform was too large for a real construction site. It could not go through doors. It was about 1.2 meters wide, and that alone made it impractical.

So we were not solving the actual problem. We had to put the human back into the loop and make things simpler. At first we were too ambitious. We then scaled everything down to a much simpler system. The revised system picks a block from a pallet and brings it close to where it needs to be placed. Obstacle avoidance is handled manually. It is basically a wheelbarrow-like platform that you can easily transport and unload.

That was exactly the point we wanted to reach: first, to make the work easier for the bricklayer, and second, to make calibration and setup faster. In this version, we introduced AI into the vision system. It uses machine vision to scan where the brick is, pick it, and bring it close to the placement point. The human does the dexterity part—the part humans are good at. That is because actual construction conditions do not allow the robot to perform perfect autonomous final placement in a robust way.

This evolution is called Brickary, which is also the title of the project. With it, we reached our main goals. The system can pass through an 80-centimeter door, it can be loaded into a van, and no crane is required. That makes it relatively inexpensive and lowers the barrier to adoption, especially for very small companies.

Everything is built with off-the-shelf components. That was intentional. The aim was not to invent exotic hardware but to integrate readily available hardware into a practical proposal that could realistically be mass-produced.

The robot itself is collaborative, which is important. A collaborative robot is much lighter than an industrial robot and stops when it touches a human. It is also relatively affordable. The total weight of the system is only about 150 kilograms, so it can be used on many types of slabs. It can reach heights up to 4 meters because of the column it is mounted on, and it is quite stable. It actually works.

Most importantly, it supports human-machine collaboration in a way that can reduce musculoskeletal disorders. It can also make bricklaying more inclusive, potentially allowing more women to enter the profession. The goal is not to replace humans but to help them. The end effector has an ergonomic geometry that we designed and manufactured, and it includes a depth-sensing camera.

In terms of software architecture, we rely on available open-source platforms and models, such as Meta’s Segment Anything for isolating the clay blocks from the background and NVIDIA FoundationPose to estimate the six degrees of freedom of each visible block and define the calibration plane for the robot. We embed everything into microservices with Docker so the system can scale.

The pipeline works like this: first, the robot approaches the pallet and scans it in a spiral pattern, almost like a human visually inspecting it. Segment Anything isolates the blocks from the background in real time. FoundationPose estimates the pose of each visible block. Then there is still a computational-design component, using Grasshopper, which simulates the pick-and-place process row by row and block by block. Finally, there is the collaboration component, where human input and output are integrated into the workflow to stop the robot and lock the Z-axis when needed.

We divided the wall-building process into manageable segments to make execution easier. Of course, the blocks need to be organized appropriately for this. We ran a series of tests to measure cycle times. We repeatedly placed single bricks under the same conditions to understand performance. The main bottlenecks were clear. One was moving the rover manually. Each time it has to be repositioned for the next block, that takes time. That could be removed if we scale up to a motorized autonomous rover, but of course that increases cost. The other bottleneck was scanning. Scanning still takes time because we were using a normal PC. That could certainly be improved with more computing power, eventually enabling near-real-time performance.

Still, the current system is already quite performative. The core idea is that AI, especially in machine vision, enables a new kind of profession: a new-generation bricklayer working together with robotic assistance. We believe that can open new opportunities in construction.

To close, I showed a roadmap for the future. We see many technologies emerging, including humanoid systems, which we are also investigating. There is still a lot of room for improvement and a lot of innovation ahead.

Q&A

Question from moderator: In the images, the blocks always seemed to have the same shape and size. Do you see these robots adapting to changes in materials in the future?

Professor Pier Paolo Ruttico: Absolutely. There is a lot of technological evolution happening. Bricks will likely become more stratified and may include finishes already applied. In the future, we will probably have more complex components that arrive already finished and possibly in different customized shapes. There is also 3D printing entering the picture. So blocks and wall components could become increasingly customized, and I see strong potential for integrating those technologies.
detailed_summary:
Professor Pier Paolo Ruttico’s presentation centered on the development and promise of lightweight, AI-assisted bricklaying robots designed for effective human-machine collaboration in the construction industry. He began by framing the urgent context: a severe labor shortage in construction, particularly in bricklaying, exacerbated by the physical demands of handling heavy bricks and the prevalence of musculoskeletal disorders among workers. Ruttico argued that while existing robotic solutions—such as Hadrian X and systems developed with Wienerberger—have made progress, they remain largely suited to large-scale, repetitive projects with straight walls, and are ill-adapted to the small, highly customized, and spatially constrained construction sites common in Europe.

Ruttico traced the historical challenges of robotic bricklaying, noting that the concept has existed since the 1990s but has struggled with adoption due to the sheer size and weight of available machines. He cited examples like MULE and Walter, which, despite their technical sophistication, require cranes for placement and are too heavy for typical residential slabs. This, he argued, risks a scenario where construction logistics and machine constraints dictate architectural design, echoing the limitations imposed by prefab systems in the mid-20th century—a situation architects are keen to avoid.

To address these limitations, Ruttico’s Index Lab adopted a ground-up approach, beginning with direct observation of real construction sites. They found that the variability and tight spaces of these environments rendered large robots impractical. Instead, the lab focused on integrating computational design, augmented reality, and robotics into flexible, algorithmic workflows that could embed design constraints directly into fabrication. Safety and regulatory concerns were paramount, especially when considering human-robot cohabitation on site. The team explored solutions such as active-feedback garments for workers and adaptive robot speed controls, but emphasized that regulatory and practical feasibility must evolve alongside technical innovation.

The lab’s early experiments in 2018 leveraged supervised and reinforcement learning within Unity simulations, demonstrating that AI agents could, in principle, learn to build walls more efficiently than humans in simplified environments. These explorations included human-robot interaction studies, but initial prototypes—such as the BRICS system—proved too ambitious and impractical for real-world deployment. The BRICS rover, for example, was too large to fit through standard doors and relied on idealized calibration methods (e.g., QR codes on perfectly aligned pallets) that did not translate to actual construction sites.

Learning from these setbacks, Ruttico’s team pivoted to a simpler, more pragmatic solution: a lightweight, wheelbarrow-like robot that assists rather than replaces the bricklayer. This system, named Brickary, uses AI-driven machine vision to locate and pick bricks, bringing them close to the placement point, while leaving the final, dexterous placement to the human worker. This approach not only accelerates setup and calibration but also supports ergonomic and inclusive work practices, potentially opening the profession to a broader demographic.

Brickary is built entirely from off-the-shelf components, prioritizing affordability, portability, and ease of adoption. The robot weighs only 150 kilograms, can pass through 80-centimeter doors, and reach heights up to four meters. Its collaborative design ensures safety, as it stops upon contact with a human, and its ergonomic end effector includes a depth-sensing camera. The software stack leverages open-source tools: Meta’s Segment Anything for real-time block segmentation, NVIDIA’s FoundationPose for six-degree-of-freedom pose estimation, and Grasshopper for computational design and simulation. The system architecture is containerized with Docker, enabling scalability and modularity.

In practice, the robot scans pallets in a spiral pattern, isolates and estimates the pose of each block, and simulates pick-and-place operations row by row. Human input is integrated to manage obstacle avoidance and precise placement. Performance tests revealed that manual rover repositioning and scanning speed were the main bottlenecks, both of which could be improved with further automation and increased computing power.

Ruttico concluded that AI-powered, collaborative robots like Brickary represent a new paradigm for construction, enabling a new generation of bricklayers to work more safely and efficiently. He highlighted ongoing research into humanoid systems and anticipated future integration with increasingly customized and stratified building components, including those produced via 3D printing. The overarching vision is one of continued innovation, with AI and robotics augmenting—not replacing—human skill and creativity in construction.

title: Hybrid Human-Technology AI Teams in Construction
summary: Karoliina Alatalo explored the development of hybrid teams combining humans, AI, and robotics in construction workflows. She presented a roadmap spanning short-term, medium-term, long-term, and extra-long-term goals for adoption. The talk framed AI integration as an organizational and operational transformation, not just a technical deployment.
transcript:
Carolina Attalla: Hello. My name is Carolina Attalla. I come from AINS Group, and although I am an architect by training, I work as a project engineer in both development and project work.

Today I’m going to talk about our research and development project, High Five—its full title is “Meaningful Industrial Work in Hybrid Human-Technology-AI Teams.” The project includes partners such as Konecranes and Elomatic, among others. It focuses on meaningful industrial work, but more specifically we are piloting future working methods in which AR tools and AI assistants become an essential part of the project management team.

Our research questions include what this means for efficiency, teamwork, and management practices—in other words, how new forms of work will develop. We are also asking how these hybrid teams would actually function, with a special focus on construction sites and offices, because those are still quite separate worlds today.

Technological development is moving so quickly that it really forces us to rethink these things. We cannot just stay still.

I assume most people here know what an AI agent is. I’ll move from that to multi-agent systems and then to hybrid teams, where humans become part of that structure.

We found that “hybrid team” did not really have a clear definition in our context, so we created one. A hybrid team is a multifaceted workgroup combining human collaboration with autonomous AI systems and technologies such as AR and robotics. It offers scalable team structures, because AI agents can call on many more AI agents. It enables seamless collaboration regardless of location, real-time data collection, problem solving, and decision-making. A team of this kind can also operate independently in some situations.

An AI agent is not just one more software program you use. It can be more like a human colleague. Are you working alongside an AI agent on one task? Are you giving tasks to multiple AI agents? Or is an AI agent giving tasks to you? Those are all possible structures.

And usually, the AI agent you see at the surface has more AI agents behind it, with one orchestrating the others.

But we also have to remember the construction site. AI agents are in the virtual world, while construction sites are in the physical world. That is where robots become important.

We also considered today’s organizational roles, which are probably familiar to everyone. Then we asked what is missing. We have heard repeatedly that AI needs data. Right now, at AINS Group, I do not think we have anyone whose role is only to handle data. But in the future, we will need someone to collect data, standardize it, correct it, maintain it, convert it into machine-readable form, and integrate it into our processes. That is a lot of work.

We may need new roles for maintenance, coordination, bringing data into our workflows, and teaching people how to use these tools. We may also need AI solution maintenance roles and AI developer roles.

Examples of what AI agents could do include preparing offers, handling data, analyzing data, training people, and scheduling calendars. Examples of robots on site include systems for measuring, drawing markings on the construction site, or carrying out logistics tasks.

Roles will change, but in different ways depending on whether someone is in a junior or expert role.

Junior roles today are often given assisting and repetitive tasks, and we all know AI is especially good at those. So junior roles may shift toward AI solutions, technical integration, and understanding why AI may produce incorrect answers—in other words, understanding the mechanics behind AI. That is a technical shift.

Expert roles, on the other hand, are already more focused on demanding projects and specialized solutions. Their work may shift more toward human interaction—understanding what the client wants, deciding what AI solution is appropriate, and working in a more consultative way. So the change is different at different levels, but it is happening across the board.

We also looked at this change from the perspective of project engineers, project managers, and site supervisors.

A project engineer today may be led by a project manager and may work with several AI agents that help with documentation or data gathering. In that case, the project engineer is using AI agents as tools or assistants.

The project manager, however, is already leading humans and may in the future also be leading not two or three but perhaps hundreds or even thousands of AI agents. That is a different type of management challenge. Leading AI agents is not the same as leading humans. At the same time, project managers will also be using AI agents in their own work.

Then there is the site supervisor, who in the future may be working directly with robots on the construction site. We need to think carefully about how that relationship works.

We also considered team and organizational structures. The traditional structure is a project director with support people and various experts under them, such as project managers. Maybe we are now moving toward a stage where each human simply gets an AI agent and becomes more efficient. But that is really just an add-on.
