The more worrying scenario people imagine is that an AI agent will replace someone’s job entirely. But maybe it is more accurate to say that AI will replace a task from each expert’s role, rather than replacing the whole person. That makes the future slightly less frightening.

Still, these are just add-ons to structures originally designed for humans leading humans. Should we rethink the structure itself? These organizations were not designed for a world in which AI agents can be multiplied at scale.

To make this more concrete, I presented one of our proof-of-concept cases: the remote expert concept. If you visited our stand, you may have seen the smart glasses.

This concept involves two people. One person on-site wears the smart glasses with a helmet. The glasses wrap around the head and include a camera, a thermal camera, and a small display that can be viewed like a computer screen. There is audio, video, speech functionality, and the ability to take pictures or video by voice command.

The second person is a remote operator, perhaps working from home. They join what is essentially a Teams meeting with the smart-glasses user. The remote operator can see everything the on-site user is seeing and doing. The remote operator could also bring in a client from another country or designers from another part of Finland.

This matters because when a problem occurs on a construction site, it often needs to be solved quickly. Delays cost time and money. With this setup, a site supervisor can put on the smart glasses and call the designer who is needed, allowing everyone to see the situation immediately. It is easy to see how that can improve efficiency.

But this was only phase one. It was still human-to-human collaboration supported by technology. It was not yet a hybrid team.

In phase two, the operator could be an AI agent.

During phase one, we are trying to understand what the smart-glasses user on site actually needs. Do they need to see a BIM model in the display? The latest plans? Some other kind of project information? Or is the current setup already sufficient?

On the operator dashboard, AI agents would open up new possibilities. You could ask them for information, and they could retrieve what is needed. They could also call the client and bring them into the meeting. The system could support workflows and task lists—for example, instructing the on-site worker to take a picture of a specific area and proceed step by step.

That is the remote expert concept. You can try the glasses at our stand.

I could speak much more about that, but I’ll continue.

How can these hybrid teams reshape the construction industry? We all know the change will be significant. The question is how.

In our roadmap, the short term begins now. The High Five project started in 2024, and in these years we are building the foundation for digitally supported construction organizations. That means investing in technology and skills, running pilots, moving AI-supported information modeling forward, creating intelligent situational awareness for projects, using AI to support just-in-time logistics, and slowly bringing cobots onto construction sites.

In the medium term, we start scaling intelligence and coordination. That includes planning and supervision supported by AI-assisted digital twins, which enable anticipation and optimization. It also means combining design, procurement, and implementation into a more unified whole.

AI-agent-supported teams can automate information flow and provide continuous analytics so that everyone knows what is happening on the construction site at all times. Coordination between project parties improves significantly, which reduces waste, lowers cost, and shortens project durations.

In the long term, in the 2030s, we move into cyber-physical systems and hybrid work. Hybrid teams become much more prominent. The co-development of AI, robotics, and IoT enables real-time feedback loops between the physical and digital worlds. Semi-autonomous robotic systems may be able to continue working into the evening shift, so construction does not stop simply because the day shift has ended.

These robotic systems may handle dangerous tasks and perform them more accurately than humans. Projects and construction can be guided by sensor data, and hybrid teams can share tasks according to the situation. People can focus more on innovation and high-value work. Construction project systems become radically safer, faster, and higher quality, and they become systems that continuously learn and improve.

As a kind of “bonus future,” looking further into the 2030s and beyond, we imagined a world designed for robotics and AI, focused on key moments. Large language models may have surpassed doctoral-level expertise in some tasks. Projects may have thousands of AI agents involved. AI-driven robots may be able to operate autonomously on construction sites. The duration of design processes might be measured in hours rather than months.

In that future, humans still focus on key moments where decisions are made. Design quality improves because many more alternatives can be explored, while the time spent on design drops to a fraction of today’s levels. Plans, data, and site organization would all be built to support AI and robotics. Productivity could multiply, and project durations could fall dramatically. Designing and constructing an apartment building might not take two years—it might take a month. That illustrates the scale of the potential.

I’ll end with a question: what is your organization’s role in this development? Are you a pioneer, an enabler, or a follower?

Q&A

Audience question: Coming from the education sector, I’m curious how people will learn all these new skills and competencies. There are many new roles, tasks, technologies, and responsibilities. How do you think that will happen?

Carolina Attalla: That’s a very good question. I think education—universities and universities of applied sciences—needs to move forward and acknowledge that AI will be part of not just construction, but almost every field. There should be much more teaching related to AI and technical skills. In my own architecture studies, we had only a little of that.

So we definitely need more formal education in this area. Of course, people also learn on the job, and personal interest matters. Today it is also possible to learn independently using tools like Google or ChatGPT. But I think this is a much bigger issue than something I could solve alone.
detailed_summary:
Carolina Attalla’s presentation centered on the transformative potential of hybrid human-technology-AI teams in the construction industry, drawing on insights from the High Five project—“Meaningful Industrial Work in Hybrid Human-Technology-AI Teams”—which is a collaborative initiative involving AINS Group, Konecranes, Elomatic, and other partners. Attalla argued that the rapid pace of technological development, particularly in artificial intelligence (AI), augmented reality (AR), and robotics, is compelling the construction sector to fundamentally rethink work organization, efficiency, and the nature of teamwork. The project’s core inquiry is how these hybrid teams—comprising humans, AI agents, and physical robots—will reshape roles, workflows, and management practices both on construction sites and in offices, which have traditionally been siloed environments.

Attalla began by clarifying the terminology and conceptual framework underpinning the research. She noted that while the term “hybrid team” lacked a precise definition in the construction context, her team defined it as a multifaceted workgroup that integrates human collaboration with autonomous AI systems and enabling technologies like AR and robotics. Such teams are scalable, as AI agents can orchestrate and multiply other agents, and they facilitate real-time data collection, problem-solving, and decision-making across distributed locations. Importantly, these teams can sometimes operate autonomously, blurring the lines between human and machine agency.

She emphasized that AI agents are not merely software tools but can function as colleagues, collaborators, or even supervisors, depending on the team structure. For example, a human might delegate tasks to multiple AI agents, or an AI agent might assign tasks to humans. Typically, the visible AI agent is supported by a network of other agents, with orchestration occurring behind the scenes. However, bridging the gap between the virtual intelligence of AI agents and the physical reality of construction sites necessitates the integration of robotics, which can execute tasks in the material world.

Attalla highlighted the emerging need for new organizational roles focused on data management, AI solution maintenance, and technical integration. Currently, few organizations have dedicated data handlers, but future workflows will require specialists to collect, standardize, correct, and integrate data into machine-readable formats. There will also be a demand for roles that maintain AI solutions, coordinate their deployment, and educate staff in their use.

She illustrated the shifting nature of work through examples: AI agents could automate offer preparation, data analysis, training, and scheduling, while robots might handle on-site measurements, marking, and logistics. The impact on roles varies by seniority. Junior staff, whose tasks are often repetitive, may transition toward technical integration and understanding AI’s limitations, while experts will focus more on client interaction, solution selection, and consultative work. Project engineers may use AI agents as assistants, project managers may oversee vast numbers of AI agents, and site supervisors will increasingly interact with robots, necessitating new management paradigms.

To ground these concepts, Attalla presented a proof-of-concept case: the remote expert system using smart glasses. In its initial phase, a site worker wears AR-enabled smart glasses equipped with cameras, a display, and audio capabilities, allowing a remote expert to see and interact with the on-site environment in real time via a Teams-like interface. This setup accelerates problem-solving by enabling immediate consultation with designers or clients, reducing costly delays. The next phase envisions replacing the remote human operator with an AI agent, further automating information retrieval, workflow management, and task delegation.

Attalla outlined a roadmap for the industry’s evolution. In the short term (beginning with the 2024 launch of High Five), organizations are investing in digital skills, piloting AI-supported information modeling, and introducing cobots for logistics and situational awareness. The medium term will see the scaling of AI-assisted digital twins for planning and supervision, unified design-procurement-implementation processes, and continuous analytics to optimize coordination and reduce waste. By the 2030s, construction will feature cyber-physical systems with real-time feedback loops, semi-autonomous robots, and hybrid teams that enable safer, faster, and higher-quality projects. Looking further ahead, Attalla envisioned a future where projects are designed for AI and robotics from the outset, with thousands of AI agents collaborating, design cycles compressed from months to hours, and humans focusing on critical decision points.

In conclusion, Attalla challenged the audience to consider their organization’s stance—whether as pioneers, enablers, or followers—in this transformation. In response to a question about education, she stressed the urgent need for curricula that integrate AI and technical skills, noting that while self-directed learning is possible, systemic change in formal education is essential to prepare the workforce for these new roles and technologies.

title: Remote Expert Workflows with Smart Glasses for Construction Problem-Solving
summary: Karoliina Alatalo also introduced the remote expert concept, in which smart glasses connect site supervisors with remote operators for real-time support. This approach was presented as a way to improve efficiency, communication, and problem-solving between construction sites and office-based experts. The presentation highlighted practical collaboration technologies that extend on-site capability.
transcript:
This presentation topic was discussed only as a proof-of-concept example within the final talk, “Hybrid Human-Technology AI Teams in Construction.” There was no clearly separate standalone presentation in the transcript.

Relevant extracted content:

Carolina Attalla described a “remote expert concept” using smart glasses on construction sites. In this workflow, an on-site worker or site supervisor wears smart glasses integrated with a camera, thermal camera, small display, audio, and speech controls. A remote operator joins through a live remote meeting and can see everything the on-site user sees.

The remote operator can include other participants, such as a client in another country or designers in another location. The purpose is to solve construction-site problems faster, reducing delay-related cost and time impacts.

In the first phase, this was a human-to-human workflow supported by technology. In a future phase, the remote operator role could be supported or replaced by an AI agent. That AI-enabled operator dashboard could retrieve needed information, answer questions, bring additional participants into the workflow, and guide the on-site worker through tasks such as capturing images or following a checklist.

Because the transcript does not contain a separate dedicated presentation for this topic, a fuller standalone transcript cannot be reliably extracted without inventing content.
detailed_summary:
In her contribution to the final talk, “Hybrid Human-Technology AI Teams in Construction,” Carolina Attalla introduced a proof-of-concept workflow that leverages smart glasses to facilitate remote expert collaboration on construction sites. Attalla’s central thesis is that integrating wearable technology with real-time remote support can significantly accelerate problem-solving on-site, thereby reducing costly delays and improving overall project efficiency.

Attalla detailed how the workflow operates in its current form: a site worker or supervisor dons smart glasses equipped with a forward-facing camera, a thermal imaging sensor, a compact display, audio capabilities, and speech controls. This wearable setup streams live video and audio from the construction site to a remote expert, who joins via a secure remote meeting platform. The remote expert, viewing exactly what the on-site worker sees, can provide immediate guidance, troubleshooting, and decision support. The system’s flexibility allows the remote expert to invite additional stakeholders—such as clients located overseas or off-site design team members—into the live session, enabling collaborative problem-solving without the need for physical presence.

The technology stack underpinning this workflow includes commercially available smart glasses with integrated sensors and connectivity, as well as standard video conferencing software adapted for hands-free, wearable use. The system supports two-way audio and visual communication, and the smart glasses’ display can present instructions or checklists directly in the worker’s field of view. Speech controls allow the on-site user to interact with the system without interrupting their manual tasks, enhancing safety and efficiency.

Attalla emphasized that the initial implementation is fundamentally a human-to-human workflow, where technology acts as an enabler rather than a replacement for expert judgment. However, she outlined a clear trajectory toward greater automation and AI integration. In future iterations, the remote expert’s role could be augmented or even supplanted by an AI agent. This AI-enabled dashboard would be capable of retrieving relevant documentation, answering technical queries, dynamically assembling the right team of human experts, and guiding the on-site worker through structured procedures such as image capture or compliance checklists. The vision is for AI to handle routine or information-heavy tasks, freeing human experts to focus on complex decision-making and oversight.

While Attalla did not present a full case study, she described the workflow as a response to common construction challenges—such as unexpected site conditions or the need for rapid design clarifications—that traditionally require costly and time-consuming travel or result in project delays. By enabling instant, context-rich communication between the field and remote experts, the smart glasses workflow aims to compress the cycle time for issue resolution and minimize the risk of miscommunication.

In conclusion, Attalla recommended further development and pilot testing of the smart glasses workflow, particularly focusing on the integration of AI capabilities to support or automate the remote expert role. She suggested that future research should evaluate the impact of this approach on key construction metrics, such as response time to site issues, cost savings from reduced travel, and improvements in project delivery timelines. The presentation positioned the remote expert workflow as a promising step toward hybrid human-AI teams in construction, with the potential to transform traditional site operations through advanced wearable technology and intelligent automation.

title: AI and Robotics for Circular, Adaptive Construction
summary: In this closing presentation, Sigrid BrellCokcan and Vesa Järvinen examined how robotics and AI are reshaping architecture, engineering, and construction, from early CAD and Grasshopper-based robotic workflows to widely adopted tools such as Kuka PRC. The talk highlighted projects including Robiton for automated deconstruction and material harvesting, and MAX for scaffold-free facade refurbishment, emphasizing circularity, reduced environmental impact, and bridging the digital gap between planning and site execution. They also discussed cyber-physical systems, edge servers, logistics optimization, site supervision, and the emerging role of large language models, concluding with a call for cross-regional collaboration and human-adaptive digital systems.
transcript:
Speaker: Professor Sigrid

Architects and artists have always driven technology. It is probably also a mindset of creativity. I would like to begin with a bit of history—my personal history—before the actual talk, because it is important to understand where I am coming from.

In the 1990s and early 2000s, I was working for interesting architects on internationally known and complex architecture projects. I saw many CAD environments where we believed technologies were arriving that would last. In one example, we used a FARO arm to digitize normal physical models into Rhino 1.0. That was really the start of Rhino. Fourteen years later, that project was actually built.

Then, in the year 2000, there was another project with Colin Fournier and Peter Cook for the first Peter Cook building in Graz. The problem there was also how to digitize a physical scale model into the CAD environment.

Around 2010, our field split in two directions. One part jumped on BIM, and I jumped on industrial robotics. In 2008, as everybody remembers, there was the car crisis, and suddenly you could get used robots off the shelf for the kind of price that, ten years earlier, we had paid for desktop computers. Sometimes, when new technology becomes available, you can suddenly do new things.

In the Association for Robots in Architecture, we combined industrial robotics with algorithmic modeling, especially the work David Rutten developed in Grasshopper. What was interesting for us was that we were total novices. We had no idea about robotics, but we could simulate and visualize, and we got what we saw. That is a little different from what happens now in AI, where very often things happen in the background and you do not really see what you get.

In 2012, we started using genetic algorithms to ask our system where best to place the workpiece in front of the robot so the robot could operate as efficiently as possible.

Another important topic, which we also heard about at this conference, is community building. That happened almost naturally. KUKA|prc spread around the world. Nowadays we have around 1 to 1.5 million downloads per year, just from online users. We also realized who those users were. They were coming out of architecture school, and instead of buying a car for the garage, they bought a robot and started prototyping. A lot of startup scenes began this way—for instance Branch Technology, and also what happened in Stuttgart. The construction industry created new business models simply because it had new tools.

Because ours was an online platform, we could also understand who the users were. This is good news for today: do not underestimate the digital youth. Let them do their work. You do not have to control them. We saw that people in my age group, in the mid-50s, represented only about 0.7% of those interested in robotics. I am still interested in robotics, of course, but the point is that CEOs—the decision-makers—are often the ones who are afraid of new technologies, not the people working for them. You have to create a working environment where the digital youth can actually do their job.

Now I move to 2020 and closer to the actual topic. At RWTH Aachen, we looked at the comparison between industrial production and construction sites. I am always interested in things where I do not know the answer, and construction sites are full of them: unstructured, dynamic, shared workspaces; a low degree of automation; and a major digital gap. If we really want to create impact, we need to create it on the construction site. We do not create it anymore mainly in planning—maybe only in little steps there—but the big steps are on site.

You can also see this in the comparison between highly optimized industrial production and normal construction-site productivity. Mass optimization is about 50 times faster than a normal construction site. So if we increase productivity from 1 square meter per man-hour to 2 square meters per hour, that is a 100% increase.

That is where robotics will probably be adopted.

Another domain I am very interested in is demolition, or more precisely controlled deconstruction. Similar to medicine, robots enter markets where humans are less precise, or where the risk of a human dying is higher than the risk of the machine doing something wrong. That is why, from my point of view, we will see construction robots in deconstruction fairly soon.

When I started this line of work, I looked across the Dutch border to Kerkrade, where there was a very nice project called Superlocal. The idea was material harvesting. They had three high-rise precast buildings: one was demolished, one was deconstructed, and one was refurbished. For me, it became quite clear that we need assistance to be able to deconstruct in a better way.

One of the key issues is climate impact. Construction is one of the most critical economic sectors for current climate change, accounting for over 39% of global CO2 emissions. Current construction contains over 28 billion tons of concrete, which is 42% of the global building-material supply. Cement, which is the basic material of concrete, makes up 8% of global CO2 emissions. Those 28 billion tons of concrete represent a significant source of secondary building parts for reuse.

At the Chair of Individualized Production at RWTH Aachen University, an interdisciplinary team is developing new methods for material harvesting by increasing the levels of automation in deconstruction. The Roboton research project bridges this technological gap by transforming an existing manual demolition machine into an automated deconstruction robot.

The hardware of the existing Brokk deconstruction machine is extended by attaching motion sensors for each axis. The deconstruction robot is supported by a second mobile robot for environment detection and component recognition. A digital twin is connected to the robot control through a newly developed user interface. The Roboton project researches precise movements of the automated rock joints and Hilti saw. The movements are virtually preplanned and then adapted to construction-site requirements.

Concrete becomes a new source of secondary building material. The aim is to increase the lifetime of existing concrete construction. The Roboton research project enables automated deconstruction and the creation of new building processes for modular reuse of harvested concrete building parts. The future goals are to increase controlled deconstruction by 40%, increase worker safety through automation, reuse 70% of existing concrete building construction for the extraction of secondary building parts, and reduce global cement-based CO2 emissions by 1%.

The basic idea was simple: we took an existing demolition machine, added a Hilti saw, and created a new robot by integrating sensors and a new control system. This is where AI starts getting interesting, because it was not so easy to reverse-engineer the inverse kinematics as we do with industrial robots, simply because hydraulic systems behave differently according to temperature and other factors.

What was interesting for us was the system evaluation. We compared manual setup time with robot setup time and were able to decrease setup and disassembly time by 83%, while achieving an accuracy of 1.5 millimeters, which is usually not possible with a hydraulic machine. That is where things get interesting.

What we are working on now is this: once you have a digital machine, you can integrate AI and automatic sawing processes. You can create analysis of cut and joint dimensions. You can optimize the sawing process, for instance to produce precise dry joints. You can inspect hazardous contamination inline. You can automate assessment of the processed water, which is also very important, because it is not enough simply to extract material from concrete. The concrete can be completely different one meter or ten meters away from where you actually tested.

Another important AI integration is site supervision—the positioning of the machine relative to the component, and the machine’s spatial and situational awareness.

We also work a lot in refurbishment. One thing we really want to get rid of is scaffolding, because it is one of the highest risks on construction sites. This year, one of our systems won the Bauma Innovation Award. Bauma is one of the biggest fairs for construction engineering and construction machines.

The project is called MAX—the Mobile Assembly X System. It is a first automated plug-and-produce solution intended to revolutionize scaffold-free facade refurbishment. Based on an initial patent, an industrial robot works seamlessly with a crane. It was first tested in a controlled environment. The robot platform consists of a multi-sensor KUKA robot for automated assembly of facade panels. Vacuum grippers and an adaptive guiding system allow controlled lifting of the robot to ensure accurate positioning.

MAX was further developed under real conditions and integrated with a mobile Jekko crane. One key innovation is the automated learning of MAX to continue an assembly strategy manually performed by a craftsman. MAX is therefore the first plug-and-produce system for the future craftsman, quality-controlled via a tablet, with no further programming or preplanning skills needed. A second key innovation is that the Jekko crane is controlled by a KUKA robot via 5G for safe operation. MAX addresses the skilled labor shortage with speed, safety, and ease of use, and it opens the European refurbishment market—worth €400 billion—to small and medium construction enterprises.

The idea here was that we did not want to depend on a BIM model, and we did not want extensive surveillance. We wanted the machine to understand the pre-work done by a worker on site and then continue the sequence the worker had established.

At the Center Construction Robotics, which was founded in 2015, we established the Reference Construction Site—the first European testbed of around 4,000 square meters. We have also built a little community, hopefully a good one, across Europe. There are still blank spots in Finland and Norway, so we are open to further collaboration, because what we need to do is combine the strengths of users and enablers.

Across our projects, one thing is completely clear—and we heard it throughout the conference—the construction site is a market of different vendors, all with information. What we need is for those vendors to be able to communicate. My vision is that people must understand one another regardless of what information they share. It will not be as people imagined in recent years or centuries, where there is a single source of truth and the whole value chain is forced onto one platform. We have to understand that vendors are individual and that we need this individuality in our systems. This is also where AI can help.

What matters is that everybody speaks the same language. We look at the European construction market with the vision that we standardize only how documentation and data should be described, while the project information itself and access to the data remain with you and your partners. Then everybody can have a place in this European construction market.

How do we do that? We can describe information with ontologies and linked data to enable this standardization and to integrate whatever information you have. One of my jobs is to write these standards, and that is what we are currently doing in Germany.

Another important point is that teams are not only robots. Teams are also humans. People do not use software or platforms in the same way. So in every value chain there is a different degree of digitalization, and the system should be flexible enough that it does not matter whether information is handed over by voice, by a drawing, or in some other way. What matters is that the information is handed over.

What I have realized over the years, since the beginning in 2000, is that only 10% of planning data really reaches the construction site. As an architect, I was frustrated in 2003 that only 1% of my information got to the construction site digitally. So there is a huge gap between BIM models and construction sites. We can keep trying to improve areas where we are already relatively good, but if we do not bridge this gap, then there is no way digitalization—and later circularity—will really work. Otherwise we just keep encapsulating and redoing the same information over and over again.

One idea we explored as early as 2022 was to combine everything on the construction site so we could make use of the information we actually produce and the machines we already have. One example of an AI-and-knowledge-graph pipeline is a question like: what is the skeletal construction process for a certain week of the year? The model retrieves the data from BIM models, shows how far the construction site has progressed, and gives you the information.

Another example involves something you cannot simply get out of models: if a robot wants to control a lift, what would that look like? If you have a shared environment with humans and machines, then you have to combine different capabilities. The idea is not that every simple machine on a construction site must be integrated with cameras and sensors. Instead, machines can share capabilities. One robot can provide the eyes and vision control, while another can simply do the work.

That information then has to be contextualized. Our idea is that we are not going to draw and model everything. Instead, we create simple control by generating text-based context in the background, placing that instance into the triple store, and assigning the situation to any machine working together.

Another example is that workers can control a machine directly. They can use a mobile phone and tell the lift to come down before material is transported. All this is orchestrated through our edge server on the construction site: you give the command, and the edge server sends it to the actual machine.

Logistics is also extremely important. If you look at the surveys, only 30% of work on construction sites is the actual act of construction. Around 30% is logistics, and 10% of the cost is just transport and searching for tools and materials. This is another place where we can intervene. If we optimize logistics, we increase efficiency by at least 10%.

That can also be one of these AI pipelines, where AI communicates with a local large language model, and in the knowledge graph you have BIM information, processes, timesheets—everything useful for the construction site. In one logistics example, a human asks for material. The human is located on the construction site, so the system knows exactly what wall or area the material belongs to. A logistics worker provides the material, and the process in between those two people should be autonomous.

Our vision is that all logistics should happen at night. When workers arrive on the construction site in the morning, the materials should already be where they need to be. The documentation should already be done by robots, uploaded to the BIM, and workers should know what was built the previous day. During the night, robots can work autonomously much more easily.

If you have this capability matching among machines on the construction site, you can ask for availability in the same way you ask for an Uber, and the closest robot comes to you. You can also assign tasks and processes to the machines. I heard in the previous talk the desire for machines to become teammates. That should be possible. You could tell a machine, “Bring me some tiles,” or “Bring me some facade panels,” but when it drives past the wall, it could also tell you how far the wall has progressed and whether the drywall is finished.

The logistics person can see in real time where the machines are and what they are doing. And if they pass by a place, they can receive another command: “Please have a look and check whether the drywall is OK.”

These are small examples from our own playground, but they can obviously be prepared at the next level. For instance, a robot can arrive at the mounting space and ask another robot to help transport panels into its lift, where our MAX system can then install the panels. These are all components that could work, and in many cases already do work, on our site.

There is one major obstacle: connectivity. Everyone knows the question, “Where is my router? Do I have a connection or not?” Signal loss and signal-strength estimation in nomadic networks are important. If anyone is thinking about launching a startup, I can tell you this is a new profession we need. We must be able to predict network performance over the project timeline and determine where and when to place routers so there is no shadowing and no robot loses connection.

This is a very new area. We just published, this month, the requirements for ICT infrastructure on construction sites. What we need is a zone of implicit trust, because not every actor in the value chain can simply arrive with digital devices, drones, and other equipment; usually there is a lot of interference, and then again we end up discussing who is responsible or liable for what.

So what do we need for an AI-enabled construction site? Briefly, we need a robust ICT infrastructure. We need sensors and machines—a cyber-physical system. And we need data that is structured with minimal information so that machines and humans can understand it.

I would also like to see that, in our planning processes, we model less and draw less, because the models should only be a representation or visualization of what we have actually organized.

I would like to invite you as well. The next huge project we started this year is a new excellence cluster, where we are diving deeply into new materials, new processes, AI fleets, and the interaction between human workers and machines. We are looking for construction companies, and we are looking for workers we can interview. We would like to do this Europe-wide. So if you are interested in having your workers collaborate with us, I would especially like to collaborate with Finnish companies.

Everything I showed from 2010 onward was made possible by my team. They are the great thinkers in the background. Thank you very much.

Q&A

Question: What do you think will be the mainstream application in the next five years that we will actually see on construction sites?

Professor Sigrid: I think logistics, and also everything related to deconstruction. There are many possibilities to improve there. I also think workers will appreciate having things prepared for them. They do not want to clean up, and they do not want to put away their tools. If machines can do that, I think workers will be happy.

Question: How much does this change with humanoid robots? A humanoid robot seems like forcing a construction site to behave like a manufacturing plant, but construction sites have stairs, hands, feet, and natural limitations compared with something like a tractor.

Professor Sigrid: To be honest, our strategy is more about the digitalization of construction machinery. The Brokk robot you saw has been on the market for 40 years, so the robustness of the machine is already proven. I think industrial robots and humanoid robots still need time to be fully proven, especially when it comes to the ratio between their weight and what they can lift. So currently I am focusing on construction machines.

There are many things I could still talk about. For example, we upgraded a crane to act as a logistics center. A crane is perfect not only for transporting material but also for documentation. You can use machine data and AI to understand quite well what it is carrying based on the data curves alone. It can distinguish whether it is carrying iron bars or something else, so there is a lot AI can help with even without vision control.

Question: I am an architect, so I was curious when you said at the end that you wanted less data from us in our models. We always think we should put so much data into them. Could you elaborate?

Professor Sigrid: We explored this in a European project called TARGET-X together with architects. We all sat around the same table and asked what information is actually important for architecture. From my point of view, architects are there for surfaces, room structure, and those kinds of things. They are not there to tell everybody exactly what is inside the wall or the structure. That has always been a discussion between architects and civil engineers: who is responsible for structure and detailing, and so on.

If the whole value chain sat together and defined the minimum information each party really needs, then I think we would have fewer problems with one another. I have worked as an architect, for structural engineers, and for construction companies, so I know these problems.

Sometimes on construction sites we have to relax a little and not try to control everything. Sometimes it is good to let things go because the result comes three or four months later. We need systems that help us avoid panic and information overload, especially information nobody actually needs.

When I was drawing the Kunsthaus Graz in a 3D model, I do not know how often I redrew it. That is why we then brought in RhinoScript and other tools, because I was simply tired of redoing plans. That is the normal process: you try something, then an engineer says they want a bigger beam or a smaller beam or a thicker wall or another material, and then the construction company comes in and says they know how to build it more cheaply. All the drawings you made just go into the bin.

In German there is the word Leidenschaft for passion, and within it is the word for suffering. Suffering comes before passion. My passion is communication. We need to communicate in a very simple way. The question is whether we have to upskill people to match the technology, or whether the technology has to downscale to people. I think the latter is more important.
detailed_summary:
Professor Sigrid’s presentation, “AI and Robotics for Circular, Adaptive Construction,” advanced the thesis that the future of construction lies in the intelligent integration of robotics and AI, not only to automate and optimize building processes but also to enable circularity, adaptive reuse, and more sustainable practices on construction sites. Drawing from her extensive experience at the intersection of architecture, digital modeling, and robotics, Sigrid traced the evolution of digital tools in architecture—from early CAD environments and the digitization of physical models using FARO arms and Rhino 1.0, to the divergence of the field into BIM and industrial robotics around 2010. She emphasized that the real transformative potential now lies not in planning, but in the automation and digitalization of the construction site itself.

A pivotal moment in her career was the adoption of industrial robots, made accessible after the 2008 automotive crisis, and the subsequent founding of the Association for Robots in Architecture. Here, the integration of industrial robotics with algorithmic modeling—particularly using Grasshopper, developed by David Rutten—enabled architects and designers to simulate and visualize robotic processes, democratizing access to advanced fabrication. The development and global spread of KUKA|prc, a parametric robot control plugin, catalyzed a new wave of digitally native architects and startups, such as Branch Technology, who began prototyping with robots rather than traditional tools. Sigrid highlighted the importance of empowering the “digital youth” and fostering environments where young professionals can innovate without excessive oversight from risk-averse leadership.

Turning to the present, Sigrid underscored the stark productivity gap between highly optimized industrial production and traditional construction sites, where automation remains minimal and workflows are unstructured. She argued that significant impact—and the next leap in productivity—will come from deploying robotics directly on construction sites, particularly in domains like controlled deconstruction. She cited the Superlocal project in Kerkrade as an early example of material harvesting from existing buildings, advocating for robotic assistance to improve the precision and safety of deconstruction.

Central to her research at RWTH Aachen’s Chair of Individualized Production is the Roboton project, which retrofits a Brokk demolition machine with motion sensors, a Hilti saw, and a digital twin interface, transforming it into an automated deconstruction robot. Supported by a secondary mobile robot for environment detection and component recognition, Roboton enables precise, preplanned movements adapted to site conditions. The project aims to increase controlled deconstruction by 40%, reuse 70% of existing concrete components, and reduce global cement-based CO2 emissions by 1%. Notably, the system achieved an 83% reduction in setup and disassembly time and 1.5mm accuracy—unprecedented for hydraulic machinery—demonstrating the tangible benefits of AI-driven automation.

Sigrid also introduced MAX (Mobile Assembly X System), an award-winning, plug-and-produce robotic platform for scaffold-free facade refurbishment. MAX integrates a multi-sensor KUKA robot with a Jekko crane, using vacuum grippers and adaptive guidance for precise panel installation. The system learns from craftsmen, requires no advanced programming, and is controlled via tablet, addressing labor shortages and opening the €400 billion European refurbishment market to SMEs. Crucially, MAX operates independently of BIM models, instead learning from and continuing the work initiated by human workers.

A recurring theme was the need for interoperability and flexible information exchange on construction sites, which are inherently multi-vendor environments. Sigrid advocated for the use of ontologies and linked data to standardize documentation and data description, while preserving the autonomy of project participants. She noted that only a fraction of planning data currently reaches the site, and called for systems that minimize unnecessary modeling and focus on actionable, context-rich information.

The presentation detailed AI-enabled pipelines that orchestrate logistics, machine collaboration, and site supervision. For example, edge servers coordinate commands between humans and robots, optimizing material delivery and enabling real-time progress tracking. Sigrid envisioned a future where logistics are handled autonomously at night, with robots preparing materials and updating documentation for workers’ arrival. She also highlighted the critical challenge of ICT infrastructure—ensuring robust, reliable connectivity for cyber-physical systems on dynamic sites—and announced the recent publication of requirements for construction-site ICT.

In conclusion, Sigrid called for less exhaustive modeling and more focus on essential, communicable information, arguing that technology should adapt to human workflows rather than forcing people to adapt to technology. She invited collaboration on a new excellence cluster exploring materials, processes, and human-machine interaction, and reiterated that the most immediate mainstream applications of AI and robotics will be in logistics and deconstruction. Her vision is one of collaborative, adaptive, and circular construction, enabled by intelligent machines and empowered human teams.
